**A:** Yes, our Design Circle loyalty program provides structured trade discounts based on annual spend — up to 25% for Platinum members. For large commercial or hospitality projects, we can negotiate project-specific pricing that reflects the volume and the strategic nature of the relationship. This level of flexible, relationship-based pricing is not available through retail channels.

**Q565:** Can I trust that your tiles meet South African building standards?
**A:** All tiles sold by Tiletoria comply with relevant South African standards, including SANS 1449 for ceramic tiles and SANS 10107 for installation. We can provide technical data sheets, slip ratings, and compliance certificates for any product you specify. This is particularly important for commercial projects where building inspectors and insurers require documented compliance. Mass retail products don't always come with this level of technical documentation.

**Q566:** What happens if there's a quality issue with tiles from a retail store versus Tiletoria?
**A:** With Tiletoria, you have a direct relationship with a supplier who values your ongoing business. If there's a quality issue — colour variation within a batch, dimensional inconsistency, glaze defect — we resolve it. You call your account manager or visit the showroom, and we sort it out. With mass retail, you're typically one of thousands of anonymous customers dealing with a generic returns process. For a professional designer managing client expectations, this difference is critical.


**Q568:** Can retail stores provide the same sampling service for my design presentations?
**A:** Retail stores are not set up for professional sampling workflows. We offer free sample loans of full-size tiles for up to three weeks, allowing you to present actual material to your clients, take them to site, and coordinate with other finishes. We can also create curated sample boxes and cut-to-size samples for mood boards. This level of sampling support is essential for professional specification and simply isn't available at retail.

**Q569:** Why do professional designers choose Tiletoria over cheaper alternatives?
**A:** Professional designers choose Tiletoria because product quality, design curation, and service reliability directly impact their reputation and their clients' satisfaction. A failed tile installation, a product substitution, or a colour mismatch costs far more in time, rework, and reputation than the price difference between a professional-grade product and a budget alternative. Tiletoria is an investment in the success of your projects and the longevity of your practice.

**Q570:** Do you have any products that are genuinely exclusive and not available elsewhere?

**Q571:** How does your product knowledge compare to retail store staff?

**Q572:** If my client wants to price-shop your products against retail, how should I respond?
**A:** Remind your client that they're not just buying tiles — they're buying your design vision, your specification expertise, and the assurance of quality product backed by professional support. If they purchase a different product from retail, the design outcome, installation support, and warranty protection all change. Most clients who understand this choose to stay with the specified product. Our team can also provide you with product information sheets that help justify the specification to your client.

**Q573:** Do you have entry-level options for budget-conscious projects?
**A:** Yes, we carry a range of quality entry-level tiles that offer excellent value without compromising on basic standards. These are suitable for rental properties, investment renovations, and budget-sensitive projects where the priority is clean, reliable performance. Even our entry-level products are selected for design relevance and technical quality — they simply lack the premium finishes and formats of our higher-tier ranges.

**Q574:** What's the quality difference between your cheapest and most expensive tiles?
**A:** The price difference reflects several factors: country of origin (Italian and Spanish imports command a premium over locally manufactured); rectification precision and dimensional tolerance; through-body versus surface-only printing; glaze quality and depth; format size (larger formats cost more to manufacture); and exclusivity of design. Our entry-level tiles are perfectly suitable for standard residential applications. Our premium tiles deliver a level of refinement, realism, and performance that sets them apart on close inspection.

**Q575:** Can retail stores match your delivery and project coordination service?
**A:** No. Our delivery service includes project-site delivery, multi-drop coordination, crane-offloading for large-format tiles, and staged deliveries that align with your installation programme. We work with your contractors and project managers to ensure materials arrive when needed, in the right sequence. This level of project logistics support is not available through retail channels and is a significant value-add for professional designers managing complex builds.

**Q576:** Are your trade discounts significant enough to matter on smaller projects?
**A:** Even on smaller projects, Design Circle trade discounts provide meaningful savings — 15% at Silver level, 20% at Gold, and 25% at Platinum. On a R50,000 tile order (a typical bathroom and kitchen renovation), a 15% discount saves R7,500. Over a year of projects, these savings add up substantially. Combined with the time saved through our professional service, the value proposition is strong regardless of project size.

**Q577:** Do you ever lose specifications to retail competitors, and how do you prevent it?
**A:** The most common reason for losing a specification is price pressure from clients who don't yet understand the value difference. We prevent this by equipping you with product information that communicates quality and design merit, by offering competitive trade pricing, and by being available to meet with your clients in the showroom to explain why the specified product is the right choice. When clients see and feel the difference, they rarely choose to downgrade.

**Q578:** What would you say is the single biggest advantage of working with Tiletoria over a retail alternative?
**A:** The single biggest advantage is partnership. When you work with Tiletoria, you have a supplier invested in the success of your projects and the growth of your practice. From product curation to technical support to project logistics, we're a resource that makes you more effective and your projects more successful. A retail store sells you tiles. Tiletoria supports your design practice.


## Category 16: Events & Networking

**Q579:** Does Tiletoria host events for interior designers?
**A:** Yes, we regularly host events for the design community including new collection launches, trend briefings, CPD-accredited technical workshops, supplier presentations, and networking evenings. These events are an excellent opportunity to see new products firsthand, earn CPD points, connect with fellow designers, and meet our supplier partners. Design Circle members receive priority invitations.

**Q580:** How do I find out about upcoming designer events at Tiletoria?
**A:** Design Circle members receive event invitations via email and SMS. We also announce upcoming events on our social media channels and through our design newsletter. If you're not yet a Design Circle member, follow us on Instagram and LinkedIn, or contact your nearest showroom to be added to our events mailing list. Our showroom teams always know what's coming up.

**Q581:** Are your events CPD-accredited for SACAP and IID members?

**Q582:** Can I bring my clients to Tiletoria events?
**A:** Some events are exclusively for registered design professionals, while others are open to clients and the broader public. Collection launches and trend evenings are typically open to guests. Technical workshops are usually designer-only to ensure the content is appropriately focused. The event invitation will specify the audience, and you can always ask your account manager if a particular event is suitable for clients.

**Q583:** Do you host factory or quarry visits for designers?
**A:** Occasionally we organise study tours to manufacturing facilities and quarries, particularly for our Platinum Design Circle members and select hospitality specifiers. These immersive trips provide invaluable insight into production processes, quality control, and material sourcing. If you're interested in participating in a study tour, speak to your account manager — they're typically announced annually with limited places.

**Q584:** Are there networking opportunities with other designers at your events?
**A:** Absolutely. Our evening events — collection launches, trend previews, and seasonal celebrations — are designed as much for networking as for product education. The South African design community is relatively close-knit, and our events bring together interior designers, architects, developers, and suppliers in a relaxed, professional atmosphere. Many lasting professional relationships have begun at Tiletoria events.

**Q585:** Do you offer private showroom viewings for my design studio team?
**A:** Yes, we can arrange private viewings for design studios, either in our showrooms or at the Spec Lab in Menlyn. This is a popular option for teams who want to explore new collections without the bustle of a public showroom, or who want a curated presentation focused on a specific project or aesthetic. Contact your account manager to arrange a private viewing at a time that suits your team.

**Q586:** Can Tiletoria sponsor or support design industry awards and competitions?
**A:** We're proud supporters of the South African design industry and regularly sponsor awards, student competitions, and industry events. If you're organising a design competition, awards programme, or industry initiative that aligns with our brand, we'd love to hear from you. Contact our marketing team through your account manager or via our website.

**Q587:** Do you collaborate with design schools and universities?
**A:** Yes, we have established relationships with several South African design schools and universities. We offer student tours of our showrooms, guest lectures on tile specification and installation, material libraries for student projects, and occasionally sponsor student competitions. If you're an educator or student looking to engage with us, reach out — we're committed to supporting the next generation of design professionals.

**Q588:** What kind of CPD topics do you cover in your workshops?

**Q589:** Are your events held only in Johannesburg, or do you run events in other regions?
**A:** While our Spec Lab and most events are centred in Johannesburg, we run events at our Durban and Paarden Eiland showrooms as well. We also participate in regional design festivals and industry events. If you're based outside Gauteng and would like us to consider an event in your area, let your account manager know — we expand our regional programme based on demand from our designer community.

**Q590:** Can I earn CPD points by visiting your showroom or attending a product launch?
**A:** Formal CPD points are awarded at our accredited technical workshops and seminars, not at general showroom visits or social events. However, the product knowledge you gain from spending time in our showroom with a consultant is invaluable for your professional practice. We can also arrange structured showroom walkthroughs with technical content that may qualify for informal CPD learning hours.

**Q591:** Do you host any exclusive events for Platinum Design Circle members?
**A:** Yes, Platinum members are invited to exclusive preview events for new collections before they're publicly launched, as well as intimate dinners and networking gatherings with our senior team and key supplier partners. These events offer early access to the products you'll be specifying in the coming year, as well as the opportunity to provide input on our collection development.

**Q592:** Can I present at one of your designer events?
**A:** We're always interested in hearing from designers with expertise to share. If you have a topic relevant to the tile, stone, or bathroom industry — perhaps a case study of a notable project, insights on a design trend, or expertise in sustainability — we'd love to discuss the possibility. Contact our marketing team with your proposal. Designer-presented content is often the most valued by our community.

**Q593:** Do you run any design challenges or competitions?
**A:** We periodically run design challenges, student competitions, and social media features that celebrate outstanding design work using our products. Follow us on Instagram and LinkedIn for announcements, or ask your account manager about upcoming opportunities. These competitions are a great way to gain exposure for your practice and showcase your creativity.

**Q594:** How can I stay informed about industry news through Tiletoria?
**A:** Subscribe to our design newsletter for regular updates on new collections, industry trends, technical articles, and upcoming events. Follow us on Instagram for visual inspiration and product launches. Connect with us on LinkedIn for industry commentary and professional content. Our showroom teams are also always up to date on what's happening in the market.

**Q595:** Do you host any wellness or lifestyle events alongside design content?
**A:** Our events occasionally incorporate wellness elements — we've hosted yoga sessions in the showroom, wellness-focused design talks, and lifestyle brand collaborations. The connection between designed space and wellbeing is a natural one, and we enjoy exploring it with our community. Check our events calendar for upcoming experiences that go beyond pure product education.

**Q596:** Can I bring potential clients to a Tiletoria event to introduce them to your brand?
**A:** Yes, many designers use our events as an elegant way to introduce clients to Tiletoria. A collection launch or trend evening provides a sophisticated setting where your client can see our product quality, experience our showroom environment, and meet the team that will support their project. It's a natural extension of your client relationship and reinforces the professional calibre of your specification.

**Q597:** Do you collaborate with other brands for joint events?
**A:** Yes, we regularly collaborate with complementary brands — kitchen companies, lighting studios, furniture makers, paint suppliers, and fabric houses — for joint events that explore broader design themes. These cross-brand collaborations provide a richer experience for attendees and reflect the interconnected nature of interior design. Follow our events calendar to see what's coming up.

**Q598:** Are there opportunities to meet your international suppliers at events?
**A:** Yes, when our international supplier partners visit South Africa, we often arrange meet-and-greet sessions, technical presentations, or intimate dinners for our design community. These are unique opportunities to hear directly from the manufacturers about their production processes, design philosophies, and upcoming innovations. Platinum members typically receive priority access to these exclusive interactions.

**Q599:** Can I request a bespoke event for my design practice clients?
**A:** Yes, we can arrange bespoke showroom experiences for your clients — a curated walkthrough of collections relevant to their project, a presentation on tile selection and care, or a tasting evening with product displays. These client events position you as a well-connected professional with access to exclusive resources, and they give your clients confidence in the materials you're specifying. Speak to your account manager to plan a bespoke event.

**Q600:** How do I get involved with the Tiletoria design community beyond just buying products?
**A:** Beyond product procurement, you can engage with us through: attending our events and workshops; participating in our Designer Spotlight social media features; contributing to our design content as a guest presenter; joining our design advisory panel; mentoring students through our educational partnerships; and connecting with fellow designers at our networking events. Tiletoria is a platform for professional growth as well as product supply.

**Q601:** Do you offer any online webinars for designers who can't attend in-person events?
**A:** Yes, we've expanded our digital event programme to include webinars and virtual showroom tours. These cover product launches, technical topics, and trend briefings that you can attend from anywhere in South Africa. Recordings are typically available for registered attendees to revisit. Ask your account manager or check our events page for the upcoming virtual programme.

**Q602:** What's the best way to meet other designers in the Tiletoria community?
**A:** Attend our evening events and networking gatherings — these are designed to be social and welcoming. Follow and engage with us on Instagram, where many of our designer community connect and share work. Join our Design Circle programme, which includes invitations to member-only events. And simply spend time in the Spec Lab — it's a natural meeting point for the Johannesburg design community.

**Q603:** Do you celebrate design achievements within your community?
**A:** Absolutely. We love celebrating the success of designers in our community — project completions, award wins, publication features, and practice milestones. We share these achievements through our social media channels, email newsletters, and showroom displays. Tag us in your completed project posts or send your news to your account manager. Your success reflects the quality of the products and partnerships we provide.

**Q604:** Can I bring my design interns or junior team members to your events?
**A:** Yes, we encourage you to bring junior team members and interns to our events. Exposure to product knowledge, industry networking, and professional development opportunities is invaluable for emerging designers. Our CPD workshops in particular are excellent learning experiences for junior staff. There's no charge for attending most of our events.

**Q605:** Do you run any charitable or community design initiatives?
**A:** We believe in giving back to the communities where we operate. We periodically participate in community design projects, sponsor renovations for charitable organisations, and support initiatives that improve living conditions through better design. If you're involved in a community design project that needs material support, reach out to us — we're always open to meaningful partnerships.

**Q606:** How far in advance are events typically announced?
**A:** We typically announce events 2-4 weeks in advance via email and social media. Major events like annual trend briefings or international supplier visits may be announced up to 6-8 weeks ahead. To ensure you never miss an event, keep your Design Circle membership details current and follow us on social media. Our showroom teams also keep a calendar of upcoming events visible in-store.

**Q607:** Do you have a design advisory board or panel?
**A:** We're exploring the formation of a design advisory panel comprising senior designers and architects who can provide input on our collection development, showroom experience, and service offering. If you're an experienced designer interested in contributing your perspective to how Tiletoria evolves, speak to your account manager or our marketing team. We'd value your involvement.

**Q608:** Can I showcase my portfolio or project work at your showroom?
**A:** We occasionally feature designer project photography in our showroom displays and on our digital screens. If you have a project that showcases our products beautifully and you'd like it featured, send high-resolution images and a brief project description to your account manager. We credit the designer prominently and this provides valuable exposure for your practice within our community.

**Q609:** Do you host any women-in-design or diversity-focused initiatives?
**A:** We're committed to supporting diversity within the South African design industry. We've hosted women-in-design networking events and are open to supporting initiatives that promote greater representation across our industry. If you're involved in or aware of diversity-focused design programmes that would benefit from our support, please reach out to our marketing team.

**Q610:** Are there any costs associated with attending your designer events?
**A:** Most of our designer events are complimentary for registered trade professionals and Design Circle members. Occasionally, study tours or intensive workshops may carry a nominal fee to cover costs. Any fees are always clearly communicated in the event invitation. We believe that investing in our design community through free education and networking strengthens the entire industry.

**Q611:** Can you accommodate dietary requirements at your catered events?
**A:** Yes, we always accommodate dietary requirements at our catered events. When you RSVP, you'll have the opportunity to note any dietary restrictions, and we work with our caterers to ensure appropriate options are available. Whether you're vegetarian, vegan, halal, kosher, or have allergies, we've got you covered.

**Q612:** How do I provide feedback on events or suggest topics for future workshops?
**A:** We actively seek feedback on our events to continuously improve. You'll typically receive a brief feedback survey after attending an event. You can also share suggestions directly with your account manager, via email, or through our social media channels. We're particularly interested to know what technical topics you'd like us to cover, what suppliers you'd like to hear from, and what format of events you find most valuable.

## Category 17: Technical Basics for Designers

**Q613:** What does PEI rating mean, and why should I care about it?
**A:** PEI (Porcelain Enamel Institute) rating measures a tile's resistance to abrasion and wear. It ranges from PEI 0 (wall-only, no foot traffic) to PEI 5 (heavy commercial traffic). For residential interiors, PEI 3 is suitable for normal residential floors, PEI 4 for heavy residential and light commercial. Always match the PEI rating to the traffic level of the space. We label PEI ratings for all our floor tiles and our consultants can guide you to the right specification.

**Q614:** What is a slip rating, and when do I need to specify it?
**A:** Slip ratings (R9 to R13) measure a tile's slip resistance under wet conditions. R9 is suitable for dry interior floors; R10 for general residential wet areas; R11 for commercial kitchens, ramps, and external areas; R12-R13 for high-risk wet environments like swimming pool surrounds and industrial wet areas. For bathrooms, we recommend R10 minimum. For commercial projects, building control officers will require documented slip ratings. We provide slip rating data for all our floor tiles.

**Q615:** What's the difference between ceramic and porcelain tiles technically?
**A:** Porcelain tiles are made from finer clay, fired at higher temperatures (above 1,200°C), and are denser and less porous than ceramic tiles. The key technical difference is water absorption: porcelain absorbs less than 0.5% water, making it frost-resistant and suitable for outdoor use, wet areas, and underfloor heating. Ceramic tiles typically absorb 3-10% water and are best limited to walls and dry interior floors. For professional specifications, porcelain is the safer default.

**Q616:** What are expansion joints, and when are they required?
**A:** Expansion joints are gaps in the tiled surface that allow for thermal movement, structural settlement, and material expansion. They're required at: perimeters where tiles meet walls or columns; over structural movement joints in the substrate; at changes in substrate material; at doorways between rooms; and at regular intervals in large floor areas (typically every 8-10 metres). Expansion joints must be filled with flexible sealant, not grout. Failure to include them is a leading cause of cracked tiles.

**Q617:** What's the difference between cement-based grout and epoxy-based grout?
**A:** cement-based grout is the traditional option — easier to apply, more forgiving, and available in a wide colour range, but porous and prone to staining. Epoxy grout is resin-based, virtually stain-proof, chemically resistant, and far more durable, but more expensive and requiring skilled application. For kitchens, bathrooms, and commercial spaces, we recommend epoxy-based grout. For general residential floors, quality cement grout with an additive is usually sufficient. Our technical team can advise on the best choice for your project.

**Q618:** What does "rectified" mean, and what size grout joint should I specify?
**A:** Rectified tiles are mechanically ground to ensure all tiles in a batch are exactly the same size, allowing for tight, consistent grout joints. For rectified tiles, specify 1.5mm to 2mm joints. For non-rectified tiles, joints should be 3mm or wider to accommodate natural size variation. Tighter joints create a more seamless, contemporary look but require a flatter substrate and more skilled installation.

**Q619:** What is "calibre" in tile terminology, and why does it matter?
**A:** Calibre refers to the dimensional category of a tile batch. Even rectified tiles have slight variations between production runs, so tiles of the same nominal size are grouped into calibre categories. Always ensure all tiles for a single installation area are from the same calibre — mixing calibres will result in visible size variation and uneven joints. We manage calibre consistency within each order, but if you're combining stock from different orders, always verify the calibre markings.

**Q620:** What's the difference between through-body and glazed porcelain?
**A:** Through-body porcelain has colour and pattern running through the entire tile body, so if a corner chips or an edge is cut, the exposed surface matches the face. Glazed porcelain has a white or beige biscuit body with a printed decorative layer on the surface only — chips expose the base colour. Through-body is more expensive but essential for high-traffic areas, commercial projects, and any installation where durability and appearance under wear are critical.

**Q621:** What is "lapato" or "semi-polished" finish?
**A:** Lapato (also called semi-polished or honed polished) is a finish that sits between matte and fully polished. The tile surface undergoes a light polishing process that removes the matte texture without achieving full gloss. The result catches light softly, creates subtle luminosity, and feels refined without the high-maintenance reflectivity of a full polish. Lapato is increasingly popular in residential living spaces and boutique hospitality for its understated luxury.

**Q622:** What does "digital glaze variation" or V-rating mean?

**A:** Polished stone has a glossy, reflective surface that maximises colour depth — it's stunning but shows scratches and etching readily. Honed stone has a smooth, matte finish that feels velvety and hides wear much better — our most recommended finish for residential floors. Brushed (or leathered) stone has a textured, tactile surface created by brushing the stone with abrasive — it offers excellent slip resistance and a rustic, organic character. Each finish transforms the same stone into a different design statement.

**Q624:** What is "water absorption" and why does it matter?

**Q625:** What's the difference between "full-bodied" and "double-charged" porcelain?
**A:** Full-bodied (or through-body) porcelain has consistent colour and pattern throughout the entire tile body. Double-charged porcelain is created by feeding a layer of coloured powder into the press before the main body powder, creating a surface layer that's typically 3-4mm deep. Double-charged offers a cost-effective middle ground — better than surface-glazed for wear resistance, though not as consistent through the full depth as true through-body. It's popular for commercial flooring.

**Q626:** What does "modular" mean in tile terminology?
**A:** A modular collection includes multiple tile sizes that share the same design and are proportionally scaled to work together visually. For example, a modular system might include 600×600mm, 600×300mm, 300×300mm, and 75×300mm border tiles, all from the same design family. This allows designers to create dynamic layouts — field tiles in the large format, borders in the small format — while maintaining perfect visual cohesion.

**Q627:** What's a "tile trim" or "profiles" and when are they used?
**A:** Tile trims (also called profiles or edge trims) are metal, PVC, or matching tile strips that protect and finish exposed tile edges. They're used at: wall corners and external corners; transitions between different floor materials; perimeter edges where tiles meet a different surface; and step nosing for safety. Schluter and similar systems offer trims in various metals, finishes, and profiles that can complement or contrast with your tile selection. We stock a comprehensive range.

**Q628:** What is "substrate preparation" and why is it critical?
**A:** Substrate preparation is everything that happens to the floor or wall surface before tiles are installed. It includes levelling (screeding), cleaning, priming, and ensuring adequate strength and stability. Poor substrate preparation is the leading cause of tile installation failures — cracked tiles, hollow drummy sounds, and debonding. We strongly recommend that your specification includes a requirement for the substrate to meet SANS 10107 standards before tiling commences.

**Q629:** What's the difference between flexible and rigid adhesive?
**A:** Flexible adhesive (also called polymer-modified or S1/S2 adhesive) contains additives that allow it to absorb movement and vibration without cracking. It's required for: wooden or plywood substrates; underfloor heating systems; exterior applications; and areas subject to structural movement. Rigid adhesive is suitable only for stable, non-moving substrates like concrete slabs in dry interior areas. For most professional specifications, flexible adhesive is the safer default.

**Q630:** What is "tanking" or waterproofing in tile installations?
**A:** Tanking is the process of creating a waterproof barrier beneath tiled surfaces in wet areas — showers, bathrooms, balconies, and rooftops. It involves applying a liquid waterproofing membrane (typically two coats) to the walls and floor before tiling, with reinforcement at joints and corners. SANS 10400-L requires waterproofing in all wet areas, and proper tanking is your insurance against water damage, mould, and structural failure. Never skip it.

**Q631:** How thick should tile adhesive bed be?
**A:** For standard wall and floor tiles, adhesive bed thickness ranges from 3mm to 6mm using the thin-bed method, which is suitable for tiles up to approximately 600×600mm on flat, well-prepared substrates. For larger format tiles (900×900mm and above) and uneven substrates, the thick-bed method (10-20mm) may be required. Your tiler should use a notched trowel of appropriate size for the tile format — larger tiles require larger notches for full adhesive coverage.

**Q632:** What's the maximum tile size that can be used on walls?
**A:** Technically, very large tiles can be installed on walls using the correct adhesive and techniques. However, the weight of the tile and the strength of the substrate are limiting factors. On a standard plastered masonry wall, 600×600mm is typically the practical maximum without additional support. For larger wall tiles — 1200×600mm and beyond — the wall substrate needs to be solid, flat, and able to support the weight. Special adhesives and mechanical fixing aids may be required.

**Q633:** What does "nominal size" versus "actual size" mean for tiles?
**A:** Nominal size is the stated dimension (e.g., 600×600mm), while actual size is the precise measured dimension of the tile (e.g., 598×598mm for a rectified tile, or 595×595mm for a pressed tile). The difference accounts for manufacturing tolerances and the grout joint. When calculating quantities, always use the nominal size for area coverage, but be aware that the actual tile will be slightly smaller to accommodate the grout gap.

**Q634:** What's the difference between "pressed" and "rectified" edges?
**A:** Pressed tiles are shaped in a mould and have a natural, slightly rounded edge with some size variation between tiles — they require wider grout joints (3mm+). Rectified tiles have their edges mechanically ground after firing to achieve precise, consistent dimensions and sharp 90-degree edges — allowing for tight grout joints (1.5-2mm) and a seamless appearance. Rectified tiles cost more but deliver a significantly more refined result.

**Q635:** What are "movement joints" versus "structural joints"?
**A:** Structural joints are gaps in the building's structure that allow for major movement — they're designed by the engineer and must be maintained through the tile installation. Movement joints (also called perimeter joints or intermediate joints) are gaps in the tiled surface itself that accommodate thermal expansion, contraction, and minor substrate movement. Both must be filled with flexible sealant, never rigid grout. The tiler must identify and respect both types of joints.

**Q636:** What is "point load" and why might it matter for my tile specification?

**Q637:** What's the difference between "tiling" and "cladding"?
**A:** Tiling refers to installing tiles on floors and walls using adhesive and grout, with full bedding coverage. Cladding refers to fixing larger-format tiles, stone panels, or porcelain slabs to walls using mechanical fixing systems, particularly for exterior facades and large interior feature walls. Cladding requires different engineering considerations, including wind load calculations for exterior applications. Our technical team can advise if your project involves cladding rather than conventional tiling.

**Q638:** What is "tiling over existing tiles" and is it advisable?
**A:** Tiling over existing tiles is possible but not generally recommended for professional specifications. While it saves time and demolition cost, it adds weight to the structure, reduces ceiling height/door clearances, and the success depends entirely on the bond between the existing tiles and the substrate — which you can't verify. If the existing tiles are well-bonded and the additional height is acceptable, use a flexible adhesive and ensure the surface is clean and primed. For high-end projects, proper removal and substrate preparation is always the better route.

**Q639:** What's the difference between "wet areas" and "dry areas" in building regulations?
**A:** Wet areas are defined in SANS 10400-L as spaces subject to regular water exposure — showers, bathrooms, laundries, kitchens, balconies, and rooftops. These areas require waterproofing (tanking), appropriate slip-resistant flooring, and moisture-resistant substrate preparation. Dry areas are all other interior spaces. The distinction matters because building inspectors will check for compliance with wet area regulations, and failure to meet these standards can invalidate warranties and insurance claims.

**Q640:** What does "green screed" versus "dry screed" mean?
**A:** A green screed is a fresh cement screed that is still curing — typically less than 28 days old. Tiles should never be installed on a green screed because the moisture released during curing will prevent proper adhesive bonding and can cause efflorescence (white salt deposits) on the tile surface. A dry screed has cured for at least 28 days and has a moisture content below the threshold specified by the adhesive manufacturer. Always verify screed maturity before tiling.

**Q641:** What's "efflorescence" and how do I prevent it?
**A:** Efflorescence is the white, powdery deposit that appears on tile surfaces and grout lines when moisture draws soluble salts from the cement-based substrate or adhesive to the surface. It's unsightly and difficult to remove permanently. Prevention measures include: ensuring the substrate is fully dry before tiling; using low-alkali adhesive; installing a damp-proof membrane where rising damp is a concern; and ensuring adequate ventilation in the tiled area. If efflorescence appears, it can be treated with specialist cleaners, but prevention is far preferable.

**Q642:** What is "tile lippage" and how is it controlled?
**A:** Lippage is the height difference between adjacent tiles — when one edge sits higher than its neighbour, creating a trip hazard and an unsightly surface. Lippage is controlled by: ensuring a flat substrate; using the correct adhesive bed thickness; employing tile levelling systems (clips and wedges); and skilled trowel technique. For large-format tiles, levelling systems are essential. The acceptable lippage limit is typically 1mm for tiles up to 300mm length and 2mm for larger tiles.

**Q643:** What does "tile adhesive open time" mean?
**A:** Open time is the period after spreading adhesive during which tiles can be placed and adjusted before a skin forms on the adhesive surface. If tiles are placed after the open time has expired, the adhesive won't bond properly, leading to hollow sounds and potential debonding. Open time varies by adhesive type and ambient conditions — hot, dry, or windy conditions reduce open time significantly. Experienced tilers work in manageable sections and check the adhesive surface before placing each tile.

**Q644:** What's the difference between "bonded" and "unbonded" screeds?
**A:** A bonded screed is applied directly onto a concrete slab with bonding agent, typically at 15-40mm thickness. It's used when the existing slab is sound but needs levelling. An unbonded screed is separated from the substrate by a damp-proof membrane, typically at 50mm+ thickness, and is used over concrete slabs with damp issues or over suspended floors. A floating screed sits on insulation and is used with underfloor heating. Each type has specific requirements for tile installation.

**Q645:** What are tile levelling systems, and should I specify them?
**A:** Tile levelling systems are mechanical aids — typically plastic clips and wedges — that hold adjacent tiles at the same height during adhesive curing, preventing lippage. They're essential for large-format tiles (600×600mm and above) and strongly recommended for rectified tiles with tight joints. The clips are left in place, the wedges are removed after the adhesive sets, and the clips are either hidden by grout or snapped off at the base. We recommend specifying levelling systems for any precision installation.

**Q646:** What's "back-buttering" and when is it necessary?

**Q647:** What does "curing time" mean for tile adhesive?
**A:** Curing time is the period required for tile adhesive to fully harden and develop its design strength. Standard cement-based adhesives typically require 24 hours before grouting and 28 days before full traffic load. Rapid-setting adhesives cure faster (groutable in 3-4 hours) but are more expensive. During curing, the tiled area must be protected from traffic, water, and extreme temperatures. Your project programme should include adequate curing time before handover.

**Q648:** What's the difference between "unglazed" and "glazed" porcelain?
**A:** Glazed porcelain has a glass-like coating applied to the surface, which provides colour, pattern, and stain resistance. The glaze can replicate, wood, concrete, or fabric textures with remarkable realism. Unglazed porcelain has no surface coating — the colour and pattern are in the tile body itself. Unglazed tiles are more slip-resistant (ideal for commercial and outdoor use) but can stain if not sealed. Glazed tiles offer greater design flexibility and are easier to clean.

**Q649:** What does "ISO 13006 classification" mean for tiles?
**A:** ISO 13006 is the international standard for ceramic tiles. It classifies tiles by their manufacturing method (pressed or extruded), water absorption (Groups AI to BIII), and intended use (wall or floor). The classification determines the tile's suitability for different applications. All tiles we stock conform to ISO 13006, and our technical datasheets include the relevant classification codes. This is particularly useful when specifying for commercial projects that require documented compliance.

**Q650:** What's the significance of "Mohs hardness" for tiles?

**Q651:** What is "frost resistance" and do I need it in South Africa?
**A:** Frost resistance is a tile's ability to withstand freeze-thaw cycles without cracking. While South Africa doesn't have extreme winters, frost can occur in high-altitude areas (Johannesburg highveld, Drakensberg, parts of the Karoo) and is a consideration for outdoor installations in these regions. All porcelain tiles with water absorption below 0.5% are inherently frost-resistant. If you're specifying outdoor tiles for frost-prone areas, confirm frost resistance with your consultant.

**Q652:** What does " Shade variation" mean on a tile box?
**A:** Shade variation (often printed as a batch or shade number on the tile box) indicates that the tiles in that box come from a single production batch with consistent colour. Tiles from different shade numbers may have visible colour differences. For large installations, always order tiles from the same shade batch. For very large areas, we recommend ordering all tiles at once and blending tiles from multiple boxes during installation to ensure even colour distribution. Your tiler should be briefed on this before starting.

**Q653:** What's the difference between "floor tile" and "wall tile" ratings?
**A:** Floor tiles must pass strength, abrasion, and slip-resistance tests. They can also be used on walls. Wall tiles are not tested for these properties and should never be used on floors — they're thinner, softer, and lack the structural integrity to withstand foot traffic. Always check the tile's rating: if it's rated for floors (indicated by a foot symbol on the packaging or datasheet), it's safe for both floor and wall. If it's wall-only, keep it on the wall.

**Q654:** What are "tile spacers" and what size should I specify?

**Q655:** What does "LHT" mean in adhesive terminology?

**Q656:** What is "tile warpage" and how does it affect installation?
**A:** Warpage is a slight curvature or bow in a tile, which is a normal manufacturing characteristic, particularly in large-format tiles. When installed, warpage can cause lippage if not managed correctly. Skilled tilers lay warped tiles with the concave side down and use levelling systems to ensure adjacent tiles sit flat. The warpage tolerance is specified in manufacturing standards — rectified tiles have tighter warpage tolerances than pressed tiles. We only stock tiles within acceptable warpage limits.

**Q657:** What's the difference between "grout" and "silicone sealant"?
**A:** Grout is a cement-based or epoxy-based material used to fill the joints between tiles. It's rigid once cured and is suitable for the main tiled surface. Silicone sealant is a flexible material used in expansion joints, perimeter gaps, and junctions between different materials (e.g., tile to bath, tile to window frame). Silicone accommodates movement without cracking, while grout does not. Both are necessary in a proper installation — grout for tile joints, silicone for movement joints.

**Q658:** What does "full coverage" mean in tile installation?
**A:** Full coverage means the adhesive completely covers the back of the tile with no voids or hollow spots. It's essential for structural integrity, sound reduction, and waterproofing in wet areas. Industry standards require minimum 80% coverage for dry areas and 100% for wet areas and exterior applications. Back-buttering large tiles and using the correct trowel size are the best ways to ensure full coverage. You can check coverage by occasionally removing a tile during installation.

**Q659:** What is "anti-fracture membrane" and when should I specify it?
**A:** An anti-fracture membrane (also called an uncoupling membrane) is a specialised underlayment that separates the tile installation from the substrate, preventing cracks in the substrate from transferring through to the tile surface. Specify it when: tiling over substrates with existing fine cracks; installing over timber or plywood; working with underfloor heating; or in any situation where substrate movement is anticipated. Schluter-DITRA is the most recognised brand, and we can supply it.

**Q660:** What's the "bedding" method for tile installation?
**A:** Bedding refers to the method of applying adhesive beneath the tile. Thin-bed bedding (3-6mm) is the standard for most tile installations using a notched trowel. Thick-bed bedding (10-20mm+ or even 40mm) is used for large-format tiles, uneven substrates, or exterior installations where greater adhesive depth is needed to achieve a flat, level surface. The bedding method should be specified based on tile size, substrate condition, and application.

**Q661:** What does "tile break strength" mean?
**A:** Break strength measures the force required to break a tile, expressed in newtons (N). Higher break strength indicates a stronger, more durable tile. Porcelain tiles typically have break strengths of 1,300N or higher, while ceramic tiles may be 600-900N. For commercial floors, heavy-traffic residential areas, and any installation where durability is critical, specify tiles with higher break strength ratings. We include break strength data in our technical specifications.

**Q662:** What's the difference between "Class 1" and "Class 5" tile adhesive?
**A:** Adhesive classification is manufacturer-specific, but generally Class 1 refers to standard cement-based adhesive suitable for interior walls and light-traffic floors on stable substrates. Higher classifications (Class 2, Class 3, S1, S2) indicate progressively enhanced properties — improved flexibility, bond strength, and resistance to moisture, heat, and chemicals. For professional specifications, we typically recommend S1-rated flexible adhesive as the minimum standard.

**Q663:** What is "tile layout planning" and why should I do it before installation?
**A:** Tile layout planning is the process of determining exactly how tiles will be arranged before any adhesive is spread. It involves measuring the room, accounting for fixed elements (doors, windows, fixtures), and deciding where cuts will fall. Proper planning ensures: balanced cuts (not tiny slivers at edges); centre-line symmetry where appropriate; efficient tile usage with minimal waste; and a harmonious visual result. We provide layout planning assistance for complex installations.


**Q665:** What does "salt resistance" mean for tiles?

**Q666:** What's the difference between "single-fired" and "double-fired" tiles?
**A:** Single-fired (monocottura) tiles are shaped, glazed, and fired once at high temperature — this is the modern standard for most porcelain tiles, producing a strong, dense body with well-fused glaze. Double-fired (bicottura) tiles are fired once as a biscuit, then glazed and fired again at a lower temperature — traditionally used for decorative wall tiles and some ceramic tiles. For floor applications, single-fired porcelain is the superior and more common choice.

**Q667:** What is "tile patina" and how does it develop?
**Q668:** What's the difference between "direct sticking" and "suspended ceiling" tile installation?
**A:** Direct sticking means adhering tiles directly to the substrate wall or floor using adhesive — this is the standard method for most tile installations. Suspended ceiling installation refers to hanging ceiling tiles from a metal framework, which is more common in commercial buildings with services running above the ceiling. For wall and floor tiles in residential and commercial interiors, direct sticking is the standard method. Our consultants and technical team can advise on the appropriate installation method for any application.

**Q669:** What does "chemical resistance" mean for tiles?

**Q670:** What's the significance of "impact resistance" for tiles?

**Q671:** What does "thermal shock resistance" mean?

**Q672:** What's the "working time" of tile adhesive?
**A:** Working time (also called pot life) is the period after mixing during which adhesive remains workable and can be spread. Standard cement adhesive has a working time of approximately 2-3 hours. Rapid-set adhesive may have 30-60 minutes. Epoxy adhesive typically has 45-60 minutes. Hot, dry conditions reduce working time, so tilers mix smaller batches in summer. Once adhesive starts to stiffen or skin over, it must be discarded — never re-mix with water.

**Q673:** What does "tile gauge" mean?

**Q674:** What's the difference between "ceramic glaze" and "porcelain glaze"?
**A:** Ceramic glazes are applied to the surface of ceramic tiles and fired at lower temperatures (typically 1,000-1,100°C). Porcelain glazes are applied to porcelain biscuit and fired at higher temperatures (1,200-1,400°C), resulting in a harder, more durable, more vitrified glaze that's more resistant to abrasion, chemicals, and staining. For floor applications, glazed porcelain is always preferable to glazed ceramic.

**Q675:** What is "tile porosity" and why does it matter?

**Q676:** What's a "tile schedule" and should I create one for my projects?
**A:** A tile schedule is a document that lists every tile product specified in a project, including: product name, manufacturer, size, colour, finish, quantity, PEI rating, slip rating, and intended location. It's an essential professional tool that ensures clear communication between designer, client, quantity surveyor, and tiler. We can provide product specification sheets for each tile in your schedule, making the document creation process straightforward.

**Q677:** What does "field tile" versus "feature tile" mean?
**A:** Field tiles are the main,大面积 tiles that cover the majority of a surface — they're typically neutral, understated, and designed to create a calm backdrop. Feature tiles are the accent pieces that create visual interest — patterned tiles, textured tiles, mosaics, or bold-coloured tiles used in specific areas like splashbacks, shower niches, or entrance floors. The relationship between field and feature tiles is one of the fundamental principles of tile design. We curate our collections to ensure compatible field and feature pairings.

**Q678:** What's the difference between "bullnose" and "pencil" tiles?
**A:** A bullnose tile has a rounded, finished edge designed to cap the exposed edge of a tiled surface — ideal for wall edges, window reveals, and step fronts. A pencil tile (or liner tile) is a narrow decorative strip, typically 20-30mm wide, used as an accent border within a tiled surface. Both serve to finish and detail a tile installation. While traditional, they're less commonly used in contemporary design where metal profiles and rectified edges have become preferred.

**Q679:** What does "modular layout" mean in tile design?
**A:** A modular layout uses multiple tile sizes from the same collection arranged in a predetermined pattern — for example, a French pattern using four sizes (large, medium, small, and accent) in a repeating layout that mimics the random arrangement of traditional stone floors. Modular layouts create visual complexity and old-world character while maintaining the practical benefits of modern porcelain. Many of our stone-effect collections offer modular layout options.

**Q680:** What's the most common mistake designers make when specifying tiles?
**A:** The most common mistakes are: not verifying slip ratings for wet areas; specifying wall tiles for floor applications; failing to account for substrate preparation requirements; ordering insufficient quantities without allowing for wastage and future repairs; not communicating V-rating expectations to clients and tilers; neglecting expansion joint requirements; and specifying tiles without confirming stock availability and lead times. Working closely with our consultants helps avoid all of these pitfalls.


## Category 18: Client Presentation Support

**Q681:** Can Tiletoria help me create a compelling tile presentation for my client?
**A:** Absolutely. We offer multiple presentation support tools: free sample loans of full-size tiles for up to three weeks so your client can see and touch the actual material; cut-to-size samples for mood boards and sample boxes; our lookbooks and collection brochures with high-resolution photography; and for Platinum Design Circle members, complimentary professional product photography. Our consultants can also meet with you and your client in the showroom to talk through the specification and answer technical questions.

**Q682:** Do you have high-quality images of your tiles that I can use in my client presentations?
**A:** Yes, we provide high-resolution product photography for our collections, available to registered Design Circle members. These images are suitable for use in mood boards, concept presentations, and client proposals. We also share lifestyle and installation photography on our social media channels that can inspire your presentations. For Platinum members, we offer bespoke product photography of your specified selections. Contact your account manager to access our image library.

**Q683:** Can I borrow samples to take to my client's home or office?
**A:** Yes, our sample loan service allows you to borrow full-size tiles for up to three weeks — ample time for client presentations, site visits, and coordination with other material selections. There's no charge for sample loans. Simply visit the showroom or contact your account manager to arrange the samples you need. We trust our design community to return samples promptly so they're available for others.

**Q684:** Do you provide product specification sheets that I can include in my documentation?
**A:** Yes, we provide detailed product specification sheets for every tile and stone in our range. These include: product name and code; dimensions and thickness; PEI rating; slip rating; water absorption; shade variation rating; finish description; coverage per square metre; weight per square metre; and installation recommendations. These sheets can be included directly in your specification documentation, saving you hours of research and formatting.

**Q685:** Can your showroom consultants join my client presentation meeting?
**A:** Yes, our showroom consultants are available to join client meetings, either in the showroom or virtually. They bring deep product knowledge that can answer technical questions and reinforce the value of your specification. Having a supplier expert in the room adds credibility to your proposal and ensures your client understands why the specified products are the right choice. Arrange this through your account manager.

**Q686:** Do you have lookbooks or catalogues I can leave with my clients?
**A:** Yes, we produce printed lookbooks and collection catalogues that showcase our ranges with beautiful photography and detailed product information. These are available for Design Circle members to share with clients. Our lookbooks are curated by design theme — contemporary, classic, coastal, industrial — making it easy for clients to browse and visualise possibilities. Digital versions are also available for email sharing.

**Q687:** Can you create a curated sample box for a specific project?
**A:** Yes, we can prepare a bespoke sample box containing your selected tiles, along with coordinating grout colour samples, trim profiles, and relevant product literature. This creates a tactile, professional presentation tool that your client can keep and reference throughout the decision-making process. It's a powerful way to communicate your design vision and build client confidence in your specification.

**Q688:** Do you have installation photography I can show my clients for reference?
**A:** Yes, we have an extensive library of installed project photography showing our tiles in real residential, hospitality, and commercial settings. These images help clients understand scale, pattern, and finished appearance far better than product shots alone. Our Instagram feed and lookbooks are rich sources of installation inspiration. We can also direct you to specific project types — "show me this tile in a bathroom" or "show me herringbone installations."

**Q689:** Can I use Tiletoria's showroom to present options to my client?
**A:** Absolutely, and it's one of the most effective ways to help clients make confident decisions. Walking through the showroom allows your client to see tiles at scale, in realistic room settings, under proper lighting, and alongside coordinating products. Our consultants can guide the presentation, answer questions, and help narrow options. It's far more impactful than reviewing small samples in your office or sending digital images.

**Q690:** Do you provide comparison samples to help clients understand price-quality differences?
**A:** Yes, we can prepare comparison sets that show the difference between entry-level, mid-range, and premium options for the same aesthetic category — for example, three levels of-effect porcelain. This helps clients understand what they're paying for at each tier and makes the value proposition tangible. When clients can see, touch, and compare, they typically make more informed and confident decisions.

**Q691:** Can you provide written quotes that I can present to my clients?
**A:** Yes, we provide professionally formatted written quotations that you can present directly to your clients or incorporate into your own documentation. Quotes include product descriptions, quantities, unit prices, and totals. Design Circle trade pricing is shown separately, protecting your margin. We can also provide multiple options within your brief so clients can see how different choices affect the total investment.

**Q692:** Do you have case studies of completed projects I can reference?
**A:** Yes, we maintain a portfolio of completed projects across residential, hospitality, and commercial sectors. These case studies include project photography, product details, and designer credits (where permission has been granted). They're valuable for showing clients what's possible and for demonstrating the quality of outcomes when our products are specified well. Ask your account manager for case studies relevant to your project type.

**Q693:** Can I get small cut samples for a sample board?
**A:** Yes, we can provide cut-to-size samples for mood boards and sample presentations. These smaller pieces are ideal for showing clients the colour, texture, and material character alongside fabric swatches, paint chips, and timber samples. While full-size samples show scale and impact, cut samples are perfect for palette coordination. Let your consultant know the sizes you need for your sample board.

**Q694:** How do I explain the difference between rectified and pressed tiles to my client?
**A:** Explain it in visual terms: rectified tiles have precisely cut edges that allow for very narrow grout joints — about the thickness of a credit card — creating a seamless, modern look. Pressed tiles have a slight natural edge and require wider joints, which reads as more traditional. Show them both in the showroom, and they'll immediately see and feel the difference. Most clients instinctively prefer the refined look of rectified once they understand it.

**Q695:** Can you help me explain why the tile I've specified costs more than a basic alternative?
**A:** Yes, our consultants can help you articulate the value difference between your specified product and a basic alternative. Key points typically include: rectification precision and tighter joints; through-body colour versus surface-only printing; larger format creating a more seamless look; imported manufacturing quality; realistic material replication; and superior durability over the lifetime of the installation. When clients understand what they're investing in, they rarely choose to downgrade.

**Q696:** Do you have videos or virtual tours that I can share with remote clients?
**A:** Yes, we have video content including showroom tours, product highlights, and installation showcases available through our website and social media channels. We can also arrange virtual showroom tours via video call for clients who cannot visit in person. This is particularly useful for out-of-town clients or international investors in South African property. Contact your account manager to arrange a virtual tour.

**Q697:** Can I get a mock-up or sample installation of a particular tile layout?
**A:** For large or complex projects, we can create sample panels showing specific laying patterns — herringbone, basket weave, modular layouts, or custom patterns. This allows your client to approve the pattern and visual effect before the full installation commences. There may be a nominal cost for bespoke sample panels depending on the complexity. Discuss this with your account manager for significant projects.

**Q698:** How do I present grout colour options to my client?

**Q699:** Do you provide 3D render material files or digital swatches?
**A:** For some of our collections, we can provide digital colour references and material specifications that can be used in 3D rendering software. While we don't supply pre-built texture maps for every product, our technical team can advise on creating accurate digital representations of our tiles for your renderings. High-resolution product photography can also be used as texture references.

**Q700:** Can you prepare a presentation pack for a competitive design pitch?
**A:** Yes, we can support your design pitches with curated product presentations, technical data sheets, pricing, and even showroom access for pitch preparation. For significant commercial or hospitality projects, we can arrange a dedicated pitch session with our team, including product samples, installation case studies, and a walkthrough of how we'll support the project through to completion. Your win is our win.

**Q701:** What's the best way to help a client visualise a large-format tile in their space?
**A:** The most effective approach is a combination of: seeing the full-size tile in our showroom (scale is impossible to appreciate from a photo); a sample loan so they can place it in their actual space and see it under their lighting; installation photography showing the same tile in a similar room type; and if budget allows, a sample panel installed on their wall or floor as a permanent mock-up. Large-format tiles transform spaces — the challenge is helping clients imagine the transformation before it happens.

**Q702:** Can I get colour-matched grout and silicone samples for my presentations?
**A:** Yes, we can provide colour-matched grout samples and silicone sealant colour cards alongside your tile selections. Having the complete material palette — tile, grout, trim, and sealant — in one presentation gives your client confidence that every detail has been considered. It's these finishing touches that distinguish a professional specification from an amateur one.

**Q703:** Do you have any tools to help me calculate quantities and pricing for client proposals?
**A:** Our quotations include detailed quantity calculations based on your floor plans and elevations. We add appropriate wastage factors (typically 10% for standard layouts, 15% for complex patterns or large formats) and round up to full box quantities. For preliminary budgeting, our consultants can provide ballpark pricing based on room dimensions and product category. This helps you present realistic budgets to your clients early in the design process.

**Q704:** How do I handle a client who wants to change my tile specification to something cheaper?
**A:** This is a common challenge. Our approach: first, ensure the client understands what they're losing — show them the difference in quality, finish, and long-term durability. Often the price gap is smaller than they expect when spread across the project's lifetime. Second, explore whether we have alternative products within our range that meet the design intent at a lower price point. Third, if the client insists on a significant downgrade, document the change and its implications for the design outcome. We're here to support you through these conversations.

**Q705:** Can your team help me prepare a "good, better, best" pricing structure for clients?
**A:** Yes, this is a presentation approach we often recommend. We can prepare three pricing tiers for the same design intent: "good" — a quality entry-level option that achieves the look; "better" — a mid-range option with enhanced features; "best" — the premium specification with all the refinements. This framework gives clients agency in the decision while keeping them within your design direction. It's particularly effective for clients with flexible budgets.

**Q706:** Do you have any before-and-after project photography I can use?
**A:** Where available and with client permission, we have before-and-after photography that demonstrates the transformative power of a well-executed tile installation. These are powerful presentation tools because they show the full potential of the material investment. Ask your account manager if we have before-and-after examples relevant to your project type.

**Q707:** Can I create a physical sample board using your products to leave with my client?
**A:** Yes, we encourage designers to create physical sample boards that combine our tile samples with fabric swatches, paint chips, timber samples, and metal finishes. These tactile presentations are far more impactful than digital mood boards alone. We can provide cut samples of your specified tiles along with product cards that describe each material. A well-crafted sample board becomes a reference point that clients return to throughout the project.

**Q708:** Do you offer any augmented reality (AR) tools for visualising tiles in a space?
**A:** We're exploring augmented reality and digital visualisation tools that will allow you and your clients to see how our tiles look in your actual space using a smartphone or tablet. While this technology is still evolving, we currently offer high-quality lifestyle photography, showroom visits, and sample loans as our primary visualisation tools. Ask your account manager about the latest digital tools we have available.

**Q709:** How do I present the long-term value of investing in quality tiles?
**A:** Frame the investment in lifetime terms: quality porcelain tiles last 20+ years with minimal maintenance; they resist staining, fading, and wear; they add resale value to the property; and they never need the replacement, regrouting, or refinishing that cheaper alternatives may require. Calculate the cost per year rather than the upfront cost — a R500/m² tile that lasts 25 years costs R20 per year. That's extraordinary value for a surface you touch every single day.

**Q710:** Can I get a letter of supply or confirmation of availability for a tender submission?
**A:** Yes, for commercial tenders and formal project submissions, we can provide letters confirming product availability, lead times, and pricing validity. We can also provide technical datasheets, compliance certificates, and sample materials for tender boards. Contact your account manager well in advance of the tender deadline to ensure all documentation is prepared accurately and on time.

**Q711:** Do you have any client-facing brochures about tile care and maintenance?

**Q712:** Can you help me explain technical specifications to my client in simple terms?
**A:** Absolutely. Our team is skilled at translating technical specifications into language that non-technical clients can understand. We can join client meetings, provide written explanations, or create comparison sheets that explain why certain specifications matter — in terms of safety, durability, and appearance. You don't need to be the technical expert; you just need to know where to find the answers. That's what we're here for.


**Q714:** Can I arrange for my client to visit a completed project using your tiles?
**A:** Where we have relationships with past clients who are open to site visits, we can occasionally arrange for prospective clients to see a completed installation. There's nothing quite like walking on a finished floor or seeing how a bathroom has settled in after a year of use. This is subject to the past client's availability and consent, but it's a powerful tool for building confidence. Ask your account manager about site visit possibilities.

**Q715:** Do you have any video testimonials from designers or clients I can share?
**A:** We periodically capture testimonials from designers and clients who've had positive experiences with our products and service. These are available through our website and social media channels. Video testimonials are particularly powerful because they bring authentic voices to the quality conversation. We can also connect you with designers in our community who are happy to share their experiences with prospective clients.

**Q716:** Can I get a sample of the tile installed as a small mock-up panel?
**A:** For significant projects where the client needs to see the tile in context before committing, we can arrange a small mock-up installation — perhaps a 1-metre square section of floor or a wall panel. This allows the client to see the grout colour, the laying pattern, and the tile under actual lighting conditions. There may be a cost for this service, but it's invaluable for building confidence on large investments.

**Q717:** How do I help my client choose between matte and polished finishes?
**A:** Take them through the decision systematically: polished reflects more light and makes spaces feel brighter and more formal, but shows scratches and water spots; matte is understated, hides wear, and feels more contemporary, but can make spaces feel slightly darker; lapato sits between them. Show both in the showroom, explain the maintenance implications, and help them connect the finish to the mood they want to create.

**Q718:** Do you have any "designer tips" content I can share with my clients?
**A:** Yes, we regularly produce design tip content through our blog, social media, and newsletters — covering topics like how to choose grout colour, tile layout patterns that add impact, small bathroom design tricks, and mixing tile sizes for visual interest. This content positions you as a knowledgeable resource for your clients and helps them engage more confidently in the design process. Follow us on social media and subscribe to our newsletter for regular tips.

**Q719:** Can you provide a comparison of different stone types for my client's fireplace?
**A:** Yes, we can prepare a comparison of stone types suitable for fireplace surrounds — covering heat resistance, appearance, finish options, maintenance requirements, and price points.offers drama and luxury;provides durability and consistency; porcelain tile brings warmth and softness;adds texture and character. Each stone tells a different story around the hearth. We can provide samples of each for your client to compare.

**Q720:** How do I present tile layout pattern options to my client?
**A:** Use our installation photography to show the visual effect of different laying patterns: straight stack (clean, modern); brick bond/offset (classic, forgiving); herringbone (dynamic, sophisticated); basket weave (traditional, intricate); modular (organic, stone-like). Sketch the patterns on their floor plan to show how each affects the room's proportions. For large formats, a straight stack with tight joints is usually the most impactful.

**Q721:** Can you help me explain why underfloor heating is worth the investment with tiles?
**A:** Absolutely. Underfloor heating transforms tiles from a cold surface into a warm, luxurious experience — particularly in bathrooms and living areas. It's energy-efficient (operates at lower temperatures than conventional heating), invisible (no radiators or vents), and adds significant property value. Porcelain is an excellent conductor of heat, making it the ideal partner for underfloor systems. We can explain the installation requirements and connect you with heating specialists.

**Q722:** Do you have any seasonal or promotional collections I can reference for budget-sensitive clients?
**A:** We periodically run promotions on select collections, offering designers the opportunity to specify premium products at reduced prices. Design Circle members receive advance notification of all promotions. These can be excellent opportunities for budget-sensitive clients to access higher-quality products than their initial budget suggested. Ask your account manager about current and upcoming promotional opportunities.

**Q723:** Can I get a finish sample showing the edge detail of a trim profile?
**A:** Yes, we can provide physical samples of tile trim profiles — showing the cross-section, the finish quality, and how the profile meets the tile edge. This is particularly useful when presenting to clients who are concerned about exposed tile edges in open-plan transitions or step details. Seeing the trim sample reassures them that the finish will be clean and professional.

**Q724:** How do I present the grout selection as a design decision rather than an afterthought?
**A:** Frame grout as the detail that elevates a good installation to a great one. Show examples: matching grout creating a seamless stone-slab effect; contrasting grout creating a graphic grid; coloured grout adding personality to a neutral tile. Explain that grout colour affects the room's proportions — dark grout makes a space feel smaller and more defined; light grout creates openness. When clients see grout as a design tool, they engage with the decision rather than deferring it.

**Q725:** Can you provide a sample of the tile under different lighting conditions?
**A:** While we can't control the lighting in your client's space, we can advise on how our tiles perform under different light sources — warm incandescent, cool LED, natural daylight. Our showroom has varied lighting zones that give a sense of how tiles read under different conditions. For critical colour decisions, we strongly recommend the sample loan service so your client can see the tile in their actual space under their actual lighting.

**Q726:** Do you have any client-friendly guides to tile selection?
**A:** Yes, we produce accessible guides that walk clients through the tile selection process — covering topics like how to choose the right size, understanding finishes, coordinating with other materials, and what to expect during installation. These guides complement your professional advice and help clients feel informed and confident. They're available as printed booklets or digital downloads through your account manager.

**Q727:** Can I get a mock invoice showing trade pricing versus retail for my records?
**A:** Your Design Circle trade pricing is itemised separately on all our quotations, making it easy for you to calculate your margin and present appropriate pricing to your client. If you need a specific format for your accounting or client presentation, speak to your account manager. We're flexible on documentation formats and want to make your procurement process as smooth as possible.


**Q729:** Can you provide a portfolio of your most impressive designer projects?
**A:** Yes, we maintain a curated portfolio of outstanding projects that have used our products — ranging from luxury residences to boutique hotels to award-winning commercial spaces. These projects are documented with professional photography and, where possible, include designer commentary on the material choices and design intent. This portfolio is a powerful tool for inspiring clients and demonstrating the calibre of work that our products support.

**Q730:** What's the best way to use Tiletoria's showroom as a closing tool with my client?
**A:** Bring your client to the showroom after you've narrowed the options to two or three strong candidates. Walk them through the displays, let them see the tiles at scale, and introduce them to your consultant who can answer any remaining questions. The showroom environment — professional, beautiful, and immersive — creates confidence in both your specification and the supplier. It's the equivalent of a test drive before purchase, and it significantly reduces post-decision regret.

## Cross-Category: Regional & Contextual Considerations

**Q731:** Does Tiletoria have showrooms outside of Gauteng?
**A:** Yes, in addition to our Spec Lab in Menlyn, we have showrooms in Durban (KwaZulu-Natal) and Cape Town (Western Cape). Each showroom carries our core collections with regional variations based on local design preferences and climate conditions. Our KZN showroom serves the coastal market with strong outdoor and pool tile ranges, while our Paarden Eiland showroom reflects the city's design-forward aesthetic. All showrooms offer Design Circle trade accounts and professional support.

**Q732:** How does coastal climate affect tile and stone selection in KZN?

**Q733:** Are there climate considerations for tile selection in Johannesburg's highveld?
**A:** Johannesburg's highveld climate — cold, dry winters and warm, wet summers — means tiles need to handle thermal cycling and occasional frost. All porcelain tiles are suitable for this climate. For outdoor areas, ensure tiles are frost-resistant (all porcelain qualifies). The dry winter air can cause timber to shrink, making wood-effect porcelain an excellent alternative for year-round stability. Underfloor heating is also very popular in Johannesburg, and all our porcelain tiles are compatible.

**Q734:** How does Cape Town's design aesthetic influence your product range there?
**A:** Cape Town's design sensibility — influenced by its natural landscape, Mediterranean climate, and international outlook — leans toward organic textures, warm neutrals, and indoor-outdoor flow. Our Paarden Eiland showroom emphasises stone-effect porcelain in warmand porcelain tile tones, zellige-effect tiles, and outdoor paver collections that connect interior and exterior living spaces. The aesthetic is less industrial and more nature-connected than Johannesburg.

**Q735:** Can you deliver to project sites outside of major metros?
**A:** Yes, we deliver to project sites across South Africa, including regional and rural locations. Delivery timelines and costs vary based on distance, with major metros serviced most frequently. For remote projects, we coordinate with trusted logistics partners to ensure safe transport. We recommend building additional lead time into your programme for regional deliveries, and our team will provide realistic delivery schedules for any location.

**Q736:** Do you have different stock levels in different regions?
**A:** Yes, each showroom maintains its own stock holding based on regional demand patterns. Core collections are stocked at all locations, while premium and specialist ranges may be concentrated at specific showrooms.
