**A77:** Natatorium flooring: pool surround (anti-slip porcelain, chlorine-resistant) R485–685/m²; changing rooms (anti-slip vinyl, welded) R225–325/m²; viewing gallery (standard porcelain) R325–485/m²; plant room (epoxy, chemical-resistant) R285–425/m². High humidity environment — all products humidity-rated.

**Q78:** Can you provide rates for a crematorium and memorial chapel?


**Q79:** Can you provide rates for a cricket stadium and sports complex?

**A79:** Sports stadium flooring: spectator areas (anti-slip porcelain, UV-stable) R485–685/m²; concourses (heavy-duty, chemical-resistant) R525–750/m²; VIP boxes (premium carpet or timber) R685–1,250/m²; changerooms (anti-slip vinyl, welded) R225–325/m²; media centre (standard commercial) R325–485/m².

**Q80:** Do you provide rates for a biotech laboratory and research facility?

**A80:** Biotech lab flooring: seamless epoxy (chemical-resistant, anti-static) R385–585/m²; coved skirting (seamless wall-floor junction) R55–85/Lm; acid-resistant joints; decontamination shower area (non-slip, drain-integrated) R485–685/m²; writeable floor finish (whiteboard epoxy) for protocol areas R425–625/m².

**Q81:** Can you provide rates for a TV studio and broadcast facility?

**A81:** TV studio flooring: camera-rated (matte, non-reflective) seamless epoxy R285–425/m²; presenter areas (premium carpet or timber) R485–685/m²; control room (anti-static vinyl) R225–325/m²; green room (standard commercial) R245–365/m². Critical: flatness ±2mm/3m, no camera glare, acoustic dampening. Studio downtime costs R50,000+/hour — fast installation essential.


---


## Category 2: Transparent Rate Build-Up

**Q82:** What does 'transparent rate build-up' mean at Tiletoria?

**A82:** Every rate we provide is **decomposed into its component parts**: (1) Supply price per m² ex-warehouse, (2) Delivery charge to site per m² (quantity-dependent), (3) Wastage allowance percentage (typically 8–10% for standard tiles, 12–15% for large-format or complex patterns), (4) Ancillary costs (adhesive, grout, trims, waterproofing if applicable), and (5) Labour coverage rate per m² (if supply-and-install). Each component is listed as a separate line item in our Excel rate schedules for direct database entry.

**Q83:** Why is decomposed pricing important for QS cost databases?

**A83:** Decomposed pricing allows you to: (1) **Update individual components** when one element changes (e.g., delivery cost rises but product cost stays flat), (2) Benchmark each component against your database averages, (3) Apply your own labour rates if our coverage rate doesn't match your METAG region, and (4) Escalate components at different rates (materials vs. labour CPI).

**Q84:** Can you provide a sample rate build-up for a standard porcelain floor tile?

**A84:** **Example — 600×600mm porcelain floor tile, PEI 4, mid-grade, Gauteng delivered:**

- Supply price (ex-warehouse): R185.00/m²
- Delivery to site (>500m²): R12.50/m²
- Wastage @ 8%: R15.80/m²
- Adhesive (flexible, 20kg bags): R28.40/m²
- Grout (anti-mould, 5kg): R8.60/m²
- Aluminium trim (straight): R6.20/m²
- **Total material rate: R256.50/m²**
- Installation labour: R85.00/m²
- **Total supply & install: R341.50/m²**

All components shown separately for database entry.

**Q85:** How is the delivery charge calculated?

**A85:** Delivery is calculated **per m² and quantity-banded**: Under 100m² = R25–35/m²; 100–300m² = R18–25/m²; 300–500m² = R14–18/m²; 500m²+ = R10–15/m². Full truckload (FLT) deliveries attract a flat rate per load rather than per m². Regional variances apply — KZN and Western Cape rates differ slightly.

**Q86:** What wastage percentage do you apply as standard?


**Q87:** Are ancillary costs (adhesive, grout, trims) included in the tile rate or separate?

**A87:** By default, ancillaries are shown as **separate line items** for full transparency. On request, we can consolidate into a single 'all-in' rate for preliminary cost plans. We recommend decomposition for tender-stage BOQs to enable substitution analysis.

**Q88:** Can I see the raw ex-warehouse price without delivery and wastage?

**A88:** Yes. Every rate response shows the **supply price ex-warehouse** as the base figure. Delivery, wastage, and ancillaries are added transparently. This allows you to compare our base supply price against other suppliers on a like-for-like basis.

**Q89:** How do you calculate adhesive coverage rates?

**A89:** Adhesive coverage is calculated based on: (1) **Trowel notch size** (3mm, 6mm, 10mm, 12mm), (2) **Tile back (buttered) or not**, (3) **Tile format** (larger tiles require buttering, reducing coverage). A 20kg bag of standard-set adhesive covers approximately 6m² at 3mm trowel, 4m² at 6mm trowel, or 2.5m² at 10mm trowel. We provide a coverage calculator with every adhesive rate.

**Q90:** How do you calculate grout coverage?

**A90:** Grout coverage depends on: (1) **Tile dimensions**, (2) **Joint width** (1.5mm, 2mm, 3mm, 5mm), (3) **Tile thickness**, and (4) **Grout type** (cementitious vs. epoxy). A 5kg unit of standard grout covers approximately 15m² for 300×300mm tiles at 3mm joint, or 8m² for 600×600mm tiles at 3mm joint. We provide a joint-width coverage matrix with every grout rate.

**Q91:** Is the labour coverage rate your own or sourced from industry data?

**A91:** Our labour coverage rates are derived from **agreements with certified tiling contractors** in each province, cross-referenced against METAG (Master Builders Association) published rates. We update quarterly and note the source and date on each rate schedule. You may substitute your own labour rate if preferred.

**Q92:** Can you provide a rate build-up for a full bathroom floor system?

**A92:** **Example — Standard bathroom floor (12m²), porcelain tile, cementitious waterproofing:**

- 600×600mm anti-slip floor tile: R245.00/m² (supply + delivery + 10% wastage)
- Cementitious waterproofing (2-coat): R95.00/m²
- Flexible tile adhesive: R32.00/m²
- Anti-mould grout: R9.50/m²
- Aluminium edge trim: R8.00/m²
- Silicone sealant (perimeter): R12.00/m²
- **Total system material rate: R401.50/m²**
- Installation labour (floor only): R92.00/m²
- **Total S&I rate: R493.50/m²**

**Q93:** How do you handle VAT in rate build-ups?

**A93:** All rates are quoted **VAT-exclusive** as standard for QS database entry, with a VAT calculation shown separately at 15%. We can provide VAT-inclusive rates on request for residential consumer quotes.

**Q94:** Do you show manufacturer rebates or volume discounts in the build-up?

**A94:** Volume discounts are shown as a **separate deduction line** below the subtotal if applicable. We do not hide rebates in inflated base prices — the ex-warehouse price is the genuine supply price before any project-specific volume rebate.

**Q95:** Can you break down the delivery cost into transport and fuel surcharge?

**A95:** Yes. For large projects (>R500k material value), we can decompose delivery into: **base transport rate + diesel surcharge (DAF-adjusted monthly) + toll fees + crane-offload if required**. This allows precise cost planning when fuel prices fluctuate.

**Q96:** How do you price for crane-offload or hi-ab delivery?

**A96:** Crane-offload or hi-ab delivery is priced as a **flat rate per delivery** (typically R2,500–R4,500 per offload depending on crane size and duration), not per m². We note this separately from standard tail-lift or hand-offload delivery rates.

**Q97:** Do you show the difference between bulk and retail packaging in the build-up?

**A97:** Yes. Where applicable, we note the **packaging unit** (e.g., '5 tiles per box, 1.08m² per box, 48 boxes per pallet') and price per box vs. per m². Full-pallet pricing typically carries a 3–5% discount vs. broken-pallet pricing.

**Q98:** Can you provide the rate build-up in my practice's standard template format?

**A98:** Yes. Provide your template and we'll populate it. We have **pre-configured mappings** for most major QS practice templates and can create a custom mapping for your specific format within 48 hours of first request.

**Q99:** How do you handle provisional items in the rate build-up?

**A99:** Provisional items are shown with a **[P]** flag and a ±10% tolerance band on the supply price component. Once confirmed, we issue a firm rate revision removing the tolerance flag.

**Q100:** Do you provide unit rates or only m² rates?

**A100:** We provide both **unit rates and m² rates** as standard. Tiles = per m² and per box; Adhesives/grouts = per bag and per m² coverage; Trims = per linear metre and per m² equivalent; Waterproofing = per litre/tub and per m² coverage.

**Q101:** What does 'all-in' rate mean vs. decomposed rate?

**A101:** An **'all-in' rate** is a single consolidated figure (e.g., R341.50/m² S&I) suitable for early-stage cost plans. A **decomposed rate** breaks out every component for database entry, substitution analysis, and escalation. We provide both simultaneously — the all-in rate for quick reference, the decomposition for analysis.

**Q102:** How do you handle price-on-application (POA) items?

**A102:** POA items are flagged with **[POA]** and a **budget estimate range** (e.g., R450–R650/m²) pending supplier confirmation. We commit to converting POA to firm within 48 hours of request. We minimise POA items to under 5% of our catalogue.

**Q103:** Can you show the rate build-up for a high-end imported tile?

**A103:** **Example — Italian rectified porcelain 1200×1200mm, premium grade, Gauteng:**

- Supply price (ex-warehouse, imported): R685.00/m²
- Import duty & clearance: R48.00/m²
- Delivery to site (>200m²): R18.50/m²
- Wastage @ 12% (large format): R90.42/m²
- Premium flexible adhesive (large-format): R52.00/m²
- Premium grout (2mm joint): R18.00/m²
- Stainless steel trim: R22.00/m²
- **Total material rate: R933.92/m²**
- Specialist large-format labour: R145.00/m²
- **Total S&I rate: R1,078.92/m²**

**Q104:** Do you include packaging removal and site waste disposal in the build-up?

**A104:** Packaging removal is **not included as standard** — it's priced as a separate line item if required (typically R3–5/m²). Site waste disposal is generally a preliminaries item but we can include it in our floor-system package on request.

**Q105:** How do you show the cost difference between local and imported equivalents?

**A105:** Where a local equivalent exists for an imported product, we show **both rates side-by-side** with a 'value gap' column. Example: Italian porcelain R933.92/m² vs. Turkish equivalent R612.40/m² — **difference R321.52/m² (34%)**. Technical equivalence note included.

**Q106:** Can you provide the rate build-up for a herringbone pattern installation?

**A106:** Herringbone pattern attracts: (1) **Higher wastage @ 12–15%** vs. 8% straight lay, (2) **Higher labour rate +25–35%** due to slower laying rate, (3) **Additional cutting charge** for border/mitred pieces. All shown as separate line items in the build-up for transparent cost impact assessment.

**Q107:** How do you handle rate build-ups for phased projects?

**A107:** Phased projects receive a **master rate schedule** valid for all phases, with phase-specific delivery charges and any anticipated rate escalation noted. Each phase can be called off against the master schedule without re-pricing.

**Q108:** Do you show recommended retail price (RRP) in the build-up?

**A108:** No. We show the **trade supply price** — the price your nominated contractor or subcontractor would pay. RRP is irrelevant for QS cost planning. We note 'retail equivalent' only where helpful for client value perception.

**Q109:** How is the waterproofing rate built up?

**A109:** Waterproofing rate build-up: (1) **Primer**: R12–18/m², (2) **Base coat**: R35–55/m², (3) **Reinforcement fleece**: R15–25/m² (where required), (4) **Top coat**: R35–55/m², (5) **Detail treatment** (pipes, corners): R25–40 per detail. System total: R95–150/m² depending on specification grade.

**Q110:** Can you provide labour-only coverage rates by tile format?

**A110:** **Labour coverage rates per m² (Gauteng, Q2 2025):**

- Mosaic (≤50×50mm): R140–165/m²
- Small format (100×100 to 200×200mm): R95–115/m²
- Standard format (300×300 to 600×600mm): R80–95/m²
- Large format (800×800 to 600×1200mm): R105–130/m²
- XXL format (1200×1200mm+): R145–180/m²
- Slip-resistant commercial (R11–R13): +15% premium

Rates include laying, grouting, and cleaning. Screeding and waterproofing excluded.

**Q111:** How do you calculate the cost per room from the m² rate?

**A111:** We provide a **room cost calculator**: Net floor area (m²) × m² rate × wastage factor + fixed items (door thresholds, perimeter sealant, accessories). Example: 15m² bedroom × R341.50/m² × 1.08 + R180 fixed = R5,712.30 all-in material.

**Q112:** Do you include shop drawings or layout plans in the rate?

**A112:** Shop drawings and **BIM layout optimisation** are available as a supplementary service, not included in standard m² rates. Priced per project from R3,500 for residential to R25,000+ for complex commercial. We can include in the BOQ as a separate preliminaries item if required.

**Q113:** How do you handle rate build-ups when multiple suppliers are involved?

**A113:** For multi-supplier packages (e.g., tiles from Tiletoria, screed from specialist, heating from third party), we provide a **consolidated rate sheet** with Tiletoria components fully decomposed and third-party components shown as 'nominated supply' with our best available budget rate.

**Q114:** Can you provide a rate build-up for a swimming pool surround?

**A114:** **Example — 20mm porcelain paver, 600×600, R11 slip rating, pedestal installation:**

- 20mm porcelain paver: R285.00/m²
- Adjustable pedestal system (50–120mm): R95.00/m²
- Laying compound/levelling: R35.00/m²
- Joint spacers and drainage mat: R18.00/m²
- Wastage @ 10%: R43.30/m²
- Delivery (FLT): R15.00/m²
- **Total material rate: R491.30/m²**
- Specialist pedestal labour: R110.00/m²
- **Total S&I: R601.30/m²**

**Q115:** Do you show the weight per m² in the rate build-up?


**Q116:** How do you handle rate build-ups for supply-only vs. supply-and-install?

**A116:** Every response includes **two summary totals**: 'Total Material Rate' (supply only, DDP site) and 'Total Supply & Install Rate'. Each component is tagged [MAT] or [LAB] so you can filter by cost type. This enables flexible BOQ structuring.

**Q117:** Can you provide a 'rate per point' for hotel room programmes?

**A117:** Yes. For hotel and residential estate programmes, we provide **rate per keys/room/unit** calculated from the m² rate × typical room floor and wall area + corridor allocation + lobby/prorata common areas. Example: Mid-scale hotel = R8,500–R12,000 per key (floor finishes only).

**Q118:** How do you show escalation risk in the rate build-up?

**A118:** We append an **escalation risk note** to every rate: 'Based on current exchange rate (EUR/ZAR 19.85). A 10% ZAR weakening would increase this import rate by approximately R42.50/m².' This allows QS risk registers to quantify currency exposure.

**Q119:** Do you provide rate build-ups for feature walls and decorative finishes?

**A119:** Yes. Feature wall rates include: decorative tile (often 3D, artisan, or handmade), specialist adhesive (often white for translucent tiles), matching grout, and any required substrate preparation. Labour rates are higher due to slower installation — typically +40–60% over standard wall tiling.

**Q120:** Can you build up a rate for a complete apartment floor finish package?

**A120:** **Example — 85m² two-bedroom apartment, mid-grade throughout:**

- Living/bedroom porcelain (600×600): 62m² × R256.50 = R15,903
- Bathroom floor (300×300 anti-slip): 10m² × R198.00 = R1,980
- Bathroom walls (300×600): 28m² × R225.00 = R6,300
- Kitchen backsplash (300×600 feature): 5m² × R285.00 = R1,425
- leading adhesive manufacturerscony floor (anti-slip 300×300): 8m² × R245.00 = R1,960
- Trims, grout, adhesive, silicone: R2,850
- **Total material package: R30,418 (R357.86/m² weighted average)**

**Q121:** How do you handle the rate build-up for products sold by weight rather than area?

**A121:** Products sold by weight (self-levelling compounds, patch repair mortars) are priced **per kg** with coverage rate noted (e.g., 'Self-levelling compound R18.50/kg, covers 1m² per 1.5kg at 1mm thickness'). We calculate the total project cost from your area and required thickness specifications.

**Q122:** Do you include testing costs in the rate build-up?

**A122:** Testing costs (moisture RH tests, adhesion pull-off tests, fall tests) are shown as a **separate line item** — not buried in the m² rate. Typical: RH test R850/location; pull-off test R1,200/location; fall test R650/wet room. This ensures transparency for testing schedules.

**Q123:** How do you present the rate build-up for a value-engineering comparison?

**A123:** Value-engineering comparisons are presented in a **three-column format**: Column A = Original specification rate (full decomposition); Column B = Proposed alternative rate (full decomposition); Column C = Variance (R/m² and % difference). A technical equivalence statement is appended below the rate table.

**Q124:** Can you provide the rate build-up in a format ready for my CCS database?

**A124:** Yes. Select 'CCS Export' when submitting your rate request. We deliver a **.csv file** with fields mapped to CCS standard cost groups: Sub-group, Item Code, Description, Unit, Rate, Labour/Material split, and Escalation category. Direct import-ready.

**Q125:** Do you include supplier discounts for early payment in the build-up?

**A125:** Early settlement discounts (e.g., 2.5% for 7-day payment) are noted as a **conditional deduction** below the standard rate. They are not applied automatically — the base rate remains the comparable figure for cost planning purposes.

**Q126:** How do you handle rate build-ups for discontinued or end-of-line products?

**A126:** Discontinued lines are flagged **[DISCONTINUED — EOL]** with the last confirmed rate and a **recommended replacement product** with equivalent or superior specification and its current rate. This prevents database entries for unobtainable products.

**Q127:** Can you provide a rate build-up summary for client presentation?

**A127:** Yes. Every rate response includes a **client summary page** (PDF) showing consolidated rates only — no decomposition. Suitable for attaching to cost plan reports or tender documents. The full decomposition is provided in the Excel workbook for your working files.

**Q128:** How do you handle rate build-ups for government or parastatal projects?

**A128:** Government projects receive rates in **NTC-approved format** with additional compliance fields: CIDB grading requirement, local content percentage, B-BBEE supplier level, and SANS standard reference. We meet National Treasury instruction note requirements for supply quotations.

**Q129:** Do you show the origin/manufacturing country in the rate build-up?

**A129:** Yes. Every product line shows **Country of Origin** (e.g., 'Manufactured in Spain / Italy / China / RSA'). This is critical for preference point calculations on public sector tenders and for client's 'buy local' requirements.

**Q130:** Can you provide a rate build-up for a complete shopping mall floor package?

**A130:** For large retail programmes, we provide a **category rate build-up**: Mall common areas (heavy traffic porcelain, R12 slip rating) at R425–550/m² S&I; Tenant shop shells (standard porcelain, R10) at R325–410/m² S&I; Food court (chemical-resistant, R12) at R485–620/m² S&I; restrooms (anti-slip mosaic) at R385–495/m² S&I; service corridors (industrial vinyl) at R195–285/m² S&I. All with full decomposition.

**Q131:** How do you update rate build-ups when component costs change?

**A131:** When a component changes (e.g., adhesive price increase), we issue a **component-specific rate update** to all affected QS clients rather than a generic 'prices have gone up' notification. The update shows old vs. new rate for each affected BOQ item.

**Q132:** Can you provide rate build-ups for acoustic ceiling tiles?

**A132:** Yes. Acoustic ceiling tile rates are **per m²** by tile size (600×600 or 1200×600mm), NRC rating (0.55–0.90), edge detail (board, tegular, microlook), and suspension system type (standard or heavy-duty). Suspension system priced separately per m².

**Q133:** Do you show the coverage rate per unit for adhesives and grouts?

**A133:** Yes. Every adhesive and grout line item includes: **Unit size** (e.g., 20kg bag), **Coverage per unit** (e.g., 4.2m² at 6mm trowel), **Units required per 100m²** (e.g., 23.8 bags), and **Rate per m²** (e.g., R28.40/m²). This enables quantity surveyors to cross-check contractor material orders.

**Q134:** How do you handle rate build-ups where the architect specifies by performance only?

**A134:** For performance-only specifications (e.g., 'PEI 4+, R11, ≤0.5% water absorption'), we provide **three compliant options** at different price points with full build-up for each. This gives the QS choice while maintaining compliance — ideal for contractor design-portion (CDP) items.

**Q135:** Can you provide rate build-ups for under-tile heating systems?

**A135:** Yes. Under-tile heating build-up: **Electric mat system**: heating mat R280–350/m² + insulation board R45–65/m² + thermostat R1,850/unit (1 per 15m²) + installation R55–75/m² = **R380–490/m² heated**. **Hydronic**: embedded pipe R180–220/m² + manifold R8,500/unit + boiler connection + screed overlay = **R285–385/m² heated**.

**Q136:** Can you provide a rate build-up for a podium deck system?

**A136:** **Podium deck system (per m²):** Screed with falls R85–125; Liquid waterproofing membrane R145–195; Protection board R15–25; Drainage composite R25–45; 20mm porcelain paver on pedestals R285–425; Jointing and sealant R15–25. **Total material: R570–840/m²**. Specialist labour: R125–185/m². **Total S&I: R695–1,025/m²**.

**Q137:** How do you handle rate build-ups where the substrate condition is unknown?

**A137:** For unknown substrate conditions, we provide **three scenarios**: Best case (sound concrete — standard adhesive), Most likely (minor prep required — primer + levelling compound), Worst case (extensive repair — screed overlay). Each scenario is fully costed, allowing the QS to apply appropriate contingency.

**Q138:** Do you show alternative fixing methods in the rate build-up?

**A138:** Yes. Where alternative fixing methods exist, we show both: **Adhesive fixing** (standard) and **Mechanical fixing** (for heavy stone, external cladding, or seismic zones). Mechanical fixing adds R45–125/m² for anchors and clips but may reduce adhesive cost. Method selection affects total system cost significantly.

**Q139:** How do you present rate build-ups for phased handover projects?

**A139:** Phased projects receive a **master rate schedule** with: Phase 1 rates (current quarter), Phase 2 rates (current + escalation), Phase 3 rates (current + 2× escalation), and so on. Each phase shows the escalation factor applied. This enables single budget line with phased cash flow projection.

**Q140:** Can you provide a rate build-up for a complete hotel room (floors + walls)?

**A140:** **Hotel room floor + wall system (28m² floor + 22m² wall):** Bedroom vinyl tile 20m² × R185 = R3,700; Bathroom floor anti-slip 5m² × R265 = R1,325; Bathroom walls 18m² × R245 = R4,410; Entry feature 3m² × R325 = R975; Adhesive/grout/trim R1,850; Sealant R285. **Total material: R12,545/room**. Labour: R4,850/room. **S&I: R17,395/room**.

**Q141:** Do you include cutting charge for complex shapes (curved walls, columns)?

**A141:** Complex cutting for curved walls, columns, and architectural features is priced **separately per item**: CNC cutting R85–145/linear metre; Waterjet cutting R125–225/linear metre; Hand-cut radius R45–85/linear metre. Standard straight cuts are absorbed into the installation labour rate. Quantified from shop drawings.

**Q142:** How do you handle rate build-ups when multiple currencies are involved?

**A142:** Multi-currency build-ups show: **Base rate in origin currency** (e.g., EUR 13.50/m²), **Exchange rate used** (EUR/ZAR 19.85), **Landed ZAR rate** (R268.00/m²), plus freight, duty, clearance, and delivery in ZAR. Full transparency of currency exposure for each import component.

**Q143:** How do you present rate build-ups for client presentations?

**A143:** Client presentation format includes: **executive summary** (single-page all-in rates), **visual product board** (product images with rates), **comparison tables** (3 grades side-by-side), **total project cost** (rates × areas), and **value engineering options** (savings available). No technical jargon — designed for non-technical client audiences.

**Q144:** Can you provide a rate build-up for a gym and fitness centre floor?

**A144:** **Gym floor system (per m²):** Rubber flooring (6–8mm, impact-resistant) R185–285; PU sports floor (sprung, basketball/ multipurpose) R285–485; Free weight area (heavy-duty rubber 15mm+) R245–385; Yoga studio ( timber or cork) R325–525; Change rooms (anti-slip vinyl) R195–285. **S&I rates shown**.

**Q145:** Do you show environmental cost (carbon) in the rate build-up?

**A145:** On request, we provide an **environmental addendum** to the rate build-up: embodied carbon per m² (kg CO₂e), carbon cost at current tax rate (R159/tCO₂e), and comparison between local and import carbon footprint. Green premium quantified separately from base rate.

**Q146:** How do you handle rate build-ups for works-to-existing buildings with asbestos?

**A146:** Asbestos-containing substrate rates include: asbestos survey and identification R45–85/m²; licensed asbestos removal R185–285/m²; air quality clearance testing R12–18/m²; new substrate preparation R65–95/m²; then standard floor system. Total asbestos remediation add: R285–465/m² before new floor installation.

**Q147:** Can you provide a rate build-up for an aircraft hangar floor?

**A147:** **Aircraft hangar system (per m²):** High-strength concrete seal/hardening R45–75; Dust-proof epoxy coating (3mm) R185–285; Chemical-resistant topcoat (hydraulic fluid, de-icing fluid) R65–95; Line marking and zoning R25–45; Anti-fatigue mat zones (maintenance pits) R125–185/m². **Total S&I: R345–585/m²**.

**Q148:** How do you handle rate build-ups for heritage-listed buildings?

**A148:** Heritage building rate build-ups include: **like-for-like material matching** (often imported specialist products), **artisan labour premium** (+50–100% over standard rates), **extended programme** (slow, careful work), **independent conservation officer inspection** R2,500–4,500/visit, and **documented methodology approval**. Premium of 40–80% over standard.

**Q149:** Can you provide a rate build-up for a veterinary clinic?

**A149:** **Veterinary clinic system (per m²):** Reception/waiting (LVT, anti-slip, warm) R185–285; Consulting rooms (seamless vinyl, disinfectant-resistant) R225–325; Surgery (epoxy, seamless, sterile) R385–525; Kennels (PU cement, urine-resistant, non-slip) R325–485; Prep areas (stainless steel overlay compatible) R285–425. **S&I rates by zone**.

**Q150:** How do you handle rate build-ups for Listed Building consent projects?

**A150:** Listed Building consent rates include: **heritage survey** R15–25/m²; **like-for-like material sourcing** (often imported, premium-priced); **specialist artisan labour** (+60–100% premium); **conservation officer inspection** R2,500–4,500/visit; **documented methodology** R5,000–8,500; and **extended programme** (slow, careful installation). Total premium: 50–100% above standard.

**Q151:** Can you provide a rate build-up for an equestrian centre (stables and arena)?

**A151:** **Equestrian centre (per m²):** Stable aisles (rubber matting, urine-resistant) R285–425; Tack room (sealed concrete or tile) R185–285; Arena viewing (standard porcelain) R285–385; Wash bay (anti-slip drainage-integrated) R325–485; Feed room (seamless, vermin-proof) R245–345. **S&I by zone**.

**Q152:** How do you handle rate build-ups for temporary event structures?

**A152:** Temporary event flooring: **short-term hire** (carpet tile, vinyl, portable floor systems) priced per event at R45–125/m² including installation and removal; **medium-term** (3–12 months) purchase at standard rates with resale buy-back option; **long-term** (12+ months) standard purchase. Programme and duration determine optimal pricing strategy.

**Q153:** Can you provide a rate build-up for a daycare and early childhood centre?

**A153:** **Daycare system (per m²):** Play areas (impact-absorbing rubber, 15mm+) R285–425; Sleeping areas (warm vinyl or carpet tile) R185–285; Feeding/kitchen (non-slip vinyl, seamless) R225–325; Bathrooms (anti-slip porcelain) R285–425; Entrance (heavy-duty, easy-clean) R325–485. **All non-toxic, low-VOC child-safe products**.

**Q154:** How do you present rate build-ups for NPV (Net Present Value) analysis?


**Q155:** Can you provide a rate build-up for a veterinary hospital surgery suite?

**A155:** **Vet surgery suite (per m²):** Seamless epoxy (2mm, solvent-free, sterile) R385–525; Coved skirting (100mm radius, seamless) R55–85/Lm; Integration with drainage (fall-to-drain 1:50) R45–65/m²; Anaesthetic gas-resistant sealant R15–25/m²; Validation and commissioning R25–45/m². **Total S&I: R525–745/m²** including sterile certification.


---


## Category 3: Rate Stability & Escalation

**Q156:** How long are your budget rates valid?

**A156:** Tiletoria budget rates carry a **90-day validity period** from date of issue. This extends across the typical feasibility-to-tender period. For projects with longer procurement cycles, we offer rate-hold extensions on request — contact your estimating manager before expiry.

**Q157:** What is your escalation policy during the rate validity period?

**A157:** During the 90-day validity, our maximum escalation is **CPI+3%** if component costs increase unexpectedly. We absorb fluctuations within CPI+3%. If a component increase exceeds this cap (rare — typically exchange-rate-driven on imports), we absorb the excess or offer an equivalent alternative at the held rate.

**Q158:** How often do your rates actually change within the 90-day validity?

**A158:** In 2024, **98.3% of our budget rates held within 5% at firm order** across 34 tracked projects. Only 1.7% of rates required adjustment beyond the 5% band, and in all cases we notified the QS proactively with options before expiry. Rate stability is a core KPI for our estimating team.

**Q159:** Do you proactively notify clients of rate changes?

**A159:** Yes. We operate a **proactive rate change notification system**. If any component of your held rate changes by more than 2%, you receive an email alert with: the affected item, old rate, new rate, effective date, and reason for change. You then have 14 days to place a firm order at the old rate.

**Q160:** How do I extend a rate beyond the 90-day validity?

**A160:** Email your estimating manager with your **original ticket number** and requested extension duration. Extensions of up to 90 additional days are typically granted at no change for standard items. Import items may require exchange rate reconfirmation — we'll advise within 24 hours.

**Q161:** Do you offer fixed-rate contracts for long-duration projects?

**A161:** Yes. For projects exceeding 6 months from rate issue to procurement, we offer **fixed-rate agreements** with a modest escalation premium (typically CPI+2% for 12 months). This locks your rates for the project duration, eliminating procurement risk.

**Q162:** How does exchange rate volatility affect your import product rates?

**A162:** Import product rates are quoted at the **exchange rate on date of issue** with a sensitivity indicator. Example: 'Rate based on EUR/ZAR 19.85. If EUR strengthens beyond R21.50, rate review triggered.' We can also quote in EUR/USD for you to manage your own forex hedge.

**Q163:** Can you provide a rate history chart for a specific product?

**A163:** Yes. Our QS portal provides **24-month rate history charts** for all standard products, showing quarterly rate movements. This data is downloadable as Excel for your own trend analysis and escalation forecasting.

**Q164:** What CPI figure do you use for escalation calculations?

**A164:** We reference the **Stats SA CPI headline figure** for the most recent quarter. As of Q2 2025, we are applying 4.5% CPI baseline. The CPI+3% cap means a maximum 7.5% rate movement during validity for locally manufactured products.

**Q165:** Do your labour coverage rates escalate at the same rate as material rates?

**A165:** No. Labour rates escalate on a **different cycle** — typically aligned with METAG wage agreement anniversary dates (1 August for most provinces). We flag upcoming labour rate changes 30 days in advance. Material and labour escalation are tracked separately in our database.

**Q166:** How do fuel price increases affect your delivery charges?

**A166:** Delivery charges include a **diesel adjustment factor (DAF)** reviewed monthly against the AA official fuel price. DAF movements are typically ±R0.50–2.00/m² — small relative to the tile rate. Large movements (>10% fuel increase) trigger a delivery charge notification.

**Q167:** Can I lock in a rate for a future phased project?

**A167:** Yes. Our **forward rate agreement (FRA)** locks rates for up to 12 months for phased projects. A nominal deposit (typically 5% of estimated material value) secures the rate lock, credited against first phase order. Ideal for multi-phase residential estates.

**Q168:** What happens if your supplier increases prices after you've quoted me?

**A168:** We honour our quoted rate for the full validity period **at our own margin risk**. This is our commitment to rate reliability. We maintain buffer stock and forward cover on key lines to insulate your rates from supplier price movements.

**Q169:** Do you provide monthly rate updates even if I haven't requested them?

**A169:** Yes. Registered QS portal users receive a **monthly rate update bulletin** by email covering: products with rate changes >2%, new product introductions, discontinued lines, and market commentary on pricing trends. You can opt out or customise frequency.

**Q170:** How stable are adhesive and grout rates compared to tile rates?

**A170:** Adhesive and grout rates are **more stable** than tile rates — typically changing once per year (January) with manufacturer price lists. Tile rates (especially imports) can move quarterly with exchange rate and shipping cost fluctuations. We track both separately.

**Q171:** Can you guarantee no price increase between budget and firm order?

**A171:** For standard local products, we guarantee **zero price increase** within 90 days. For imports, we guarantee within the CPI+3% cap. Beyond 90 days, our fixed-rate agreement option provides certainty for a modest premium. No supplier can guarantee absolute zero movement indefinitely — we manage the risk transparently.

**Q172:** How do you handle rate escalation on provisional sum items?

**A172:** Provisional sum rates are issued with a **±10% tolerance band** at budget stage. At firm order, the confirmed rate replaces the provisional rate. If the confirmed rate exceeds the upper tolerance, we notify immediately with alternatives to bring the rate back within budget.

**Q173:** Do you track your rate accuracy over time?

**A173:** Yes. We publish our **rate accuracy statistics** quarterly: in 2024, our budget-to-firm-order variance averaged 2.1% across 34 projects. This means a QS using our R400/m² budget rate would typically see a firm order rate of R408.40/m² — well within acceptable variance.

**Q174:** What factors most commonly cause your rates to change?

**A174:** The top three rate change drivers are: (1) **Exchange rate movement** on imports (45% of changes), (2) **Manufacturer price list updates** (35% of changes), and (3) **Fuel/transport cost changes** (20% of changes). We itemise the driver in every change notification.

**Q175:** Can I set up automatic rate alerts for specific products?

**A175:** Yes. In the QS portal, add products to your **Watch List**. When any watched product rate changes by >2%, you receive an instant email alert. You can maintain up to 50 products on your watch list.

**Q176:** How does your rate stability compare to competitors?

**A176:** Industry feedback from QS practices suggests competitor budget rates vary by **8–15% between budget and firm order** on average. Tiletoria's 2.1% average variance is significantly tighter. Our 90-day validity with CPI+3% cap is also longer and more protective than typical 30-day competitor validity.

**Q177:** Do you adjust rates downward when input costs decrease?

**A177:** Yes. Rate adjustments are **bidirectional**. When exchange rates strengthen or manufacturer costs decrease, we pass the saving to clients. In Q1 2025, 12% of our rate notifications were downward adjustments as ZAR strengthened against EUR.

**Q178:** How do you handle rate escalation for framework agreement clients?

**A178:** Framework agreement clients receive **quarterly rate schedule updates** with full transparency on what changed and why. Escalation is capped at CPI+3% per quarter with annual reconciliation. This provides predictability for multi-year development programmes.

**Q179:** Can I get a fixed exchange rate for import orders?

**A179:** For firm orders on import products, we can lock the exchange rate at **order placement** for up to 30 days of forward cover through our forex facility. This eliminates exchange rate risk between order and vessel arrival.

**Q180:** How do shipping cost changes affect your import rates?

**A180:** Shipping costs are a component of our landed rate calculation. Container shipping rates from Europe/Asia to Durban have **stabilised post-2022** at approximately $1,800–2,400 per 20ft container. We monitor monthly and notify if shipping surcharges apply.

**Q181:** Do ceramic tile rates escalate differently to porcelain tile rates?

**A181:** Yes. Ceramic tiles (single-fired, more porous) have **lower raw material energy input** and thus lower energy-price sensitivity. Porcelain tiles (double-fired, higher kiln temperature) are more sensitive to electricity and gas price movements. We track both categories separately in our cost data.

**Q182:** How do electricity tariff increases affect local tile manufacturing costs?

**A182:** Electricity represents approximately 18–22% of local tile manufacturing cost. Eskom tariff increases directly affect local tile pricing. We model this impact and include electricity sensitivity in our local product rate notes.

**Q183:** Can you provide an escalation forecast for my project programme?

**A183:** Yes. For projects with known procurement timelines, we provide a **projected rate path** showing anticipated quarterly movements based on current inflation, exchange rate forward curves, and manufacturer announcement schedules. This enables forward-loaded cost planning.

**Q184:** What is your policy on rate changes after tender submission but before award?

**A184:** Rates held for tender are **valid through the tender evaluation period** (typically 30 days from tender close). If tender award extends beyond 30 days, we offer a one-time 30-day extension at no change. Beyond 60 days total, we review and confirm.

**Q185:** Do you provide rate escalation indices specifically for floor finishes?

**A185:** Yes. Our quarterly **Construction Cost Update** includes a **Floor Finishes Index** tracking composite tile, adhesive, grout, and installation cost movement. The index is base-referenced to Q1 2020 = 100 and updated quarterly for Gauteng, KZN, and Western Cape.

**Q186:** How do you manage rate stability during currency crises?

**A186:** During periods of extreme ZAR volatility (>5% weekly movement), we implement **temporary rate holds** — all issued rates are frozen for 7 days to prevent whipsaw changes. We communicate this policy proactively to all framework clients.

**Q187:** Can I hedge my project's tile procurement against exchange rate risk?

**A187:** For projects with >R5m import material value, we can structure a **currency hedge** in conjunction with our forex provider. The hedge locks the ZAR/EUR or ZAR/USD rate for the procurement period. Terms and conditions apply — contact our commercial manager.

**Q188:** Do you provide different escalation rates for different provinces?

**A188:** Yes. Escalation rates vary by province due to: (1) different METAG labour agreement dates, (2) different transport cost bases, and (3) regional warehouse cost differences. Our quarterly reports show province-specific escalation indices.

**Q189:** How do I know if a rate I'm using from 6 months ago is still current?

**A189:** Enter the product code in our QS portal **Rate Checker** — it shows the current rate, your last-held rate, and the percentage change. Alternatively, email your estimating manager for a bulk rate reconfirmation against an old schedule.

**Q190:** What is your longest rate validity period?

**A190:** Our longest standard validity is **12 months** under a fixed-rate agreement with escalation premium. For exceptional cases (e.g., government projects with extended procurement cycles), we can negotiate bespoke terms — contact our commercial director.

**Q191:** How do you handle rate escalation for products sourced from multiple countries?

**A191:** Multi-origin products show **rate sensitivity by currency**: e.g., 'Product available from Spain (EUR exposure) or Turkey (USD exposure). Current pricing favours Turkish supply by R22/m².' This allows you to select the lower-risk currency option.

**Q192:** Do you publish an annual rate escalation forecast?


**Q193:** Can I get a credit note if rates decrease after I've placed a firm order?

**A193:** If rates decrease between firm order and delivery (rare, but happens on strengthening currency), we automatically apply the **lower rate** at invoicing. We do not hold you to a higher rate if our costs have reduced.

**Q194:** How do you communicate urgent rate changes?

**A194:** Urgent rate changes (triggered by sudden currency or manufacturer movements) are communicated by **email + SMS to your estimating manager contact** within 4 hours of the change occurring. The notification includes the reason, magnitude, and your options.

**Q195:** Do you track competitor rate changes?

**A195:** Yes. Our market intelligence function monitors **competitor rate movements** on comparable products monthly. This intelligence is shared in our quarterly Construction Cost Update and on request for specific product categories.

**Q196:** What is your rate accuracy by product category?


**Q197:** How do you handle rate escalation for government projects with extended tender periods?

**A197:** Government tenders with 60–90 day evaluation periods are handled under our **public sector rate protocol**: rates valid for 90 days from tender close, with extension to 120 days available on written request. We factor the extended risk into our public sector pricing — typically 1–2% premium.

**Q198:** Can you provide a rate escalation clause for insertion into our construction contracts?

**A198:** Yes. We provide a **model rate escalation clause** drafted by our contracts advisor, suitable for insertion into FIDIC, JBCC, or GCC contracts. The clause references our 90-day validity and CPI+3% cap mechanism. Available on request from your estimating manager.

**Q199:** Do you offer price protection for bulk orders placed in advance?

**A199:** Yes. **Advance purchase orders** (APO) for quantities >1,000m² can be placed at quoted rates with delivery scheduled up to 6 months later. A 20% deposit secures the rate and stock allocation. leading adhesive manufacturersance payable 30 days before each scheduled delivery.

**Q200:** How do you handle rate changes for products on back-order?

**A200:** If a product is on back-order at time of firm order, the **quoted rate is locked** regardless of how long the back-order takes to fulfil. We absorb any cost increase during the back-order period. If the wait exceeds 8 weeks, we offer a substitution option at the same rate.

**Q201:** Can you provide a sensitivity analysis for a product's rate?

**A201:** Yes. On request, we provide a **rate sensitivity matrix** showing the product rate at different exchange rate scenarios: -10% ZAR, current rate, +10% ZAR, +20% ZAR. This is particularly useful for import-heavy projects in feasibility stage.

**Q202:** What documentation do you provide to support a rate change?

**A202:** Every rate change notification includes: (1) Old rate vs. new rate, (2) Effective date, (3) Reason for change, (4) Supporting document reference (e.g., 'Manufacturer price list effective 1 March 2025', 'EUR/ZAR moved from 19.50 to 20.85'), (5) Options to mitigate the increase.

**Q203:** How does seasonality affect your rates?

**A203:** Seasonal effects are minimal on product pricing but affect **delivery costs** (wet season road conditions in KZN and Eastern Cape can increase transport by 5–8%). We note seasonal delivery premiums in our quarterly rate schedules for affected regions.

**Q204:** Do you provide rates inclusive of future expected increases?

**A204:** No. We quote **current rates** with escalation noted separately. Loading future increases into current rates would make our rates non-competitive at tender stage and distort your cost database. Transparency requires separation of current rate from future escalation.

**Q205:** How do you manage rate stability for your largest volume clients?

**A205:** Our top 20 volume clients receive **12-month fixed-rate schedules** reviewed quarterly but changed only by mutual agreement. This priority treatment recognises the volume predictability these clients provide.

**Q206:** Can you provide an insurance-backed rate guarantee?

**A206:** For projects exceeding R20m in floor finishes value, we can arrange an **insurance-backed rate guarantee** through our trade credit insurer. This provides contractual certainty for project financiers and funders. Terms available on application.

**Q207:** How do I update my cost plan when your rates change?

**A207:** When we issue a rate change notification, we include a **cost plan impact calculator**: enter your project's quantities and the calculator shows the total budget impact of the rate change. This saves you recalculating manually.

**Q208:** Do you track and publish your rate promise delivery statistics?

**A208:** Yes. Our estimating team KPIs are published monthly: **Average response time** (target <4 hours), **Rate accuracy** (target <3% variance), **Rate validity extensions granted** (target 100% within policy), and **Client satisfaction score** (target >4.5/5). All framework clients receive this dashboard.

**Q209:** Can I request a rate freeze during my tender period?

**A209:** Yes. Notify us of your **tender opening and award dates** and we'll place a rate freeze on all quoted items through to award date + 30 days. This is standard service for all tender invitations — no additional charge.

**Q210:** How does the MPC (Monetary Policy Committee) rate affect your pricing?

**A210:** SARB MPC rate changes affect our pricing indirectly: higher interest rates increase contractor finance costs (often passed through as higher labour rates), reduce construction activity (potential for discounting), and strengthen ZAR if foreign capital flows in (benefit for imports). We analyse MPC decisions in our quarterly report.

**Q211:** Do you track tile consumption trends (which sizes are growing/shrinking)?

**A211:** Yes. Consumption trend data: **600×600mm** remains the volume leader (45% of market); **800×800mm** growing fastest (+15% YoY); **1200×600mm** emerging in premium residential; **300×600mm** (wall tile) stable; **Mosaic** declining (-8% YoY, replaced by large-format). Trends inform specification cost forecasting.

**Q212:** How does the PPI (Producer Price Index) affect your rate forecasts?

**A212:** PPI for manufactured products correlates with our material rates at 0.78. PPI movements (Stats SA monthly) are a leading indicator with 2–3 month lag. When PPI exceeds 5% annual growth, manufacturer price increases typically follow. We include PPI trend in our forecast model.

**Q213:** Do you track construction insolvency rates as a market indicator?

**A213:** Yes. Construction insolvency rates are tracked via CIPC data and MBA reports. Rising insolvency (industry stress) typically leads to: competitive discounting from suppliers chasing reduced volume, contractor failure risk (nominated supplier payment concerns), and employer caution (delayed projects). We report quarterly insolvency trends.

**Q214:** How do interest rate changes affect construction cost planning?

**A214:** SARB interest rate increases affect cost planning through: higher contractor finance costs (+0.5–1.0% on project cost per 100bp increase), reduced construction activity (potential supplier discounting), and stronger ZAR (potential import cost reduction). Net effect on floor finishes: typically neutral to slightly negative (small cost increase).

**Q215:** Do you track tile import volumes through SARS customs data?

**A215:** Yes. SARS import data tracks monthly tile import volumes by HS code (6907), country of origin, and declared value. Data shows: China (42% of imports by volume, 28% by value), Italy (18% by volume, 35% by value), Spain (15% by volume, 22% by value), Turkey (12% by volume, 10% by value). Volume trends signal pricing pressure.

**Q216:** How does the depreciating ZAR affect local tile manufacturers' competitiveness?

**A216:** ZAR depreciation benefits **local manufacturers** relative to European imports: local products become 5–10% more competitive in ZAR terms with each 10% ZAR weakening. However, local manufacturers import some raw materials (glazes, pigments), partially offsetting the advantage. Net effect: local manufacturers gain 3–7% competitive advantage per 10% ZAR weakening vs. European imports.

**Q217:** How does the construction industry wage negotiation cycle affect your forecasts?

**A217:** METAG wage negotiations occur annually (August–September effective). We factor anticipated wage increases into our Q3 forecast. Historical pattern: 5–7% annual increase. If negotiations stall or exceed 8%, we issue a supplementary forecast alert. Wage increases affect labour-only and S&I rates from 1 October each year.

**Q218:** How does the National Development Plan (NDP) infrastructure pipeline affect flooring demand?

**A218:** NDP infrastructure projects (hospitals, schools, courts, transport hubs) drive demand for **institutional-grade flooring**. Increased NDP spending typically: increases demand for mid-range durable products, extends lead times for large orders, and firms pricing due to volume absorption. We monitor National Treasury budget allocations quarterly.

**Q219:** How does the informal construction sector affect formal market pricing?

**A219:** The informal sector (estimated 30–40% of residential flooring) operates below formal pricing: no VAT, no B-BBEE compliance, no warranty, cash payments. This creates a floor price reference that formal sector suppliers must exceed. Our formal rates reflect: compliance costs, warranty provision, and professional service — value-adds the informal sector cannot match.

**Q220:** How do you handle rate escalation when the contractor requests a price adjustment under FIDIC Clause 13.8?

**A220:** FIDIC Clause 13.8 (Adjustment for Changes in Cost) requires proof of cost increase beyond normal inflation. We provide: documented rate change with effective date, supporting manufacturer price increase letter or exchange rate data, percentage increase calculation, and the pre- and post-change rates. Documentation suitable for FIDIC submittal to the Engineer.


---


## Category 4: Value Engineering Support

**Q221:** How does Tiletoria support value engineering exercises?

**A221:** Tiletoria provides **full value engineering support**: we identify equivalent or superior products at lower cost, prepare side-by-side rate comparisons, confirm technical compliance against the original specification, and draft substitution motivation letters for architect approval. A documented case: **R2.3 million saved on a 180-unit Fourways project** substituting Turkish rectified for Italian porcelain with full compliance confirmation.

**Q222:** What is the typical process for a value engineering request?

**A222:** **Step 1:** Send us the original specification and your target saving (or 'maximum saving sought').
**Step 2:** We identify equivalent alternatives within 48 hours with unit rate comparison.
**Step 3:** You review and select preferred alternatives.
**Step 4:** We issue a Technical Compliance Letter confirming the alternative meets or exceeds all specification criteria.
**Step 5:** The substitution is presented to the architect for approval with our compliance documentation.

**Q223:** How much can value engineering typically save on a tile package?

**A223:** Typical savings range from **15% to 40%** on the material portion depending on the original specification grade. Substituting a premium European porcelain with an equivalent-quality mid-range import typically saves 20–30%. Our largest single-project saving was **R2.3 million on an 180-unit residential project** — a 28% reduction in the floor finishes package.

**Q224:** Do you approach value engineering from a 'cheaper at all costs' perspective?

**A224:** Absolutely not. Our value engineering principle is **'same performance, lower cost'** — never 'lower cost, lower performance'. Every proposed substitution is supported by technical data sheets confirming the alternative meets or exceeds the original specification's key performance indicators.

**Q225:** What technical data do you provide to support a substitution?

**A225:** Every value-engineering proposal includes: (1) **PEI (abrasion) rating** comparison, (2) **Water absorption** comparison, (3) **Slip rating (R-value)** comparison per SANS 10107, (4) **Breaking strength** (N) comparison, (5) **Chemical resistance** classification, (6) **Stain resistance** rating, and (7) **Warranty equivalence**. This data enables the architect to assess compliance objectively.

**Q226:** Can you defend a substitution if the architect challenges it?

**A226:** Yes. Our **Technical Compliance Letters** are drafted to address common architect objections preemptively. If challenged post-submission, we provide additional technical justification, independent test report references, and where beneficial, arrange a video conference between our technical manager and the architect's team. Our success rate on substitution approvals is **94%**.

**Q227:** Do you charge for value engineering services?

**A227:** Value engineering is **provided free of charge** to registered QS practices and contractors. We view it as a service that builds long-term relationships. The only cost is your time reviewing our proposals. We do not add margin to value-engineered products — you get the same trade rate as any other product.

**Q228:** How do you identify equivalent products across different countries of origin?

**A228:** We maintain an **equivalence matrix** cross-referencing products by technical specification: PEI rating, water absorption, dimensional calibre, surface finish, and slip resistance. Example: Italian 600×600 PEI 4 rectified (R685/m²) = Turkish 600×600 PEI 4 rectified (R445/m²) = Chinese 600×600 PEI 4 rectified (R295/m²) — all with comparable technical certificates.

**Q229:** Can value engineering apply to the full system or just the tile?

**A229:** Full-system value engineering delivers the largest savings. Beyond tile substitution, we analyse: **adhesive grade** (do you need flexible for this substrate?), **grout type** (standard vs. epoxy for this wet area?), **waterproofing system** (cementitious vs. membrane — cost difference R25–45/m²), and **trim profile** (aluminium vs. stainless steel). Cumulative savings often exceed tile-only substitution.

**Q230:** What is the fastest you've completed a value engineering exercise?

**A230:** Our fastest turnaround was **4 hours from brief to proposal** — a Sandton consulting QS needed a R400/m² reduction on a 2,500m² office floor by 17:00 for a client presentation. We identified a Turkish rectified alternative saving R142/m² (R355,000 total), issued the compliance letter, and the architect approved next day.

**Q231:** Can you value-engineer after construction has started?

**A231:** Post-contract value engineering is more complex but achievable for **phased projects** where later phases haven't been procured. We've helped developers apply lessons from Phase 1 value engineering to Phases 2 and 3, achieving cumulative savings of R1.8 million+ on multi-phase estates.

**Q232:** Do you have a library of past value engineering successes?

**A232:** Yes. Our **Value Engineering Case Study Library** (available to registered QS portal users) contains 40+ documented projects showing: original specification, alternative proposed, saving achieved, architect response, and outcome. Average documented saving: **22% of original floor finishes budget**.

**Q233:** How do you handle value engineering for public sector projects with preference points?

**A233:** Public sector substitutions must maintain or improve **local content points** and **B-BBEE scoring**. We verify the alternative supplier's B-BBEE level, local manufacturing status, and SANS certification before proposing. We never suggest a substitution that would reduce tender evaluation points.


**Q235:** How do you quantify the total project impact of a substitution?

**A235:** Our value engineering proposals include a **Total Project Impact** line: not just the unit rate saving (R/m²), but the saving multiplied by total quantity, plus ancillary cost changes (different adhesive, different trim), plus any labour rate impact, minus any added cost (e.g., longer lead time). Net saving = clear total.

**Q236:** What if the architect rejects your substitution proposal?

**A236:** If rejected, we **analyse the architect's objection** and provide a counter-proposal addressing the specific concern. Example: architect rejected Turkish tile citing 'colour consistency concern' — we provided batch-sample photos from 5 different production runs showing ΔE<0.5 colour deviation, and the substitution was approved. Our resubmission success rate is 67%.

**Q237:** Can you provide a value engineering feasibility assessment before full analysis?

**A237:** Yes. Send us the specification and we provide a **VE feasibility snapshot** within 4 hours: estimated saving range (e.g., '15–25% achievable'), key substitution candidates, and any risks. This helps you decide whether to proceed with full VE analysis before investing time.

**Q238:** Do you offer CPD-accredited value engineering workshops?

**A238:** Yes. Our **'Best Practice in Floor Finishes Cost Planning'** workshop (ASAQS CPD-accredited, 2 CPD points) includes a dedicated value engineering module with real case studies, substitution protocol, and architect-engagement strategies. Held quarterly in Johannesburg, Durban, and Cape Town.

**Q239:** Can value engineering maintain warranty equivalence?

**A239:** Yes. All our value-engineered alternatives carry **equivalent or longer warranties** than the original specification. We document warranty comparison in the compliance letter. Example: Italian tile (10-year warranty) vs. Turkish equivalent (10-year warranty) — no warranty downgrade.

**Q240:** How do you handle slip rating equivalence in substitutions?

**A240:** Slip rating per SANS 10107 is **non-negotiable** — we never propose a substitution with a lower R-value than specified. Where the original specification exceeds minimum requirements, we match or exceed the R-value. The compliance letter includes the alternative's test report reference number.

**Q241:** Can you value-engineer to reduce installation complexity and cost?

**A241:** Yes. A common strategy is substituting **rectified tiles** (minimal grout joints, faster installation) for pressed-edge tiles with wider joints. Despite a higher material cost, the labour saving and reduced grout quantity often produces net project saving. We model this full-system impact.

**Q242:** Do you provide sample boards for value-engineered alternatives?

**A242:** Yes. Upon request, we prepare **A4 sample boards** of the proposed alternative posted to your office and the architect's office within 48 hours. Larger sample boards (600×600mm) are available for major projects. This accelerates architect visual approval.

**Q243:** How do you ensure the value-engineered product is actually available?

**A243:** Before proposing any substitution, we **confirm stock availability** or production lead time. We never propose a product on back-order or with uncertain supply. Stock confirmation is included in the compliance letter.

**Q244:** Can you handle value engineering for the entire building envelope?

**A244:** While our expertise is floor and wall finishes, we partner with **Spec Lab (Spec Lab)** for integrated value engineering across the full building envelope — facade, roofing, waterproofing, and internal finishes. We can coordinate a single VE exercise covering all finish categories.

**Q245:** What role does the QS play in your value engineering process?

**A245:** The QS is the **coordinator and decision-maker**: you identify the saving target, review our proposals, decide which alternatives to pursue, present to the architect/client, and manage the substitution through approval. We are the technical resource — you retain control of the process.

**Q246:** Can you value-engineer for BREEAM or Green Star projects?

**A246:** Yes. Green building substitutions must maintain or improve **ECO points**. We verify the alternative's environmental credentials: recycled content percentage, low-VOC certification, energy-efficient manufacturing, and responsible sourcing. We provide the ECO data sheets needed for Green Star documentation.

**Q247:** How do you document the 'before and after' for cost reports?

**A247:** Every value engineering exercise concludes with a **VE Report** suitable for insertion into your cost plan revision or cost report: original BOQ value, revised BOQ value, total saving (R and %), substitution schedule with approval status, and risk assessment. Saves you hours of report writing.

**Q248:** Can you provide value engineering for small projects (under R500k)?

**A248:** Yes. There is no minimum project size for value engineering support. Even a R150,000 bathroom renovation package can benefit — we've saved clients R35,000+ on small projects through specification optimisation.

**Q249:** Do you have experience value engineering for specific developer clients?

**A249:** Yes. We have documented VE experience with major SA developers including **leading adhesive manufacturerswin, Calgro M3, Growthpoint, and Attacq**. Each has preferred VE protocols — we know their approval processes, preferred alternative origins, and risk tolerance. This institutional knowledge accelerates approvals.

**Q250:** How does value engineering affect contractor pricing behaviour?

**A250:** Contractors are more likely to **sharpen their pricing** when the QS has already value-engineered the specification because: (1) lower material cost = lower risk = lower risk premium, (2) simpler specification = lower installation complexity = lower labour risk, and (3) competition is more intense at lower price points.

**Q251:** Can you value-engineer adhesive and grout specifications?

**A251:** Yes. Adhesive VE is common: flexible adhesive (R32/m²) vs. standard-set (R22/m²) — if the substrate is stable concrete, flexible may be unnecessary. Grout VE: cementitious (R8.50/m²) vs. epoxy (R28/m²) — epoxy is only required in food processing and chemical exposure areas. We justify every downgrade with substrate/usage assessment.

**Q252:** Do you provide value engineering for waterproofing systems?

**A252:** Yes. Waterproofing VE saves R25–55/m² typically: **Cementitious 2-coat** (R85–110/m²) is often sufficient for standard bathrooms where **flexible membrane** (R130–165/m²) was overspecified. We confirm the fall, drainage, and exposure conditions support the alternative. Compliance with SANS 10400-L (waterproofing) is verified.

**Q253:** Can you value-engineer to reduce lead times as well as cost?

**A253:** Yes. A **shorter lead time** reduces project preliminaries (finance, site establishment, programming risk). Local products typically deliver in 2–4 weeks vs. 8–14 weeks for European imports. We quantify the preliminary cost saving from reduced lead time and add it to the material saving.

**Q254:** How do you handle value engineering when the architect specifies by brand name?

**A254:** Where the architect specifies 'Brand X or similar approved', we propose a **'similar approved'** alternative with full technical justification. Where the spec is 'Brand X only' (nominated supplier), we engage directly with the architect's office to present the alternative. Our 94% approval rate reflects the quality of our technical submissions.

**Q255:** Can you provide a value engineering saving estimate before detailed analysis?

**A255:** Yes. Our **'Quick VE Scan'** service provides an estimated saving range within 4 hours based on your specification grade and total floor area. We categorise the estimate: 'High probability 20–30% saving', 'Medium probability 10–20%', or 'Low probability <10% — limited VE opportunity'. This guides your resource allocation.

**Q256:** Do you track value engineering savings achieved across all your projects?

**A256:** Yes. Our cumulative value engineering savings for QS clients in 2024 exceeded **R18 million** across 56 projects. Average saving: **23% of original floor finishes budget**. We publish an annual VE impact report for framework clients.

**Q257:** How does value engineering interact with the contractor's own VE proposals?

**A257:** When the contractor proposes their own VE substitution, having our **pre-approved alternative already in the architect's hands** strengthens your position in tender evaluation. You can direct the contractor to our approved alternative rather than accepting an unknown substitution with unverified compliance.

**Q258:** Can you provide value engineering for heritage or listed building projects?

**A258:** Heritage projects require **like-for-like material matching**. We source specialised reproduction products (encaustic tiles, Victorian floor patterns, handmade subway tiles) and provide cost comparisons against custom-manufactured alternatives. Savings are typically modest (5–15%) but still material on high-value restoration budgets.

**Q259:** Do you offer a value engineering guarantee?

**A259:** We guarantee that every proposed substitution is **technically compliant** as documented. If an approved substitution subsequently fails to meet the claimed specification, we accept return and replacement at our cost. This guarantee has never been invoked — testament to our technical rigour.

**Q260:** How can I introduce value engineering into my cost planning process?

**A260:** We recommend a **two-stage approach**: Stage 1 — include our entry-grade rate as the budget rate from inception, with a premium-rate contingency for architect upgrade. Stage 2 — at design development, present the VE options to the architect with our compliance data. This normalises VE as standard practice, not a cost-cutting emergency.

**Q261:** Can you value-engineer floor finishes in BIM models?

**A261:** Yes. Through our **Spec Lab BIM integration**, we can overlay alternative product data onto your BIM model's floor finish schedule, showing cost impact by room, level, and building. The VE analysis becomes a visual, model-integrated exercise rather than a spreadsheet abstraction.

**Q262:** How do you handle value engineering for slip-resistant commercial floors?

**A262:** Commercial slip ratings (R10–R13) are **performance-specified by test result**, not by brand. Any product with the required R-value is technically substitutable. We maintain a database of R-rated products by cost tier, enabling rapid VE for retail, hospitality, and healthcare projects.

**Q263:** Can you value-engineer to improve durability while reducing cost?

**A263:** Counter-intuitively, yes. Some 'premium' products trade on brand rather than performance. A **higher-PEI, lower-absorption mid-range product** can outperform a premium fashion tile at half the cost. We identify these 'performance over prestige' opportunities through our technical database analysis.

**Q264:** What documentation does the architect need to approve a VE substitution?

**A264:** The architect typically requires: (1) Product data sheet for proposed alternative, (2) Side-by-side comparison with original spec, (3) Sample or high-resolution image, (4) Confirmation of SANS compliance, (5) Warranty comparison, and (6) Statement of no aesthetic compromise. Tiletoria prepares all six items.

**Q265:** How do you handle value engineering for custom or designer tiles?

**A265:** Custom/decorative tiles (designer collaborations, artisan production) have limited substitution options. Where possible, we suggest **retaining the feature tile** for visible areas while substituting the field tile (80% of area) with a cost-effective alternative. This preserves design intent while maximising saving.

**Q266:** Can you provide VE support during the tender period?

**A266:** Yes. During tender, contractors may propose substitutions not in our original VE schedule. We **rapid-assess contractor-proposed alternatives** within 4 hours: compliant or non-compliant, rate comparison with our approved alternative, and recommendation. This prevents non-compliant substitutions entering the tender evaluation.

**Q267:** Do you track architect preferences for future VE exercises?

**A267:** Yes. We maintain an **architect preference register**: which architects are open to VE, which resist substitutions from specific origins, which require physical samples vs. data sheet approval. This intelligence streamlines future submissions to the same architect.

**Q268:** How does value engineering affect my professional indemnity as a QS?

**A268:** Using Tiletoria's VE service **reduces your PI risk** because: (1) We provide the technical compliance verification, (2) Our compliance letters are drafted by technically qualified staff, (3) Our 94% approval rate demonstrates due diligence, and (4) Our substitution guarantee provides a backstop. Document our compliance letter in your project file.

**Q269:** Can you value-engineer for NHBRC-warranty projects?

**A269:** Yes. All NHBRC-warranty projects must use **NHBRC-compliant products and systems**. We verify every VE alternative against NHBRC Technical Requirements and document compliance. Our NHBRC compliance rate is 100% — no substitution we have proposed has triggered a warranty challenge.

**Q270:** Do you provide value engineering for industrial flooring (food factory, warehouse)?

**A270:** Industrial VE requires **HACCP compliance, chemical resistance, and heavy-load rating**. We specialise in these sectors: polyurethane cement screed vs. epoxy, slip-resistant profile by zone, and coving integration. Savings of 15–25% are typical on industrial flooring packages exceeding R2 million.

**Q271:** Can you value-engineer to reduce maintenance costs over the building life?


**Q272:** How do you handle VE when the interior designer is involved?

**A272:** Interior designer VE requires **aesthetic approval** in addition to technical compliance. We provide: high-resolution product images, physical sample boards, colour palette matching, and mood board integration. Designer sign-off is sought before architect submission. Our 94% approval rate includes designer-endorsed projects.

**Q273:** Can you value-engineer flooring for logistics and distribution centres?

**A273:** Logistics flooring VE focuses on: **wear zone differentiation** (heavy traffic aisles vs. light traffic staging); **joint design** (narrow joints where possible = lower joint filler cost); **Repairability** (modular systems for high-damage zones). Savings of 10–18% achievable on large (>5,000m²) distribution floors.

**Q274:** Can you value-engineer a project that is 40% over budget?

**A274:** Yes. A 40% overrun requires **aggressive value engineering**: substituting premium imports with mid-range equivalents (20–30% saving), reducing format size to standard 600×600 (10–15% saving), simplifying layouts to straight lay (5–8% saving), and optimising waterproofing scope (5–10% saving). Cumulative savings of 35–45% are achievable with architect cooperation.

**Q275:** How do you handle VE for projects with fixed-price contracts already signed?

**A275:** Fixed-price post-contract VE requires **employer and contractor agreement** to a variation. We provide: technical compliance for the alternative, rate comparison showing saving, and draft variation documentation. The saving is typically shared between employer and contractor (50/50 or 60/40). Our VE report supports the negotiation.

**Q276:** Can you provide VE for floor finishes in a LEED Platinum project?

**A276:** LEED Platinum VE is challenging — every substitution must maintain or improve credit contributions. We verify: MRc1 (Building Life-Cycle Impact Reduction), MRc2 (Building Product Disclosure), MRc3 (Environmental Product Declarations), MRc4 (Sourcing of Raw Materials), and EQc2 (Low-Emitting Materials). Substitution only proceeds if credits are maintained or improved.

**Q277:** Can you value-engineer flooring for an art gallery or museum?


**Q278:** How do you handle VE when the structural engineer imposes load limits?


**Q279:** Can you provide VE for a speculative office development targeting 4-star Green Star?

**A279:** Green Star speculative office VE: mid-range porcelain (not premium imported) for 80% of area + premium feature tile for lobby only = 25% material saving; standard-set adhesive (not flexible) for dry areas = 15% adhesive saving; cementitious grout (not epoxy) for general areas = 12% grout saving. Green Star credits maintained through ECO-label selection.

**Q280:** Can you value-engineer for a developer targeting first-time buyers?

**A280:** First-time buyer development VE: entry-grade porcelain (R185–245/m² vs. R385–525/m² premium) for living areas; standard ceramic for bathrooms (R125–185/m²); basic vinyl for bedrooms (R85–125/m²); minimal feature areas. Total floor finish cost: R850–1,200/unit vs. R1,500–2,200/unit premium. NHBRC compliance maintained.

**Q281:** How do you handle VE when the budget is confidential?

**A281:** Confidential budget VE: you provide the **saving target percentage** (e.g., '20% reduction sought') without revealing the absolute budget. We identify alternatives achieving the target. Our proposal shows percentage saving and absolute saving — you decide whether to reveal the absolute figures to other stakeholders.

**Q282:** Can you value-engineer a flooring package for a build-to-rent development?

**A282:** Build-to-rent VE priorities: durability over premium aesthetics (tenant turnover), easy maintenance (reduce operating costs), replaceability (individual unit refresh without building-wide disruption), and acoustic performance (reduce tenant complaints). LVT and high-quality vinyl sheet often outperform tile for build-to-rent. Life-cycle cost analysis drives specification.

**Q283:** How do you handle VE for government housing projects with strict cost caps?

**A283:** Government housing VE works within **NHBRC Technical Requirements** and **SANS 10400** minimums: entry-grade ceramic (R85–125/m² S&I), standard adhesive, cementitious grout, no unnecessary upgrades. Focus on compliance at lowest cost while meeting 5-year warranty. Documented value engineering report supports subsidy application compliance.

**Q284:** Can you value-engineer for a luxury boutique hotel where brand image is critical?

**A284:** Luxury boutique hotel VE balances cost with brand: **premium tile for visible areas** (lobby, restaurant) + **mid-range equivalent for back-of-house** (kitchen, service corridors) + **standard for staff areas**. Visible areas: 60% of guest-facing budget; back-of-house: 30% saving through substitution. Brand integrity preserved where it matters.

**Q285:** How do you handle VE when the developer has already marketed units with premium finishes?

**A285:** Marketing commitment VE is sensitive — downgrading from marketed specs risks legal challenge. We recommend: **maintaining marketed spec for visible areas**, **VE only in non-visible areas** (store rooms, plant rooms, parking), or **offering upgrade options** to buyers at marginal cost. Our compliance letter confirms marketed spec is maintained in promised areas.

**Q286:** Can you value-engineer for a defence force base with security restrictions?

**A286:** Defence base VE respects security protocols: all personnel security-cleared, no photography, coded project references, and secure communication channels. VE focuses on: durability (long replacement cycles reduce security disruption), local sourcing (supply chain security), and low-maintenance finishes (reduce contractor access frequency). Military-grade specifications where required.

**Q287:** How do you handle VE when the funder (bank) has approved a fixed budget?

**A287:** Funder-fixed budget VE requires: **funder notification** of any specification changes, **demonstrated saving** with no quality reduction, **compliance confirmation** that alternatives meet all regulatory requirements, and **updated cost plan** for funder re-submission. We provide the full documentation package for funder approval.

**Q288:** Can you value-engineer to meet a 'cost per bed' target for a hospital project?

**A288:** Hospital 'cost per bed' VE: target R85,000–120,000 per bed for floor finishes (typical mid-size hospital). We allocate budget across zones: wards (vinyl sheet, R195–285/m²), theatres (epoxy, R385–525/m²), ICU (seamless vinyl, R285–425/m²), circulation (rubber, R225–325/m²). Total divided by bed count = cost per bed. Adjust specification to meet target.


---


## Category 5: BOQ & Cost Planning Tools

**Q289:** What BOQ templates do you provide?

**A289:** Tiletoria provides **downloadable BOQ templates** in Excel format pre-populated with: standard floor and wall finish categories, typical specification descriptions, unit rates (m², linear metre, each), coverage rates for adhesives and grouts, and wastage factors. Templates are available for residential, commercial, retail, and hospitality project types.

**Q290:** Are your BOQ templates compatible with SMM7 or AESMM?

**A290:** Yes. Our templates follow **AESMM (African Edition SMM)** classification for floor and wall finishes, with element codes compatible with SMM7. Categories align to: E30 (Resilient and textile floor finishes), E40 (Rigid floor finishes), and F30 (Wall and ceiling finishes).

**Q291:** Can I download pre-filled rate schedules for my region?
