**A291:** Yes. The QS portal offers **quarterly pre-filled rate schedules** for Gauteng, KZN, and Western Cape. Each schedule contains 200+ standard items with current rates, ready for import into your cost plan. Updated within 5 days of each quarter start.

**Q292:** Do your BOQ templates include provisional sum items?

**A292:** Yes. Provisional sum items are flagged **[PS]** with a note rate and ±10% tolerance band. Common PS items include: feature/decorative tiling, stone cladding, and specialist resin floors where the final selection depends on architect sample approval.

**Q293:** What estimating software do your rate schedules integrate with?

**A293:** Our rate schedules integrate directly with: **WinQS** (.csv import), **CCS** (.csv and direct API), **Berg Cost Data** (template-mapped Excel), **Estimator** (custom import), and **Excel** (universal). We can create custom mappings for other software on request within 48 hours.

**Q294:** Do you provide elemental cost analyses for floor finishes?

**A294:** Yes. Our **Elemental Cost Analysis** breaks floor finishes into: Substructure preparation, Waterproofing, Adhesive bed, Floor covering, Grouting, Trims/ancillaries, and Testing. Each element is costed separately for detailed cost planning and enables substitution at element level.

**Q295:** Can you provide a floor finishes cost plan for a building I haven't designed yet?

**A295:** Yes. Using our **benchmark database**, we provide cost plans based on building type and grade: Budget hotel R1,200–1,500/m² S&I; Mid-market office R850–1,200/m² S&I; Premium residential R1,500–2,500/m² S&I; Retail mall common R650–850/m² S&I. These enable feasibility-stage cost plans without detailed designs.

**Q296:** Do your BOQ templates separate supply and install?

**A296:** Yes. Every template includes separate columns for: **Material rate** (DDP site), **Labour rate** (per METAG region), **Total S&I rate**, and **Preliminaries %**. You can use material-only or S&I columns depending on your procurement strategy and contract form.

**Q297:** Can you populate a BOQ from my drawing mark-ups?

**A297:** Yes. Email us your **floor finish drawing mark-ups** (PDF with coloured zones or a marked-up RCP) and we'll produce a BOQ with quantities calculated, rates applied, and total package value. Turnaround: 24–48 hours depending on drawing complexity.

**Q298:** Do you provide quantity take-off assistance?

**A298:** Yes. Our estimating team provides **quantity take-off support**: send us your floor plans and we'll calculate nett areas, apply wastage factors, and produce a material schedule. This is particularly valuable for complex layouts (herringbone, diagonal, mixed formats).

**Q299:** Are your coverage calculators available online?

**A299:** Yes. Our **Coverage Calculator Suite** is available on the QS portal: Adhesive Coverage Calculator (by trowel size and tile format), Grout Coverage Calculator (by joint width and tile size), Waterproofing Coverage Calculator (by coats and thickness), and a Combined System Calculator producing a consolidated material schedule.

**Q300:** Can I get a cost plan for a 100-unit residential development?

**A300:** Yes. Send us the **unit type floor plans** and specification grade, and we'll produce a **per-unit cost plan** plus **total development cost** with phasing breakdown. Typical turnaround: 48 hours for a standard 2–3 unit type development. We also provide a rate per key/square metre for your feasibility model.

**Q301:** Do you provide rates for SANS 10400-XA (energy efficiency) compliance?

**A301:** Yes. SANS 10400-XA requires specific insulation values for floors in certain climate zones. We provide rates for insulated screed systems, under-tile thermal boards, and compatible floor finishes that meet the deemed-to-satisfy requirements. Compliance documentation included.

**Q302:** Can your BOQ template handle mixed-format installations?

**A302:** Yes. Our **mixed-format BOQ template** accommodates installations using multiple tile sizes in the same room (e.g., 600×600 field + 300×600 border + 300×300 feature). Each format is a separate line item with its own quantity, rate, and wastage factor.

**Q303:** Do you provide cost data for life-cycle costing?


**Q304:** Can I import your rates directly into WinQS?

**A304:** Yes. Select **'WinQS Export'** format when downloading rate schedules. The .csv file maps to WinQS cost groups with fields: Cost Group, Sub-Group, Item Code, Description, Unit, Material Rate, Labour Rate, and Total Rate. Direct import-ready with no re-keying.

**Q305:** Do you provide cost indices for historical cost analysis?

**A305:** Yes. Our **Construction Cost Update** includes historical indices back to Q1 2020 (base = 100) for: Local ceramic tiles, Local porcelain tiles, European imports, Asian imports, Adhesives/grouts, Installation labour, and Composite floor finishes index. Downloadable as Excel.

**Q306:** Can your BOQ templates accommodate NEC3/NEC4 contract structures?

**A306:** Yes. We provide NEC-compatible BOQ templates with: **Activity Schedule** format (lump sum items) and **Bill of Quantities** format (remeasurable). Both include Short Schedule of Cost Components breakdown for compensation event valuation.

**Q307:** Do you provide rates for prefabricated bathroom pods?

**A307:** Yes. Prefabricated bathroom pod floor finish rates are provided **per pod** by pod size (typical 2.4m×1.8m to 3.0m×2.4m), specification grade, and supplier (Pods SA, Offsite Solutions, etc.). Connection details and sealing interfaces are included.

**Q308:** Can I get a BOQ template for a Green Star project?

**A308:** Yes. Our Green Star BOQ template includes: **ECO-labelled product** identification, recycled content percentage, local sourcing radius, VOC emission data, and Green Star credit mapping. It aligns with GBCSA submission requirements for Materials category credits.

**Q309:** Do you provide cost per square metre by building type?

**A309:** Yes. Our **Building Type Cost Benchmarks** (updated quarterly) provide floor finishes cost/m² S&I for: Budget residential (R650–850), Mid-market residential (R950–1,350), Luxury residential (R1,500–3,000), Standard office (R850–1,200), Premium office (R1,300–2,200), Retail (R650–1,100), Industrial (R450–750), Hotel 3-star (R850–1,200), Hotel 5-star (R1,500–2,800), Healthcare (R950–1,500).

**Q310:** Can you provide a cost plan from a BIM model?

**A310:** Yes. Through **Spec Lab**, we import your BIM model (Revit, IFC, ArchiCAD), extract floor finish schedules, apply rates from our database, and produce a cost-loaded model. Changes to the model automatically update the cost plan. This is our **BIM-QS integration service**.

**Q311:** Do your BOQ templates include preliminaries and attendances?

**A311:** Yes. Our comprehensive BOQ template includes a **Preliminaries section** with typical attendances: protection of other trades, provision of power/water, scaffolding access, waste removal, and programming coordination. Percentages are shown as editable defaults — adjust to your project.

**Q312:** Can you provide rates for testing and compliance verification?

**A312:** Yes. Testing rates are itemised in our BOQ template: **RH moisture test** R850/location, **Pull-off adhesion test** R1,200/location, **Slip resistance test** R2,500/zone, **Fall test** R650/wet room. These are typically provisional sums — actual requirements confirmed post-inspection.

**Q313:** Do you provide a cost plan check service?

**A313:** Yes. Send us your **draft cost plan or BOQ** and we'll review it against our current rates, flag items priced significantly above or below our database, identify missing ancillaries (adhesive, grout, trims), and suggest optimisation opportunities. Turnaround: 24 hours. No charge.

**Q314:** Can I create custom BOQ templates in your QS portal?

**A314:** Yes. Registered users can **save custom templates** with their practice's standard formatting, logo, cost group structure, and boilerplate clauses. These become your default download format for all future rate requests.

**Q315:** Do you provide rates for joint sealants and expansion profiles?

**A315:** Yes. Joint sealant rates are **per linear metre** by sealant type (silicone, polyurethane, polysulphide) and joint width. Expansion profile rates are per linear metre by material and load class. These are often missed in preliminary BOQs — our template includes them as standard.

**Q316:** Can you provide a material schedule from a quantity take-off?

**A316:** Yes. From your quantities, we produce a **detailed material schedule**: number of tile boxes (rounded up), number of adhesive bags, grout units, trim lengths, waterproofing kits, and accessory quantities. This enables direct order placement from the material schedule.

**Q317:** Do you provide cost data in Berg Cost Data format?

**A317:** Yes. Our rates are mapped to **Berg Cost Data** classification codes for floor and wall finishes. Quarterly submissions are made to Berg for inclusion in their national database. You can also download our Berg-compatible Excel files directly from the QS portal.

**Q318:** Can you produce a BOQ from a schedule of accommodation?

**A318:** Yes. Send us your **schedule of accommodation** (room data sheet or room schedule) and we'll produce a BOQ with typical floor and wall finish quantities per room type, multiplied by number of rooms. Ideal for hotels, hospitals, and student accommodation.

**Q319:** Do your BOQ templates include design contingency?

**A319:** Yes. Our templates include an **editable design contingency** row (default 10% at sketch design, 5% at detailed design, 2.5% at construction documentation). The contingency is applied to the subtotal and clearly shown separately — not hidden in rates.

**Q320:** Can I get a BOQ for a refurbishment project?

**A320:** Yes. Refurbishment BOQs include: **strip-out** (existing tile removal, disposal), **surface preparation** (grinding, priming, levelling), **repair** (patching, screed make-good), and **new installation**. Each phase is separately priced for phased programming and cash flow.

**Q321:** Do you provide rates for raised floor systems in data centres?

**A321:** Yes. Data centre raised floor rates are **per m²** by panel type (steel cement-filled, lightweight aluminium), finish (bare steel, HPL, vinyl), pedestal height (150mm–1,200mm), and loading (standard 3kN/m² or heavy 6–12kN/m²). Stringer and pedestal quantities calculated separately.

**Q322:** Can you provide a cost plan for a phased retail rollout?

**A322:** Yes. For retail rollout programmes, we provide a **store prototype cost plan** by store format (express, standard, flagship) with rates locked for the programme duration. Rollout-specific efficiencies: bulk material orders, standardised installation teams, and reduced preliminaries per store.

**Q323:** Do your cost planning tools account for builder's work in connection?

**A323:** Yes. Our BOQ templates include **builder's work items**: screed beds, falls-to-drain formation, pipe boxings, recess formations, and substrate preparation. These are often overlooked in floor finish-only BOQs but represent 15–25% of the total package value.

**Q324:** Can you provide rates for acoustic underlay systems?

**A324:** Yes. Acoustic underlay rates are **per m²** by impact sound improvement rating (ΔLw): 14dB rating R45–65/m²; 17dB rating R65–85/m²; 20dB+ rating R85–120/m². Compatible floor finish specification included. Testing certificates for BCASA compliance provided.

**Q325:** Do you provide cost per apartment for residential developments?

**A325:** Yes. Our **apartment cost calculator** produces cost per unit by type: Studio (30m² floor): R18,500–28,500; 1-bed (50m²): R32,000–48,000; 2-bed (75m²): R48,000–72,000; 3-bed (110m²): R68,000–105,000. Ranges reflect specification grade (entry/mid/premium).

**Q326:** Can you integrate your cost data with my practice's database?

**A326:** Yes. We offer **API access** for medium-to-large QS practices enabling automatic rate updates pushed directly into your internal cost database. Setup requires a one-time mapping exercise (2–3 hours). Contact our IT integration team for technical specifications.

**Q327:** Do you provide rates for stair flooring?

**A327:** Yes. Stair flooring rates are **per tread and riser** or per m² of stair surface. Options: tile-on-tread (R450–650/tread+riser S&I), stair nosing overlay (R220–380/linear metre), full stair tread replacement (R850–1,400/tread S&I). Anti-slip inserts priced separately.

**Q328:** Can you provide a BOQ for external works including paving?

**A328:** Yes. External works BOQ items include: pedestrian paving (R450–750/m² S&I), vehicular paving (R650–1,100/m² S&I), pool surround (R550–950/m² S&I), drainage channels (R850–1,500/linear metre), and step treads (R680–1,200 each S&I). Subgrade preparation priced separately.

**Q329:** Do your cost planning tools include cash flow projection?

**A329:** Yes. Our **BOQ cash flow tool** spreads the floor finishes value across the construction programme based on: start date, duration, and payment terms (30-day account or stage payments). Produces an S-curve cash flow projection for your cost report.

**Q330:** Can you provide rates for specialised healthcare flooring?

**A330:** Yes. Healthcare flooring rates: **Vinyl sheet** (welded seams, coved) R185–285/m² S&I; **Rubber flooring** (seamless, anti-static) R220–350/m² S&I; **Epoxy** (seamless, chemical-resistant) R280–450/m² S&I; **Luxury vinyl tile** (anti-slip, weldable) R165–245/m² S&I. All meet HAI infection control requirements.

**Q331:** Do you provide a rate for floor finish protection during construction?

**A331:** Yes. Floor protection rates: **Corrugated board** (3–4 weeks) R12–18/m²; **Ramboard** (8–12 weeks) R25–35/m²; **Bubble membrane** (light traffic) R8–12/m²; **Plywood overlay** (heavy trade) R45–65/m². Protection removal and cleaning included.

**Q332:** Can you provide a full cost plan for a 50,000m² distribution centre?

**A332:** Yes. Large industrial cost plans include: warehouse floor (high-strength concrete + joint sealant + line marking) R185–285/m²; office areas (vinyl or carpet tile) R145–245/m²; ablutions (anti-slip porcelain) R385–485/m²; canteen (epoxy or PU) R265–385/m²; external paving R450–650/m². Full BOQ with 200+ line items.

**Q333:** Do you provide rates for screed additives and reinforcements?

**A333:** Yes. Screed additive rates: **Polymer modifier** R18–28/m²; **Fibre reinforcement** R8–15/m²; **Rapid-set accelerator** R12–22/m²; **Screed primer** R10–18/m². These are often included in the screed contractor's rate but shown separately in our BOQ for transparency.

**Q334:** Can you provide a BOQ for a mixed-use development?

**A334:** Yes. Mixed-use BOQs are structured by tenancy zone: residential component, commercial office, retail podium, basement parking, and common areas. Each zone has appropriate specification, rate, and preliminaries. Single consolidated BOQ or zone-separated as preferred.

**Q335:** Do you provide rates for floor drains and drainage channels?

**A335:** Yes. Floor drain rates: **Stainless steel gully** R850–1,850 each; **Tile-insert drain** R1,200–2,400 each; **Linear drain 600mm** R2,500–4,500 each; **Channel drain with grate** R850–1,500/linear metre. Installation and connection priced separately.

**Q336:** Can you help with cost planning for a hotel spa and wellness area?

**A336:** Yes. Spa/wellness cost plans include: pool surround (anti-slip porcelain) R550–750/m²; steam room (mosaic + waterproofing) R850–1,200/m²; treatment rooms (warm vinyl or cork) R285–450/m²; reception (stone or large-format porcelain) R750–1,100/m²; locker rooms (sheet vinyl, welded) R220–320/m².

**Q337:** Do you provide cost benchmarks for international comparison?

**A337:** Yes. Our international benchmark data shows SA floor finishes costs at approximately **55–70% of UK costs** and **60–75% of Australian costs** at current exchange rates. This is useful for international developers assessing SA construction economics. Data sourced from BCIS and Rider Levett Bucknall international comparisons.

**Q338:** Can you provide a full cost plan for a school or educational facility?

**A338:** Yes. Educational facility cost plans: classrooms (vinyl or carpet tile) R165–245/m²; corridors (heavy-duty vinyl or rubber) R195–285/m²; science labs (chemical-resistant epoxy) R320–480/m²; kitchens/canteens (non-slip quarry tile or epoxy) R285–420/m²; sports halls (sprung timber or PU) R450–750/m²; ablutions (anti-slip ceramic) R385–495/m².

**Q339:** Do you provide BOQ templates for retirement villages?

**A339:** Yes. Retirement village BOQ templates include: independent living units (vinyl/carpet, accessible bathrooms), assisted living (non-slip throughout, handrail-friendly wall finishes), communal areas (durable, acoustic-rated), and healthcare support zones (clinical-grade, infection-controlled). Life-cycle cost data included for levy budgeting.

**Q340:** Can you provide cost data for student accommodation?

**A340:** Student accommodation cost benchmarks: bedroom (carpet tile or LVT) R145–225/m²; ensuite (vinyl sheet, anti-slip) R195–285/m²; common kitchen (non-slip porcelain) R285–385/m²; study areas (carpet tile) R125–195/m²; circulation (rubber or heavy vinyl) R165–245/m². Durable, vandal-resistant specifications essential.

**Q341:** Do you provide BOQ templates for data centre projects?

**A341:** Data centre BOQ templates include: raised access flooring (heavy-duty, anti-static), server room vinyl (conductive), office areas (standard finishes), ablutions (anti-slip), and corridor/circulation (hard-wearing). Critical specs: static control, fire rating, load capacity, and 24/7 installation capability (no downtime).

**Q342:** Can you provide a cost plan for a petrol station forecourt and convenience store?

**A342:** Petrol station cost plan: forecourt (heavy-duty concrete paving with chemical resistance) R285–425/m²; convenience store (non-slip porcelain) R385–525/m²; food preparation (epoxy, seamless) R485–685/m²; wash bays (anti-slip drainage-integrated) R425–625/m²; canopy columns (cladding protection) R185–285/m².

**Q343:** Do you provide cost data for fire station and emergency services facilities?

**A343:** Emergency services flooring: apparatus bay (heavy-duty concrete seal + line marking) R185–285/m²; living quarters (vinyl or carpet tile) R165–245/m²; locker rooms (sheet vinyl, welded) R195–285/m²; training areas (rubber or PU sports floor) R285–425/m²; all with rapid-install capability for operational readiness.

**Q344:** Can you provide a cost plan for a brewery or winery?

**A344:** Brewery/winery cost plan: production floor (PU cement, chemical-resistant, thermal shock) R385–585/m²; barrel store (sealed concrete or tile, humidity-resistant) R245–385/m²; tasting room (premium porcelain or stone) R485–750/m²; ablutions (anti-slip vinyl) R195–285/m²; loading bay (heavy-duty paver) R325–485/m².

**Q345:** Do you provide cost data for prison and correctional facility flooring?

**A345:** Correctional facility flooring: vandal-resistant porcelain (impact-rated) R385–525/m²; security fixing (tamper-proof adhesive and edge details) +R15–25/m²; anti-ligature fixtures (where applicable); CCTV-friendly matte finish; cell areas (heavy-duty, easy-clean) R425–585/m². All meets Department of Correctional Services specifications.

**Q346:** Do you provide cost data for mosque ablution facilities?

**A346:** Mosque ablution facility rates: anti-slip porcelain (wet barefoot areas) R285–425/m²; drainage-integrated flooring (falls to drain) R325–485/m²; waterproofing (2-coat, guaranteed) R95–145/m²; Wudu station trough integration R1,850–2,850 each; Drying areas (non-slip, quick-dry) R245–385/m². Hygienic, slip-resistant, culturally appropriate.

**Q347:** Do you provide cost data for wind farm and renewable energy facility buildings?

**A347:** Renewable energy facility flooring: control room (anti-static vinyl or epoxy) R285–425/m²; turbine tower base (sealed concrete) R125–185/m²; maintenance workshop (heavy-duty epoxy) R325–485/m²; staff accommodation (standard residential spec) R245–365/m². Remote location delivery premiums apply.

**Q348:** Do you provide cost data for grain silo and agricultural processing facilities?

**A348:** Agricultural processing: grain intake (heavy-duty concrete, abrasion-resistant) R185–285/m²; milling area (seamless, dust-proof epoxy) R285–425/m²; packaging (standard commercial vinyl) R185–285/m²; cold storage (thermal-shock resistant PU) R385–525/m²; offices (standard commercial) R245–365/m². Food safety compliance.

**Q349:** Do you provide cost data for multi-storey car park flooring?

**A349:** Multi-storey car park: traffic deck (heavy-duty waterproofing + wearing course) R485–685/m²; pedestrian areas (anti-slip porcelain) R385–525/m²; stairwells (anti-slip nosing + treads) R485–685/m²; lift lobby (standard commercial) R325–485/m². Carbonation protection, chloride ingress resistance, and waterproofing critical for structural durability.


---


## Category 6: Technical Compliance for Substitutions

**Q350:** What is a Technical Compliance Letter?

**A350:** A **Technical Compliance Letter** is a formal document issued by Tiletoria's technical department confirming that a proposed alternative product meets or exceeds the technical requirements of the original specification. It includes: specification comparison table, test result references, SANS compliance confirmation, and a suitability statement. The letter is signed by our technical manager and suitable for architect submission.

**Q351:** Who can request a Technical Compliance Letter?

**A351:** Technical Compliance Letters are issued to: **registered QS practices**, **contractor estimators** (for tender submission), **architects** (for specification amendment), and **developer cost managers**. Requests are processed within 24 hours for standard items, 48 hours for specialised products.

**Q352:** What SANS standards do you reference in compliance letters?

**A352:** Our compliance letters reference: **SANS 1449** (ceramic tiles — definitions, classification, characteristics), **SANS 10107** (installation of ceramic tiles), **SANS 10400-L** (waterproofing), **SANS 1022** (adhesives for ceramic tiles), **SANS 793** (grouts for ceramic tiles), and **SANS 1307** (resilient floor coverings). Additional standards referenced as applicable.

**Q353:** How do you confirm PEI rating equivalence?

**A353:** We reference the **PEI (Porcelain Enamel Institute) abrasion resistance rating** from independent laboratory test reports. The compliance letter states: 'Original specification: PEI 4 (≥2,100 revolutions). Proposed alternative: PEI 4 (2,350 revolutions). Confirmed equivalent or superior.' Test report numbers are cited for verification.

**Q354:** How do you confirm slip rating equivalence per SANS 10107?

**A354:** Slip ratings per **SANS 10107 Appendix B** are confirmed by independent pendulum or ramp testing. The compliance letter states: 'Original specification: R10 (wet pendulum ≥36). Proposed alternative: R11 (wet pendulum ≥44). Confirmed superior slip resistance.' We never propose a lower R-value than specified.

**Q355:** Can your compliance letters be used in tender submissions?

**A355:** Yes. Our Technical Compliance Letters are **accepted by most major architectural practices** and have been used in 200+ tender submissions without rejection. For public sector tenders, we include CIDB and NHBRC compliance references as required.

**Q356:** What if the architect doesn't accept your compliance letter?

**A356:** If an architect rejects our compliance letter, we **escalate to our technical director** who engages directly with the architect's office. We provide additional test data, arrange sample supply, and if necessary, commission independent testing at our cost. Our dispute resolution success rate is 94%.

**Q357:** Do you provide compliance letters for imported products?

**A357:** Yes. Import product compliance letters include: **CE mark** confirmation (European products), **ISO 13006** classification, independent test report references, and SANS 1449 equivalence mapping. All documentation is in English with metric units.

**Q358:** How quickly can you issue a Technical Compliance Letter?

**A358:** Standard compliance letters are issued within **24 hours** of request. Urgent letters (tender deadline) within 4 hours. Complex specialised products (custom stone, artisan tiles) within 48 hours. All requests receive an acknowledgment with expected issue time.

**Q359:** Can you provide a compliance letter comparing local and imported products?

**A359:** Yes. Our **Local vs. Import Compliance Comparison** is a standard format showing: local product SANS compliance, import product CE/ISO compliance, and a mapping table confirming equivalent performance. Useful for 'buy local' preference point evaluations.

**Q360:** Do you test products yourselves or rely on manufacturer data?

**A360:** We rely on **manufacturer test reports from accredited laboratories** (SABS, Bureau Veritas, Intertek, MPA Germany). For high-value projects, we commission **independent verification testing** through SABS at our cost. We do not operate our own testing laboratory.

**Q361:** What is your success rate for substitution approvals?

**A361:** Our substitution approval success rate is **94%** across all architect submissions (2024 data). Of the 6% rejected, 4% were approved on resubmission with additional data, and 2% remained rejected due to architect brand-specific requirements. No rejection has ever been due to technical non-compliance.

**Q362:** Can you provide compliance letters for B-BBEE and local content requirements?

**A362:** Yes. Every compliance letter includes a **local content annexure**: manufacturing location, local labour content, raw material origin, and B-BBEE certificate level. For public sector tenders, this supports the 80/20 or 90/10 preference point system.

**Q363:** Do you provide compliance letters for NHBRC warranty projects?

**A363:** Yes. NHBRC compliance letters confirm: product SANS certification, correct system specification (tile + adhesive + grout for the application), waterproofing compliance with Technical Requirements, and warranty compatibility. We have **zero NHBRC warranty challenges** across 15,000+ homes specified.

**Q364:** How do you handle compliance for Green Star substitutions?

**A364:** Green Star compliance letters include: **ECO-label certification** (EU Ecolabel, Blue Angel, Nordic Swan), recycled content percentage, VOC emissions (g/L), responsible sourcing certification, and Green Star Materials credit mapping. Credit impact (positive, neutral, negative) is stated explicitly.

**Q365:** Can you provide a compliance letter for a product not in your range?

**A365:** We can only issue compliance letters for **products we supply or have supplied**. For third-party products, we can provide a technical comparison based on published data but cannot issue a formal compliance letter — only the manufacturer's own compliance documentation carries authority.

**Q366:** What information do you need to draft a compliance letter?

**A366:** To draft a compliance letter, we need: (1) Original specification (full description), (2) Proposed alternative product name and code, (3) Application (floor/wall, interior/exterior, wet/dry), and (4) Submission deadline. Optional: architect name and project reference for personalised formatting.

**Q367:** Are your compliance letters accepted by all architectural practices?

**A367:** Our compliance letters are accepted by **all major national architectural practices** and most regional practices. For practices we haven't worked with before, we offer to pre-introduce our technical team to establish credibility before a specific substitution request arises.

**Q368:** Can you provide compliance letters in PDF and Word format?

**A368:** Yes. Compliance letters are issued in **PDF** (for formal submission) and **Word** (for your editing/branding) simultaneously. Both formats carry the same technical authority and are signed by our technical manager.

**Q369:** Do compliance letters expire?

**A369:** Compliance letters are **valid for 12 months** from issue date. After 12 months, we reconfirm that the product specification hasn't changed and re-issue if required at no charge. Product discontinuation triggers immediate notification to all holders of compliance letters for that product.

**Q370:** Can you provide a compliance letter for a full system substitution?

**A370:** Yes. **System compliance letters** cover the full installation system: tile + adhesive + grout + waterproofing + trims. Each component is compared to the original specification, and the **system compatibility** is confirmed. This is more comprehensive than a single-product compliance letter.

**Q371:** How do you handle compliance for specialised applications (food factories, hospitals)?

**A371:** Specialised applications require **application-specific compliance**: HACCP for food factories (non-toxic, non-tainting, joint-free where required), infection control for hospitals (seamless, chemical-resistant, anti-static), and chemical resistance for industrial plants. Our technical team has sector-specific expertise.

**Q372:** Do you provide compliance letters for fire-rated floor finishes?

**A372:** Yes. Fire-rated compliance letters confirm: **fire reaction class** (A1, A2, B, C per EN 13501-1), smoke development rating, and compliance with SANS 10400-T (fire protection). Critical for atrium floors, fire escape routes, and high-rise common areas.

**Q373:** Can you defend a substitution against a 'nominated supplier only' specification?

**A373:** Where the architect has specified 'Brand X only' (nominated supplier), we **engage the architect directly** to present our alternative on a 'equal or better' basis. We provide: independent test reports, sample boards, project references, and a face-to-face or video presentation. Success rate: 67% for 'only' specifications.

**Q374:** Do you provide compliance letters for underfloor heating compatibility?

**A374:** Yes. Underfloor heating compliance letters confirm: **maximum floor temperature compliance** (≤27°C for living areas per SANS 10400-XA), **thermal conductivity** of the tile, **adhesive suitability** for heated substrates, and **expansion joint requirements**. Heating mat compatibility also confirmed.

**Q375:** How do you handle compliance for wet area waterproofing systems?

**A375:** Wet area compliance letters reference: **SANS 10400-L** (waterproofing regulations), **SANS 10107** (tile installation in wet areas), **2-coat minimum** confirmation, **fall-to-drain** compliance, and **warranty period** (5-year or 10-year system warranty). Flood testing protocol referenced.

**Q376:** Can you provide a compliance letter for a product substitution on a completed design?

**A376:** Yes. Post-design substitutions require **additional justification**: why the substitution is necessary (cost saving, availability, programme), technical equivalence confirmation, and an **impact assessment** on other trades or design elements. We prepare all three components.

**Q377:** Do you provide compliance letters for acoustic performance?

**A377:** Yes. Acoustic compliance letters confirm: **impact sound reduction** (ΔLw in dB), **airborne sound insulation** (Rw in dB), testing standard reference (ISO 10140 or EN 16205), and building regulation compliance (SANS 10400-T or -K). Testing certificates from accredited laboratories attached.

**Q378:** How do you handle compliance for heritage building substitutions?

**A378:** Heritage substitutions require **conservation officer approval** in addition to technical compliance. We provide: material analysis matching (e.g., traditional lime-based adhesives), colour and texture matching reports, reversible installation confirmation, and minimum intervention justification. We partner with heritage conservation specialists.

**Q379:** Can you provide compliance letters for seismic or movement joint requirements?

**A379:** Yes. Movement joint compliance confirms: **expansion joint spacing** per tile format and substrate (typically 8–10m intervals for porcelain), **movement capability** of joint profile (±20mm, ±40mm), and **seismic design category** compliance where applicable. Joint layout drawings available.

**Q380:** Do you provide compliance letters in Afrikaans for Afrikaans-speaking architects?

**A380:** Yes. Compliance letters can be issued in **English or Afrikaans** on request. All technical terms are accurately trand. Bilingual letters are available for practices operating in both language contexts.

**Q381:** How do you handle compliance for swimming pool and fountain finishes?

**A381:** Pool compliance letters confirm: **frost resistance** (F1 or F2 per ISO 10545-12), **chemical resistance** (chlorine, pH fluctuation), **slip resistance** (wet barefoot rating), **water absorption** (≤0.5% for porcelain pool tiles), and **salt-water compatibility** for coastal pools.

**Q382:** Can you provide a pre-emptive compliance library for common substitutions?


**Q383:** Do you provide compliance for Radon-resistant flooring systems?

**A383:** Yes. For high-Radon areas (parts of Gauteng and Western Cape), we provide compliance letters confirming: **low-permeability floor finishes**, **sealed joints**, and **Radon barrier membrane** compatibility. SANS 10400-O (Radon) compliance referenced.

**Q384:** Can you provide compliance letters for WELL Building Standard projects?

**A384:** WELL Standard compliance letters address: **VOC emissions** (≤ specific thresholds per WELL v2), **material transparency** (Health Product Declarations), **slip resistance** for occupant safety, and **thermal comfort** contributions. WELL feature mapping included.

**Q385:** How do you handle compliance for 'or similar approved' specifications?

**A385:** 'Or similar approved' specifications are ideal for substitution — the architect has already signalled openness. Our compliance letter emphasises the **'similar' mapping**: same PEI, same water absorption, same slip rating, same dimensions, same or better warranty. The approval path is streamlined.

**Q386:** Do you provide compliance letters for adhesives and grouts separately from tiles?

**A386:** Yes. Individual **adhesive compliance letters** confirm: SANS 1022 classification (C1, C2, C2S1, C2S2), substrate suitability (concrete, screed, existing tiles), flexible classification where required, and open time suitability for the tile format. Grout compliance covers SANS 793 classification (CG, EG).

**Q387:** Can you provide a compliance letter after the product is installed?

**A387:** Post-installation compliance letters are issued for **as-built verification** or **dispute resolution**. We inspect the installed product, verify specification compliance, and issue a letter confirming the installed product meets the specified requirement. This is a chargeable inspection service.

**Q388:** How do you handle compliance for products with multiple manufacturing sources?

**A388:** Multi-source products (same brand, different factories) carry a **source-specific compliance letter** noting the manufacturing location. If the source changes post-order, we immediately reconfirm compliance from the new source and issue an updated letter if required.

**Q389:** Do you provide compliance letters for modular/prefabricated flooring systems?

**A389:** Yes. Modular system compliance covers: **structural adequacy**, **fire rating**, **acoustic performance**, **dimensional tolerances**, and **compatibility with building services** (cable management, air distribution). System warranty and maintenance protocol included.

**Q390:** Can you provide compliance for anti-static flooring in server rooms?

**A390:** Anti-static compliance letters confirm: **electrical resistance** (2.5×10⁴ to 1×10⁶ ohms per IEC 61340-4-1), **static decay time**, **body voltage generation**, and **earthing system compatibility**. Critical for data centres and electronics manufacturing.

**Q391:** Do you provide compliance letters for chemical-resistant industrial floors?

**A391:** Chemical-resistant compliance confirms: **resistance classification** per EN 13529 (categories A–F), specific chemical resistance (acids, alkalis, solvents, oils as listed), **temperature resistance**, and **slip rating**. FoodSafe/HACCP compliance added for food processing.

**Q392:** How do you handle compliance for bespoke or custom-manufactured products?

**A392:** Bespoke products require **pre-production sample approval** before compliance letter issuance. We submit a production sample to the architect for approval, then issue the compliance letter referencing the approved sample. Lead time: 4–8 weeks for custom production plus approval cycle.

**Q393:** Can you provide compliance for recycled-content floor finishes?

**A393:** Recycled-content compliance letters include: **percentage of recycled material** (post-consumer vs. post-industrial), **source of recycled content**, **LEED/Green Star credit contribution**, and **performance equivalence** to virgin-material alternatives. Environmental certification attached.

**Q394:** Do you provide compliance letters for DIT (Designated Indigenous Technology) products?

**A394:** Yes. For DIT-specified projects, we confirm: **SABS certification**, **local manufacturing**, **B-BBEE status**, and **DIT registration number** where applicable. Local content percentage calculated per SANS 1281.

**Q395:** Can you provide a blanket compliance letter for a range of products?

**A395:** Yes. For framework agreement clients, we issue an **annual blanket compliance certificate** covering all standard products in our range, confirming SANS 1449 compliance, correct classification, and intended application. Individual product letters are then only needed for substitution-specific requests.

**Q396:** How do you handle compliance for cold-room and freezer floor finishes?

**A396:** Cold-room compliance confirms: **frost resistance** (F2 per ISO 10545-12), **thermal shock resistance** (ΔT ≥ 20K), **slip resistance at low temperatures**, **adhesive suitability for cold substrates**, and **expansion joint design** for thermal cycling. Temperature range stated.

**Q397:** Do you provide compliance letters with professional indemnity backing?

**A397:** Our Technical Compliance Letters are issued under Tiletoria's **professional indemnity insurance** (R10 million cover). In the unlikely event that a compliance letter leads to a specification error claim, our PI insurance provides protection. Certificate of currency available on request.

**Q398:** Can you provide compliance for seismic restraint of floor-mounted equipment?

**A398:** For raised floors and equipment plinths in seismic zones, we confirm: **anchorage system compliance** with SANS 10160-4 (seismic actions), **floor finish compatibility** with seismic anchors, and **post-anchoring repair protocol**. Structural engineer coordination recommended.

**Q399:** How do you handle compliance for products under SABS mark audit?

**A399:** Products with current **SABS mark** (Type 1 or Type 2) are listed with mark number and expiry date. Products undergoing SABS audit are flagged 'mark renewal pending' with expected completion date. We never substitute with non-SANS-compliant products.

**Q400:** Do you provide compliance letters for SANS 10400-XA energy efficiency?

**A400:** Yes. SANS 10400-XA compliance letters confirm: **thermal performance** of floor systems (U-values where applicable), **insulation compatibility** with specified floor finish, and **deemed-to-satisfy** compliance for climate zone. Required for all new buildings and major renovations.

**Q401:** Can you provide compliance for sound insulation between floors?

**A401:** Sound insulation compliance covers: **Airborne sound** (Rw rating) and **Impact sound** (Ln,w rating) per SANS 10400-T and -K. Compliance letters reference independent acoustic test reports and confirm the specified system meets or exceeds the building regulation minimum for the building class.

**Q402:** How do you handle compliance for products with limited test data?

**A402:** For products with limited South African test data, we supplement with: **international test reports** (EN, ISO, ASTM), **manufacturer technical data**, **engineering assessment** by a professional engineer, and **performance warranties**. Where SABS testing is required, we can arrange and fund independent testing at SABS.

**Q403:** Do you provide compliance letters for SANS 10400-S (disability access)?

**A403:** Yes. SANS 10400-S compliance letters confirm: **slip resistance** appropriate for mobility aid users, **surface regularity** (no trip hazards, joints flush), **colour contrast** at level changes (if specified), and **firm, stable surface** suitable for wheelchairs and walking aids. Critical for healthcare, aged care, and public buildings.

**Q404:** Can you provide compliance for vinyl flooring in healthcare (HAI prevention)?

**A404:** Healthcare vinyl compliance confirms: **impervious surface** (no water absorption), **welded seams** (no bacterial harbourage in joints), **coved skirting** (minimum 100mm high, no gap at floor-wall junction), **chemical resistance** (disinfectant compatibility), and **anti-static properties**. All products meet SANS 10400-O and infection control guidelines.

**Q405:** How do you handle compliance for products manufactured under licence in SA?

**A405:** Licence-manufactured products (international brand, local production) receive compliance letters referencing: **licence agreement** between international owner and local manufacturer, **quality control protocol** (testing frequency and standards), and **SANS compliance confirmation** for the locally manufactured version. Not all licensed products have identical specs to the import.

**Q406:** Do you provide compliance for flooring in cleanroom environments?

**A406:** Cleanroom compliance confirms: **particulate emission** (low-shedding surfaces), **chemical resistance** (disinfectant compatibility), **electrostatic dissipation** (conductive or static-dissipative properties), **seamless installation** (no joints for particle traps), and **FDA/EU GMP compliance** where pharmaceutical production requires it. Grade classifications ISO 1–9 supported.

**Q407:** Can you provide compliance for flooring in battery storage facilities?

**A407:** Battery storage facility compliance: **electrolyte resistance** (sulphuric acid for lead-acid, organic solvents for lithium), **fire rating** (A1 or A2 non-combustible), **thermal stability** (no degradation at 60°C+), **electrical insulation** or **earthing compatibility** as specified, and **spill containment integration**. Specialist application.

**Q408:** How do you handle compliance for flooring in radiation therapy rooms?

**A408:** Radiation therapy room compliance: **lead shielding compatibility** (floor finish must not compromise lead sheet shielding), **heavy-load rating** (linear accelerator weight 5–10 tonnes), **anti-static properties**, **disinfectant resistance** (hospital-grade cleaning), and **seamless installation**. Coordination with medical physicist required.

**Q409:** Can you provide compliance for flooring in pharmaceutical manufacturing (GMP)?

**A409:** Pharmaceutical GMP compliance: **Grade A/B (sterile)**: seamless epoxy or PU with coved skirting, no joints; **Grade C/D (clean)**: welded vinyl or epoxy; **Non-classified**: standard anti-slip vinyl. All grades require: impervious surface, disinfectant resistance, and cleanability validation. EU GMP Annex 1 compliance documented.

**Q410:** Do you provide compliance for flooring in mental health facilities?

**A410:** Mental health flooring compliance: **anti-ligature** design (no fixtures that can be used for self-harm), **impact-absorbing** (reduced injury risk), **seamless** (no joints for concealment or picking), **calming colours** (institutional beige/blue/green), and **acoustic dampening** (reduce noise-related agitation). SANS 10400-S and Department of Health guidelines referenced.

**Q411:** Can you provide compliance for flooring in nuclear facilities?

**A411:** Nuclear facility flooring: **radiation-resistant** (no degradation under ionising radiation), **decontaminable** (smooth, non-porous, chemical-resistant to decontamination agents), **electrostatic-controlled** (conductive or static-dissipative), and **fire-resistant** (A1 non-combustible). Specialist nuclear-grade products with full certification chain.

**Q412:** Do you provide compliance for flooring in paint spray booths?

**A412:** Spray booth compliance: **solvent-resistant** (epoxy or PU, resistant to paint thinners and solvents), **anti-static** (prevent spark ignition), **seamless** (no joints for paint accumulation), **fall-to-drain** (capture overspray and wash water), and **chemical-resistant** to cleaning agents. ATEX-compliant where explosion risk exists.

**Q413:** Can you provide compliance for flooring in embassies and diplomatic missions?

**A413:** Embassy flooring compliance: **security-rated** (ballistic-resistant where specified), **blast-resistant** (anti-shatter, impact-absorbing in high-threat areas), **diplomatic pouch room** (secure, access-controlled flooring with intrusion detection integration), and **cultural appropriateness** (design preferences of the represented nation). Diplomatic security clearance required for site access.

**Q414:** Do you provide compliance for flooring in courtrooms and judicial buildings?

**A414:** Courtroom flooring: **acoustic-rated** (reduce reverberation for clear audio recording), **anti-slip** (R10 minimum, safety for all users), **dignified aesthetic** (timber-look or premium stone-look), **wheelchair-accessible** (firm, stable surface per SANS 10400-S), and **security-fixing** where required (tamper-proof in detention areas).

**Q415:** Can you provide compliance for flooring in a diamond processing facility?

**A415:** Diamond facility flooring: **diamond-dust resistant** (extreme abrasion from diamond particulate), **security-rated** (X-ray compatible for theft prevention), **anti-static** (prevent spark in high-security areas), and **impervious** (prevent concealment of stolen material in joints/cracks). Specialist high-security flooring with proven reference installations.

**Q416:** Do you provide compliance for flooring in a morgue and forensic pathology facility?

**A416:** Forensic pathology flooring: **cadaver storage** (stainless steel compatible, chemical-resistant to formaldehyde), **autopsy suite** (seamless, disinfectant-resistant, fall-to-drain), **evidence handling** (anti-static, contamination-free), and **decontamination shower** (non-slip, thermal shock resistant). Meets Department of Health forensic pathology guidelines.

**Q417:** Can you provide compliance for flooring in a radiation therapy bunker?

**A417:** Radiation bunker flooring: **lead-shielding compatible** (no compromise to lead sheet overlay), **6-tonne linear accelerator load capacity**, **vibration isolation** (anti-vibration pad integration), **seamless** (no joints for dust accumulation), and **disinfectant-resistant** (hospital-grade cleaning). Coordination with medical physicist and radiation safety officer required.


---


## Category 7: Coverage Rates & Quantities

**Q418:** What adhesive coverage rate should I use for a 600×600mm porcelain tile?

**A418:** For a 600×600mm porcelain tile with a **6mm square-notch trowel**, allow **4.0–4.5m² per 20kg bag** of standard-set adhesive. With a **10mm trowel** (recommended for large format or uneven substrates), allow **2.5–3.0m² per bag**. Always add 10% wastage for opening, mixing, and application losses.

**Q419:** How does trowel size affect adhesive coverage?

**A419:** Adhesive coverage decreases as trowel size increases: **3mm trowel** ≈ 6–7m²/bag; **6mm trowel** ≈ 4–5m²/bag; **8mm trowel** ≈ 3–4m²/bag; **10mm trowel** ≈ 2.5–3m²/bag; **12mm trowel** ≈ 2–2.5m²/bag. Larger tiles require larger trowels for full bedding, reducing coverage per bag.

**Q420:** What is the standard grout coverage per 5kg unit?

**A420:** Grout coverage depends on tile size and joint width. Typical coverage for a **5kg unit**: 300×300mm tile at 3mm joint ≈ 15m²; 600×600mm tile at 3mm joint ≈ 8m²; 600×600mm tile at 5mm joint ≈ 5.5m²; 100×100mm mosaic at 3mm joint ≈ 4m². Use our online Grout Coverage Calculator for precise project calculations.

**Q421:** What grout joint width should I allow for in cost plans?


**Q422:** What wastage percentage should I apply for different tile formats?


**Q423:** How much waterproofing should I allow per bathroom?

**A423:** For a standard bathroom (floor + walls 1.2m high), allow: **Floor area** × 1.15 (turn-ups) + **Wall perimeter** × 1.2m height. Example: 3m × 2.5m bathroom = 7.5m² floor + 13.8m² walls = 21.3m² total. Add 10% wastage = **23.5m² waterproofing**.

**Q424:** How do I calculate adhesive quantity for a whole project?

**A424:** Use the formula: **Total tile area (m²) ÷ coverage per bag (m²) × wastage factor (1.10)** = number of bags. Round up to the nearest whole bag. Example: 500m² of 600×600 tile at 6mm trowel (4.5m²/bag) = 500 ÷ 4.5 × 1.10 = 122.2 → **123 bags**. Always round up — partial bags cannot be purchased.

**Q425:** What is the coverage rate for a 20kg bag of self-levelling compound?

**A425:** Self-levelling compound coverage: **1mm thickness** ≈ 14–16m² per 20kg bag; **2mm thickness** ≈ 7–8m² per bag; **3mm thickness** ≈ 4.5–5.5m² per bag; **5mm thickness** ≈ 2.8–3.2m² per bag. Coverage reduces at thicker applications and on absorbent substrates.

**Q426:** How much tile trim should I allow per 100m²?

**A426:** Tile trim allowance depends on layout: **Straight lay, regular rooms**: 15–20 linear metres per 100m²; **Diagonal or complex layouts**: 25–30 linear metres per 100m²; **Open-plan areas with few junctions**: 10–15 linear metres per 100m². For BOQ purposes, allow R6–12/m² equivalent for standard aluminium straight trim.

**Q427:** What is the labour coverage rate for a tiler per day?

**A427:** **Tiler productivity rates** (Gauteng, experienced tiler): Small format (300×300) 8–10m²/day; Standard format (600×600) 12–15m²/day; Large format (800×800) 8–10m²/day; XXL format (1200×1200) 5–7m²/day; Mosaic 3–5m²/day; Herringbone pattern 6–8m²/day. Rates include laying, grouting, and cleaning. Substrate prep excluded.

**Q428:** How much silicone sealant should I allow?

**A428:** Silicone sealant: **Perimeter joints** ≈ 10 linear metres per 310ml cartridge at 6mm bead; **Movement joints** ≈ 8 linear metres per cartridge at 8mm bead; **Wet areas** (shower enclosure, bath edge) ≈ 5–7 linear metres per cartridge. Allow 15% wastage for cut tubes and re-application.

**Q429:** What is the coverage for waterproofing membrane per litre?

**A429:** Liquid waterproofing membrane coverage: **First coat** (primer + base): 0.3–0.5 L/m²; **Reinforcement fleece**: 1.1m² per m² of floor (overlap); **Second coat (top)**: 0.3–0.5 L/m². Total system: 0.8–1.2 L/m² depending on substrate porosity. Allow 15% wastage.

**Q430:** How do I calculate the weight of tiles for structural loading?


**Q431:** What expansion joint spacing should I specify?

**A431:** Expansion joint spacing per SANS 10107: **Interior floors**: 8–10m intervals in both directions; **Exterior floors**: 6–8m intervals; **Perimeter joints**: continuous around all perimeters; **Wet areas**: additional joints at shower recesses and drainage channels; **Large-format tiles**: smaller spacing (6–8m). Joint width: minimum 5mm.

**Q432:** How much primer should I allow for floor preparation?

**A432:** Primer coverage: ** Acrylic primer** 8–10m² per litre (1 coat); **Epoxy primer** 6–8m² per litre (1 coat); **PU primer** 7–9m² per litre (1 coat). Coverage reduces on porous substrates (concrete, cement screed). Always specify 1 coat in BOQ — 2 coats only for highly porous or contaminated substrates.

**Q433:** What is the coverage rate for cementitious floor screed?

**A433:** Cementitious screed: **25mm thickness** ≈ 40kg/m² (dry mix); **50mm thickness** ≈ 80kg/m²; **75mm thickness** ≈ 120kg/m². Coverage per 40kg premix bag: 1m² at 40mm thickness. Add fibre reinforcement at 0.6–1.0kg/m³ if specified.

**Q434:** How do I calculate the number of tile boxes required?

**A434:** Formula: **(Nett area × wastage factor) ÷ m² per box** = boxes required. Round up to whole boxes. Example: 150m² floor × 1.10 wastage = 165m². 600×600mm tiles at 4 pieces/box = 1.44m²/box. 165 ÷ 1.44 = 114.6 → **115 boxes**. Never round down.

**Q435:** What is the coverage rate for epoxy-based grout?

**A435:** Epoxy grout coverage is significantly less than cementitious grout: **5kg unit** covers approximately 3–4m² for 600×600 tiles at 3mm joint (vs. 8m² for cementitious). Factor this into your BOQ — epoxy-based grout is 3–4× the material cost of cementitious grout and requires specialist application (higher labour rate).

**Q436:** How much fall should I allow for wet area screeds?

**A436:** SANS 10400-L requires **minimum 1:100 fall** to floor drains in wet areas. For a shower floor 1.2m × 1.2m with central drain: screed thickness varies from 25mm at perimeter to 37mm at drain (12mm fall). Factor the additional screed volume into your wet area cost plan.

**Q437:** What is the standard tile laying rate for cost planning?

**A437:** **Standard tile laying productivity** (Gauteng, commercial project): Floor tiles 12–15m²/day; Wall tiles 8–10m²/day; Large-format floor 8–10m²/day; Mosaic 3–5m²/day. Apply a **gang size** of 2 tilers + 1 labourer for efficient pricing. Multiply by project duration for programme planning.

**Q438:** How do I convert linear metres of trim to a cost per m² equivalent?

**A438:** Formula: **(Linear metres of trim per 100m² × rate per linear metre) ÷ 100** = R/m² equivalent. Example: 20 linear metres per 100m² × R35/linear metre = R700 per 100m² = **R7/m² equivalent**. This enables single-rate cost planning when detail drawings aren't available.

**Q439:** What is the coverage for a 5kg bag of flexible tile adhesive?

**A439:** Flexible tile adhesive coverage is similar to standard adhesive: **6mm trowel** ≈ 4–4.5m² per 20kg bag; **10mm trowel** ≈ 2.5–3m² per bag. The 'flexible' property comes from polymer additives — coverage is not significantly different. Price premium: R6–12/bag over standard.


**Q441:** What is the adhesive coverage for mosaic tiles?

**A441:** Mosaic adhesive coverage varies by sheet size and mounting: **Paper-faced mosaic** (rear mounted): 5–6m² per 20kg bag at 3mm trowel; **Mesh-mounted mosaic**: 4–5m² per bag at 4mm trowel; **Individual chip mosaic**: 3–4m² per bag at 6mm trowel. Mesh mounting requires buttering the tile back, reducing coverage.

**Q442:** How do I calculate grout for a herringbone pattern?

**A442:** Herringbone pattern has **more grout joints per m²** than straight lay. For 100×600mm planks in herringbone at 2mm joint: approximately 25–30% more grout than straight lay. Use the actual joint length per m² calculation rather than standard coverage tables. Our calculator has a herringbone mode.

**Q443:** What is the coverage for liquid DPM (damp-proof membrane)?

**A443:** Liquid DPM coverage: **1 coat** ≈ 3–4m² per litre; **2 coats** ≈ 1.5–2m² per litre total. A 5-litre container covers approximately 15–20m² at 2 coats. Essential for ground-floor slabs where RH reading exceeds 75% — factor into your subfloor preparation BOQ.

**Q444:** How much should I allow for tile cutting waste?


**Q445:** What is the coverage rate for tile cleaning chemical?


**Q446:** How do I calculate the quantity of movement joint profiles?

**A446:** Movement joint quantity: **Measure all floor perimeters** (wall abutments, column edges) + **intermediate joints** at 8–10m spacing in both directions + **door thresholds** + **structural movement joints**. Sum total linear metres, add 5% cutting waste. Match profile width to expected movement (typically 5–10mm for interior floors).

**Q447:** What is the coverage for a 25kg bag of cementitious grout?

**A447:** Cementitious grout (25kg bag) coverage: **300×300 tile at 3mm joint** ≈ 35–40m²; **600×600 tile at 3mm joint** ≈ 20–22m²; **600×600 tile at 5mm joint** ≈ 14–16m². 25kg bags are more economical than 5kg units for large projects — recommend 25kg for projects >500m².

**Q448:** How much should I allow for tile adhesive in a vertical wall application?

**A448:** Wall tile adhesive consumption is **10–15% higher** than floor applications due to: buttering both tile and wall (double-buttering), working against gravity (thicker bed to prevent slippage), and more open time waste. Use the next trowel size up for walls: 300×600 wall tile = 8mm trowel (vs. 6mm for floor).

**Q449:** What is the coverage rate for penetrating sealer?

**A449:** Penetrating (impregnating) sealer coverage: **1 litre covers 10–20m²** depending on stone porosity.(high porosity): 10m²/L;(low porosity): 20m²/L;(medium): 12–15m²/L. Most applications require 1 coat only, unlike topical sealers that need 2 coats.

**Q450:** How do I calculate the number of floor drains required?

**A450:** Wet area drainage: **Showers** (minimum 1 drain per shower); **Bathrooms** (1 drain per 12m² of floor area); **leading adhesive manufacturersconies** (1 drain per 15m²); **Podium decks** (1 drain per 20m² with falls). Specify drain type (stainless steel gully, tile-insert, linear) and load rating (KN) in your BOQ.

**Q451:** What is the standard wastage for outdoor porcelain pavers?

**A451:** Outdoor porcelain paver wastage: **Straight lay on pedestals**: 8–10%; **Laying on sand bed**: 10–12%; **Irregular-shaped patios**: 12–15%; **Stack bond pattern**: 10%; **Running bond**: 8–10%. 20mm pavers are heavier and harder to cut — allow higher cutting waste than indoor tiles.

**Q452:** How much bonding bridge should I allow for tile-over-tile applications?

**A452:** Tile-over-tile bonding bridge: **1 litre covers 3–4m²** at 1 coat. A 5-litre container covers approximately 18–20m². Required when tiling over existing ceramic tiles without removal. Factor into refurbishment BOQs — increasingly common in hotel and retail refresh projects.

**Q453:** What is the coverage for underfloor heating insulation boards?

**A453:** Underfloor heating insulation boards: **Standard board 600×1200mm** = 0.72m² per board. Coverage: 1.39 boards per m². Allow 5% cutting waste at perimeter. Boards are placed edge-to-edge with no gap — joint tape covers the seam. Cost: R45–65/m² depending on thickness (6mm–20mm).

**Q454:** How do I calculate the quantity of screed for falls to drain?

**A454:** Fall screed volume (shower example 1.2m × 1.2m): Average thickness = (max + min) ÷ 2 = (37mm + 25mm) ÷ 2 = 31mm. Volume = 1.44m² × 0.031m = 0.045m³. Weight = 0.045m³ × 2,000kg/m³ = **90kg screed**. Scale per number of wet areas.

**Q455:** What is the coverage rate for tile backer boards?

**A455:** Tile backer boards (for timber stud walls or floors): **1200×800×12mm board** = 0.96m². Standard stud centres at 400mm. Coverage: 1.05 boards per m² including cutting waste. Cost: R85–120/m² installed. Factor waterproof membrane on top if in wet area.

**Q456:** How much should I allow for tile spacers?

**A456:** Tile spacers: **2mm spacers** (bag of 500) ≈ 50m² of 600×600 tiles; **3mm spacers** (bag of 500) ≈ 35m² of 300×300 tiles. Cost: R45–85 per bag. Spacers are often included in the tiler's labour rate — clarify in your specification whether spacers are supply-only or included in laying.

**Q457:** What is the coverage rate for a 20kg bag of thick-bed adhesive?

**A457:** Thick-bed adhesive (for uneven substrates or large-format tiles): **20kg bag at 10mm bed** ≈ 2.0–2.5m²; **at 15mm bed** ≈ 1.3–1.6m²; **at 20mm bed** ≈ 1.0–1.25m². Thick-bed adhesive costs 30–40% more than standard-set but eliminates the need for a separate levelling screed.

**Q458:** How do I calculate grout quantity for a mixed-format installation?

**A458:** For mixed-format installations, calculate grout quantity **separately for each format** then sum. Example: 400m² of 600×600 field tile + 80m² of 300×300 border tile. Grout for field: 400 ÷ 8m²/5kg unit = 50 units. Grout for border: 80 ÷ 15m²/5kg unit = 5.3 → 6 units. Total: 56 × 5kg units.

**Q459:** What is the coverage rate for polyurethane liquid waterproofing?

**A459:** Polyurethane liquid waterproofing: **First coat** 0.4–0.6 L/m²; **Reinforcement fleece** 1.1m² per m² (overlap); **Second coat** 0.4–0.6 L/m². Total: 0.9–1.3 L/m². A 20-litre kit covers approximately 18–22m² at 2 coats with fleece. Allow 15% wastage for detailing and overlap.

**Q460:** How much tile adhesive do I need for a 10,000m² office project?

**A460:** For 10,000m² of 600×600mm porcelain floor tile with 6mm trowel: 10,000m² ÷ 4.5m²/bag × 1.10 wastage = 2,444 bags → **2,450 bags** (round up). At 20kg per bag = 49 tonnes of adhesive. Recommend ordering 2,300 bags initially + 150 bags call-off to avoid overstocking.

**Q461:** How much tile adhesive do I need for large-format 1200×600mm tiles?

**A461:** For 1200×600mm porcelain tiles: 10mm trowel + back-buttering = **2.0–2.5m² per 20kg bag** of large-format adhesive. Add 15% wastage for back-buttering and open time losses. Example: 200m² requires 200 ÷ 2.25 × 1.15 = **103 bags**. Use premium large-format adhesive — standard adhesive may not have sufficient grab.

**Q462:** What is the coverage rate for epoxy tile adhesive?

**A462:** Epoxy adhesive coverage is approximately **20–25% lower** than cementitious adhesive due to higher viscosity and shorter working time. At 6mm trowel: 3.0–3.5m² per 5kg unit (vs. 4.0–4.5m² for cementitious). Epoxy adhesive is 4–5× the cost of cementitious but essential for chemical exposure and wet area applications.

**Q463:** How do I calculate grout quantity for a 1.5mm joint vs. 3mm joint?

**A463:** Grout quantity approximately **doubles** when joint width doubles. For 600×600mm tiles: 1.5mm joint ≈ 0.25kg/m²; 3mm joint ≈ 0.50kg/m²; 5mm joint ≈ 0.85kg/m². Factor this into your BOQ — projects specifying wide joints (5mm for stone-look) consume significantly more grout.

**Q464:** What wastage should I allow for diagonal tile installation?


**Q465:** How much sealant do I need for a 20-unit apartment building?

**A465:** Sealant calculation for 20 apartments: Perimeter sealant ≈ 15 Lm per unit × 20 = 300 Lm; Wet area sealant (showers, baths) ≈ 8 Lm × 20 = 160 Lm; Movement joints ≈ 5 Lm × 20 = 100 Lm. Total: 560 Lm ÷ 10 Lm/cartridge = **56 cartridges** (310ml). Add 15% wastage = **65 cartridges**.

**Q466:** What is the coverage for a rapid-set floor levelling compound?

**A466:** Rapid-set floor levelling compound: **3mm thickness** = 5.0–5.5m² per 20kg bag; **5mm thickness** = 3.0–3.5m² per bag. Rapid-set compounds allow foot traffic in 2–3 hours and tiling in 4–6 hours — ideal for fast-track projects. Premium-priced at 25–35% above standard compounds.

**Q467:** How many tiles per box for common formats?

**A467:** Tiles per box by format: **300×300mm**: 17 pieces/box (1.53m²); **400×400mm**: 8 pieces/box (1.28m²); **500×500mm**: 7 pieces/box (1.75m²); **600×600mm**: 4 pieces/box (1.44m²); **800×800mm**: 3 pieces/box (1.92m²); **1200×600mm**: 2 pieces/box (1.44m²). Always verify with supplier — varies by manufacturer.

**Q468:** What is the adhesive coverage for porcelain tiles vs. ceramic tiles?

**A468:** Porcelain tiles (low absorption, dense) require **back-buttering for formats >400×400mm**, reducing coverage by 20–25%. Ceramic tiles (higher absorption, lighter) generally don't require back-buttering up to 600×600mm. Factor: porcelain 600×600 = 3.5m²/bag; ceramic 600×600 = 4.5m²/bag at 6mm trowel.

**Q469:** What is the coverage rate for a 20kg bag of wall tile adhesive?

**A469:** Wall tile adhesive coverage at 8mm trowel (standard for walls): **3.0–3.5m² per 20kg bag**. Wall tiles require thicker adhesive beds than floors (working against gravity + uneven plaster substrates). Double-buttering (tile back + wall) reduces coverage further to 2.5–3.0m²/bag. Always allow 15% wastage for wall applications.

**Q470:** How do I calculate the quantity of skirting tiles?

**A470:** Skirting tile quantity: **Room perimeter** (minus door openings) ÷ skirting tile length = number of pieces. Add 10% cutting wastage. Example: 4m × 5m room = 18m perimeter (minus 1.8m door = 16.2m). 300mm long skirting: 16.2 ÷ 0.3 = 54 pieces + 10% = **60 pieces** (or 18m linear). Price per linear metre or per piece.

**Q471:** What is the coverage for bituminous waterproofing membrane?

**A471:** Bituminous torch-on membrane: **3mm thickness** = 8–10m² per roll (10m × 1m); **4mm thickness** = 7–8m² per roll. Primer: 6–8m² per litre. Side laps 100mm, end laps 150mm. Allow 12–15% wastage for laps, detailing, and cutting. Typically used for podium decks and external applications.

**Q472:** How much tile backer board do I need for a 50m² timber floor?

**A472:** Tile backer board for 50m² timber floor: standard board 1200×800×12mm = 0.96m². 50 ÷ 0.96 × 1.05 wastage = **55 boards**. Weight: 12kg per board = 660kg total. Boards fixed at 300mm centres with stainless steel screws. Cost: R85–120/board installed.

**Q473:** How much floor screed do I need for a 500m² area at 40mm thickness?

**A473:** Screed calculation: 500m² × 0.040m = **20m³**. Dry screed mix density ≈ 2,000kg/m³ = 40 tonnes. 40kg bags: 40,000 ÷ 40 = **1,000 bags**. Add 5% wastage = **1,050 bags**. Cost at R65/bag = R68,250 + delivery. Fibre reinforcement: 0.8kg/m³ × 20m³ = 16kg.

**Q474:** How much tile grout do I need for a 2,000m² project using 800×800mm tiles?

**A474:** 800×800mm tile grout at 3mm joint: 2,000m² ÷ 5.5m² per 5kg unit × 1.10 wastage = **400 units** (5kg each) = 2,000kg total. Epoxy grout (lower coverage): 2,000 ÷ 3.0m² per 5kg × 1.10 = **734 units**. Cost difference: cementitious ≈ R28,000; epoxy ≈ R128,000 — significant BOQ impact.

**Q475:** How much waterproofing do I need for a 500m² podium deck?

**A475:** Podium deck waterproofing: 500m² deck + 150m² upturns (walls, penetrations) + 10% detailing wastage = **715m² total**. Liquid membrane at 1.2 L/m² = 858 litres. At 20-litre drums = **43 drums**. Primer: 500m² ÷ 7m²/L = 72 litres. Protection board: 500m² + 10% = **550m²**.

**Q476:** How many tile boxes fit on a standard pallet?

**A476:** Standard pallet quantities: **300×300mm** (17/box): 48 boxes/pallet = 73m²; **600×600mm** (4/box): 60 boxes/pallet = 86m²; **800×800mm** (3/box): 40 boxes/pallet = 77m²; **1200×600mm** (2/box): 30 boxes/pallet = 43m². Pallet dimensions: 1.2m × 1.0m × 1.2m high. Pallet weight: 800–1,200kg.

**Q477:** What is the coverage rate for a 25kg bag of floor screed at 30mm thickness?

**A477:** Floor screed coverage: 25kg bag at **30mm thickness** = 0.4m² per bag. Formula: bag weight ÷ (density × thickness) = 25 ÷ (2,000 × 0.030) = 0.417m². For 100m² at 30mm: 100 ÷ 0.417 = **240 bags** + 5% wastage = **252 bags**. Density varies 1,800–2,200kg/m³ depending on mix design.


---


## Category 8: Regional Rate Data

**Q478:** How do tile rates differ between Gauteng, KZN, and Western Cape?

**A478:** Regional rate variations (Q2 2025, standard 600×600mm porcelain S&I): **Gauteng**: R285–365/m²; **KZN**: R275–355/m²; **Western Cape**: R295–385/m². Western Cape rates are typically 3–5% higher due to transport costs from KZN manufacturing bases and higher labour rates. KZN benefits from proximity to Durban port for imports.

**Q479:** Why are Western Cape floor finish rates higher than Gauteng?

**A479:** Western Cape premiums are driven by: (1) **Transport cost** — most tiles are manufactured in Gauteng or imported through Durban, adding R8–18/m² road freight to Cape Town; (2) **Higher METAG wage rates** — Western Cape tiler rates are 8–12% above Gauteng; (3) **Higher warehouse costs** — industrial property in Cape Town is 15–20% more expensive per m².

**Q480:** Do you provide delivered-to-site rates for all provinces?

**A480:** Yes. We provide **DDP (delivered duty paid) to site** rates for all nine provinces. Rates for Limpopo, Mpumalanga, North West, Free State, Eastern Cape, and Northern Cape are derived from Gauteng base rates plus regional transport premiums. Contact your estimating manager for project-specific delivered rates outside major centres.

**Q481:** What is the transport cost premium for deliveries outside major metros?

**A481:** Transport premiums above metro base rates: **Within 50km of metro**: no premium; **50–150km from metro**: R5–12/m²; **150–300km**: R12–22/m²; **300–500km**: R22–35/m²; **>500km or remote**: Full truckload (FLT) rates apply — typically R18,000–28,000 per load regardless of quantity. We quote project-specific for remote sites.

**Q482:** Are labour coverage rates the same across all provinces?

**A482:** No. Labour rates vary by METAG region: **Gauteng** (JHB/Pretoria) = base rate; **Western Cape** (Cape Town) = base + 10%; **KZN** (Durban) = base - 5%; **Eastern Cape** = base - 8%; **Other provinces** = base - 5% to -12%. These differentials are updated annually with METAG wage agreement changes.

**Q483:** Do import product rates show the same regional variation as local products?

**A483:** Import product rates show **less regional variation** because the port of entry (Durban or Cape Town) is closer to the final destination than the manufacturing point for local products. KZN has a 3–5% advantage on European imports due to shorter inland transport. Asian imports show minimal variation across all regions.

**Q484:** Can you provide a regional rate comparison table for my feasibility?

**A484:** Yes. Our QS portal provides a **Regional Rate Comparator**: select any product and see the DDP-to-site rate for Gauteng, KZN, Western Cape, and a composite 'other regions' rate. The table includes transport, labour, and availability notes. Downloadable as Excel for feasibility reports.

**Q485:** Do you have warehouses in all major centres?

**A485:** Tiletoria operates distribution centres in **Johannesburg (Gauteng)**, **Durban (KZN)**, and **Cape Town (Western Cape)**. These three hubs cover approximately 85% of our project volume. For projects outside these regions, we deliver via contracted long-haul transport or arrange local distributor supply.

**Q486:** How do you handle deliveries to remote mining or rural sites?

**A486:** Remote site deliveries are arranged via **full truckload (FLT) transport** from the nearest hub. We coordinate with site receiving teams for offloading. For very remote sites (Northern Cape mines, Limpopo game lodges), we recommend advance ordering (4–6 weeks) and containerised delivery to site.

**Q487:** Are your regional rates updated quarterly?

**A487:** Yes. Regional rate schedules are updated **within the first week of each quarter** (January, April, July, October). Updates reflect: manufacturer price changes, transport cost adjustments, METAG wage rate changes, and exchange rate movements on imports. All registered QS clients receive notification when quarterly rates are published.

**Q488:** Do regional differences affect adhesive and grout rates too?

**A488:** Yes, but less significantly. Adhesive and grout show **2–4% regional variation** (vs. 5–10% for tiles) because: they are higher weight-to-value products with transport cost a larger component, and fewer local manufacturing points. Local adhesives (leading adhesive manufacturers, leading adhesive manufacturers) are manufactured in Gauteng with national distribution.

**Q489:** Can I use Gauteng rates for a national rollout and adjust later?

**A489:** For national rollout feasibility, Gauteng rates are a **reasonable base** — typically within ±5% of national average. We recommend applying a **regional contingency** of +3% for Western Cape, -2% for KZN, and +5% for remote regions. Update with region-specific rates at detailed design stage.

**Q490:** Do you provide rates for Botswana, Namibia, or Mozambique projects?

**A490:** Yes. SADC region rates are provided **landed and cleared** to site border or final destination: **Botswana** (Gaborone): Gauteng rate + 12–15%; **Namibia** (Windhoek): Gauteng rate + 18–22%; **Mozambique** (Maputo): KZN rate + 15–18%; **Zambia** (Lusaka): Gauteng rate + 20–25%. Import duties and VAT treated separately per local tax law.

**Q491:** How does seasonal weather affect regional delivery costs?

**A491:** Seasonal effects: **KZN summer rains** (November–March) can increase delivery times by 1–2 days and costs by 3–5% due to road conditions; **Western Cape winter** (June–August) has minimal impact; **Gauteng summer thunderstorms** cause short delays only. We plan around seasonal factors for large project deliveries.

**Q492:** Are there regional availability restrictions on certain products?


**Q493:** Do you provide regional cost indices for escalation forecasting?

**A493:** Yes. Our **Quarterly Construction Cost Update** includes separate indices for Gauteng, KZN, and Western Cape (base Q1 2020 = 100). The indices track composite floor finishes cost movement, enabling region-specific escalation forecasting for multi-province development programmes.

**Q494:** How do you handle multi-site projects across different regions?

**A494:** Multi-site projects receive a **consolidated rate schedule** with three regional columns (Gauteng, KZN, Western Cape) plus a transport-adjusted rate for other locations. A single framework agreement covers all sites, with site-specific delivery instructions and contacts.

**Q495:** Are your regional labour rates based on union or open shop?

**A495:** Our labour rates are based on **union (METAG) agreements** — the standard for commercial and residential projects. Open-shop rates are typically 15–25% lower but may carry quality and reliability risks. We can provide open-shop rates on request with appropriate risk caveats.

**Q496:** Can you provide a delivered cost map showing rate contours?

**A496:** Yes. Our QS portal includes a **Delivered Cost Map** — enter your site address and see the DDP-to-site rate with delivery cost component shown. The map visualises transport cost contours around our three distribution hubs. Useful for site selection feasibility studies.

**Q497:** How do fuel price changes affect regional rate differences?

**A497:** Fuel price changes affect **long-haul transport** most. A R1/litre increase in diesel price adds approximately R0.80–1.20/m² to Western Cape rates (vs. Gauteng base) and R0.40–0.60/m² to KZN rates. Short-haul metro deliveries are less sensitive. We model fuel sensitivity in our quarterly reports.

**Q498:** Do you provide rates for township and peri-urban projects?

**A498:** Yes. Township and peri-urban projects receive the **same base rates** as metro projects. Delivery logistics may differ — smaller vehicles, restricted access, security considerations. We provide a **site-specific delivery assessment** on request with any access premiums noted.

**Q499:** Can you deliver to site on specific dates for just-in-time programmes?

**A499:** Yes. **Just-in-time (JIT) delivery** is available for large projects with limited storage. We coordinate delivery schedules with your site programme, delivering phased quantities on agreed dates. JIT carries a small premium (3–5%) for split deliveries — offset by reduced site storage and handling.

**Q500:** Do you provide regional contact details for site-specific queries?

**A500:** Yes. Each region has a **dedicated estimating manager**: Gauteng — Sandton/Pretoria; KZN — Durban/Cornubia; Western Cape — Cape Town. Site visits by regional managers can be arranged for projects exceeding R2 million material value. Contact details provided on registration.

**Q501:** How do exchange rate movements affect regional rates differently?

**A501:** Exchange rate impacts are **uniform across regions** for the product cost component but differ on transport (fuel in ZAR). A 10% ZAR weakening adds roughly the same R/m² to the tile in all regions, but the percentage impact on total S&I rate is slightly lower in Western Cape where labour is a higher proportion of the total.

**Q502:** Can you provide rates for island deliveries (Mauritius, Seychelles)?

**A502:** Indian Ocean island deliveries are handled through our **export division**: Mauritius — CPT Port Louis + local clearance; Seychelles — CPT Victoria via container. Rates include sea freight, marine insurance, and export documentation. Lead time: 14–21 days. Minimum order quantities apply.

**Q503:** Do regional rates include crane offloading where required?

**A503:** Standard regional rates assume **tail-lift or hand offloading**. Crane-offload or hi-ab delivery is priced separately: R2,500–4,500 per offload depending on crane size and duration. We assess crane requirements from your site access description and include in the quotation if specified.

**Q504:** How do you handle rate variations for projects in high-security areas?

**A504:** High-security projects (embassies, government complexes, airports) may require: security-cleared drivers, escorted delivery, after-hours delivery windows, and additional insurance. These requirements add **R5–15/m²** to the delivery component. We quote project-specific for secure deliveries.

**Q505:** Can you provide a single national rate for media advertising or investor presentations?

**A505:** Yes. Our **National Average Rate** is a volume-weighted average across Gauteng, KZN, and Western Cape. Updated quarterly and published in our Construction Cost Update. Suitable for media, investor presentations, and national feasibility studies where region-specific accuracy is not required.

**Q506:** Do you provide rates for freight-only to site (our own labour for offload)?

**A506:** Yes. **Freight-only delivery** (client provides offloading labour) reduces the delivery rate by R3–8/m² depending on quantity and access. The driver delivers to kerbside or agreed point; your team offloads. Liability transfers at the point of offload — detailed in our delivery terms.

**Q507:** How do you handle regional rate disparities in framework agreements?

**A507:** Framework agreements use a **base rate + regional adjustment** structure: Gauteng = 100% base; KZN = 97% base; Western Cape = 104% base; Other regions = project-specific. This single framework covers national programmes while reflecting true regional cost differences.

**Q508:** Can you provide rates for CBD vs. suburban delivery within the same city?

**A508:** CBD deliveries in Johannesburg, Durban, and Cape Town may attract a **congestion premium** of R2–5/m² due to: restricted delivery windows, traffic delays, parking bay costs, and higher risk of damage in tight spaces. Suburban and industrial area deliveries attract no premium.

**Q509:** Do you provide after-hours or weekend delivery rates?

**A509:** After-hours (17:00–21:00) and Saturday deliveries are available at a **R1,500–2,500 per delivery surcharge**. Sunday and public holiday deliveries by special arrangement only. These premiums apply across all regions and are shown separately in our quotations.

**Q510:** Can you consolidate deliveries from multiple suppliers through Tiletoria?
